- Why Even High Earners Are Living Paycheck To Paycheck: Kind: captions Language: en For many Americans, work can feel like a hamster wheel. No matter how hard they run, it never feels like enough. Americans, on average, say they would need $520,000 a year to feel rich. And the more people earn, the more they say they need to feel comfortable. Americans making under $50,000 felt they needed $157,000 a year to feel comfortable. Those making $100,000 or more said they would need $246,000. When I was in middle school, a song came out called "Mo Money Mo Problems," and I think that that's true. Your problems scale. You still are dealing with a finite number every month. Your worries, your anxiety, your budget scales to that. You still are dealing with a ceiling. About 14% of all U.S. households make $200,000 or more every year. But many of them, dubbed HENRYs or "High Earners, Not Rich Yet," still don't feel rich. 62% of people with salaries over $300,000 a year struggle with credit card debt. Earning doesn't actually make you feel rich, spending it does. If most people spent 99% of their paycheck, they'd feel quite rich. And it's the paradox here: when we're in accumulation mode, it's very difficult to feel rich. It can be pretty easy for someone to feel like, "I'm making really good money, but I don't have a lot of discretionary income." Earning more and feeling like you're still on a hamster wheel is what a lot of HENRYs experience. And that's because they're not living within their means. They may have been living above their means when they weren't nearly making as much money, and now they're just continuing to add more and more and more. I earn $300,000 and I live in New York City. I definitely don't feel rich. I feel very lucky. I feel privileged, but I do not feel rich. I could not stop working right now. I have more money anxiety almost now than I ever did in my 20s. I am on a hamster wheel and there's no question about it. HENRYs often don't feel rich compared to others. Many HENRYs are cash-rich and asset-poor. So they're typically people under 40, they don't have childcare hours available, they're first-time homebuyers in the housing market, they're paying off student loans early, hopefully. And their income usually hasn't hit a career peak, so they're very much in that "not rich yet" bucket, but they very well could be in the future. Oh, I 100% feel like a HENRY. Marie Incontrera was a professional composer, bandleader and pianist who started her virtual assistant business in 2016. That year, I started to grow slowly, and I was suddenly earning more money than I had ever earned in my life, and I think the number was something like $35,000 by the end of the year. And I went, "Wow, this is really great. I can grow this." She then expanded into a digital marketing consulting agency during the pandemic. For the calendar year of 2025, we're just on track to make $1.4 million. We're hoping to get to $1.5 million or possibly even $1.6 million. I'm anticipating my owner's draw to be anywhere between $300,000 and $400,000. I had a pretty successful career as a musician through most of my 20s. The thing they don't tell you about having a career as a musician in music school is that you can be playing Carnegie Hall, which I was, and I was making $15,000 a year, and I didn't realize how much freedom money affords you. Now that I see the kind of freedom that somebody who earns what I do or even more has, that's my goal. That is absolutely what gets me out of bed in the morning. A lot of the "High Earners, Not Rich Yet," they're first generation wealth. So they still take their wealth into perspective against their family members or others in their community, and they realize how well they're doing, so they don't take it for granted. But I think what makes them feel not rich is we were told, "Once you make six figures, all your problems will go away. You'll just have so much excess." And I think we know that narrative is not true anymore. I would have thought back then that the amount of money that I have in the bank right now, I would be rich. I would have just thought, "Oh, yeah, she's made it." And I don't feel that way. I know that I am on a hamster wheel with my business. I actually really love the hamster wheel, because I love what I do, but I also realize that I can't stop. So, HENRYs often don't feel rich They work very hard for their money, but they also spend a lot more than the average person. In a lot of ways, HENRYs may be victims of their zip codes. $100,000 in LA is definitely very different than $100,000 in Iowa. Someone makes $200,000 a year in LA. They're going to probably take home $140,000ish. Rent for a one-bedroom is going to be at least $3,000. Their car payment is going to be $700. Car insurance is going to be maybe $200 a month. Gas is going to be $200 a month. If you were to annualize that, that's $50,000 right there. The remainder is for all the other things. We haven't eaten yet, we haven't purchased groceries, we haven't paid for subscriptions to Amazon and Netflix, we haven't gone on vacation yet, we haven't purchased clothes yet. It can whittle away your discretionary income if you're living in an area that has a high cost of living. When my business started to grow, one of the first things that I started spending more money on right away was food, because I don't have time to cook. I had more money than time and so I started eating Grubhub three meals a day most days. After that, it became rent because I hit $1 million a year in revenue and I said, "That's it. I'm moving to Manhattan." And so, my rent doubled and I suddenly was paying $4,000 in rent. What is very worth it to me is to live where I want to live. I was proud that I could live here. For about a year, I would walk outside my apartment and just like, look around and just be like, "Oh my gosh, here I am." So that was lifestyle creep for me. Lifestyle creep is the common phenomenon of spending a little more money as you earn more. It can happen to anyone at any income. Lifestyle creep can really sneak up on you. You start earning more and then you decide, "Well, I can buy more things. I need a second car. I need a bigger home. I need to go on this European vacation. I deserve this European vacation because I work hard for my money." The problem is, if you're not continuing to increase your savings at the same time, you are putting your financial future in jeopardy. That shift happens everywhere in our lives, from the small purchases to the really big ones: cars, houses, vacation. And most of these expenses are fixed. Lifestyle creep impacts almost all tax brackets, but it can take different forms. One 2024 study found that when low and middle-income households were given an additional $1,000, no strings attached every month, their spending increased primarily on basic necessities such as food, transportation, and rent. However, when higher income households were given the same $1,000, they increased spending on both necessities as well as discretionary expenses: like Marie did. I spend more on my hair, I spend more on makeup, I get my lashes done, I get my nails done. I think it's just something that comes with the territory. Birds of a feather tend to flock together and their spending tends to be more aligned. If you make about $85,000, your friends probably also make so you're doing similar activities in the same economic bracket. Once you have friends that are making $200,000 or $300,000 a year, your activities will change. The restaurants that you will eat at will differ and change. They're going to probably be a little bit more elevated. Your vacations are going to be more elevated. Your clothing is going to be more It's not necessarily spending irresponsibly, but they're spending aligned with their friend group and their community. That's why a lot of them oftentimes don't feel rich because they're spending along with their friends. Emotions are tied so tightly to what we do with our money. It happens to everyone. You get a raise, you're feeling great. You deserve to go out to dinner and, in fact, you deserve to book that cruise. But the reality is that money was supposed to be earmarked for a bump-up in your retirement savings, or money that you were going to put toward your child's education. Or maybe you don't have an emergency fund, or at least not an adequate one, and the money should have gone there. When we allow ourselves to let our emotions overtake what we're doing with our money, we lose sight sometimes of what our goals are and how we have planned to meet them. A HENRY will sometimes look to the mega-millionaires or the billionaires and wish that they had more, but it's really just that upwards comparison for more that keeps them stuck. More will never be enough at a certain point, and you have to decide what you have is ultimately enough and start appreciating all that you do have instead of fixating on all that you don't. Experts say the best way for HENRYs to start to feel rich and to get off the hamster wheel is by budgeting and planning for the You can't really figure out if you're rich yet, unless you know where you stand right now and where you want to go. You need to know your net worth. Doesn't matter how much money you have, it's not just for rich people. You have a net worth. What you own, minus what you owe, is your net worth. Everyone has one. Yours may be negative at this point, but you have to figure out how to turn that around. Once you know your net worth, then you can start to build on what are your goals, what do I want to have in terms of my retirement savings, in terms of savings for college, if you have children, in terms of emergency savings, in case anything happens, how secure will I be? Once you have significant emergency savings, enough to cover, say, six months of your expenses or more, you're going to start to feel rich. Surveys have shown that financial well-being is tied closely to emergency savings. And so, if you know that you're not going to have to whip out a credit card, that you're going to be able to pay for the roof that needs to be fixed or the car that breaks down, you're going to feel rich. I think wealth is often an emotional feeling. It's a mindset to feel truly secure and not to have to worry about finances at all. Feeling rich, it can feel much more about the bottom line and the dollar amount. So, one is more about the tangible number in your bank account and the other more is a feeling. Most people aspire to reach a level of wealth where they don't have to stress or worry about their finances, or even about the future generations to know that there's a cushion there. One of the things I focus on with my clients is a budget should be a representation of your values. The issue is you can't value everything. You have to prioritize it. And when people see the hard numbers of what they're spending on a monthly or annualized basis and they see, "Oh my gosh, I'm spending $5,000 a year eating out." I will ask the question, "Are you enjoying those meals at $5,000? Is it bringing you that much excitement and that much joy?" And most people will say, "No, Kamila, it's not. The food is okay. I'm doing it to be social." Well, we'll talk about other ways that you can be social without spending $5,000 a year eating out. You can't buy clothes consistently, travel consistently, eat out consistently. You have to pick maybe one or two things where you're going to focus your discretionary spending and then take that extra and reroute that to savings so you can start feeling rich. Spending gives this immediate dopamine fix, and seeing wealth in an account with numbers and commas often can be far more satisfying, though we don't realize it in the moment. It's almost like the difference between an exciting yet very toxic relationship versus a secure and stable long-term partner. I think the feeling of safety and the feeling that you can weather this long-term financial storm is far more satisfying, and we have to train ourselves to value that safety over the quick fix of a fancy car or a shiny new watch. My advice for somebody who's on the hamster wheel is understanding the trade-off. I could get off the hamster wheel at any time, and I know that. And I know that my circumstances may change. For right now, this is the answer for me. My advice to somebody who is feeling that anxiety is understand that this is a choice. There are trade-offs. What I have learned is that I have been very burned out and exhausted as a person who doesn't earn a lot of money, and I have been burned out and exhausted as a person who does earn a lot of money, and I would take being a person who's burned out and exhausted and has money any day of the week. I would rather do that than be burned out and tired and also worrying about my bills.

- 740 Park Avenue: Where the Ultra-Wealthy Go to Live | Vanity Fair: Kind: captions Language: en with apologies to F Scott Fitzgerald the rich are different from you and me they live in nicer Apartments none more so than 740 Park [Music] Avenue designed in 1929 by Rosario Candela and encased in a simple Limestone facade 7 40 Park seems at first glance rather understated the famous architect understood that wealth is something you possess not flaunt his Apartments were designed to be palaces with Walnut paneled rooms libraries Galleries and an abundance of light the largest was - This Is The RICHEST Invisible Billionaire Family Who Owns NEW YORK...: Kind: captions Language: en Every American woman over 45 has held a copy of Vogue or Vanity Fair at some point in her life. On a dentist's coffee table, in an airport [music] newsstand, or pressed flat across her lap on a long Sunday afternoon. She has studied the covers. She has read the profiles. She has absorbed [music] the taste, the authority, the opinions radiating from these pages like a cultural instruction manual. And she has never, not once, >> [music] >> heard the name of the family that owns them. Not on the masthead. Not in the editor's letter. Not in the celebrity profiles [music] that fill 200 pages every month. The Newhouse family has owned the most famous magazines in America for over 60 years, [music] accumulated a combined fortune of $24.1 billion, and remain so invisible to the culture they helped create that two biographers built their books from court records, >> subpoenas, and recollections of people who were fired. In today's episode of Old Money Luxury, we uncover how a son of Jewish immigrants from a New Jersey tenement built the most formidable media dynasty Americans have never heard of. Vogue, Vanity Fair, GQ, The New Yorker, Wired, and Architectural Digest. Six titles that represent a disproportionate share of what educated, affluent Americans have read, believed, and aspired to for the better part of a century. And every one of them is owned by a single family whose name does not appear anywhere in the pages of any of them. The family's name is Newhouse. Their holding company [music] is called Advance Publications, which sounds like the kind of name a focus group would reject for being too aggressively [music] corporate. And yet it has operated without serious competition in the private media world since 1949. The year it officially became Advance Publications Inc. Though the empire had been accumulating for nearly three decades before anyone bothered to give it a proper title. As of February 2024, Forbes placed the combined Newhouse family fortune at $24.1 billion, ranking them 13th among America's richest families. [music] A figure that includes at least 24 family members, and reflects holdings so diversified they now extend well beyond magazines into [music] cable television equity, a 30% stake in the social media platform Reddit, and a piece of Warner Bros. Discovery large enough to generate $1.1 billion when the family quietly sold a portion in June 2025. You will note that none of that made front-page news. That invisibility is not accidental. It is, in the Newhouse [music] family tradition, the entire point. This is a channel dedicated to old money, which means it is a channel dedicated to understanding the mechanics of wealth that does not need to announce itself. And the Newhouse story is, among other things, a graduate-level seminar in exactly [music] that discipline. If you want to go deeper on the architecture of generational wealth, subscribe to our free Substack newsletter, where we examine how the world's oldest fortunes are structured, protected, and passed down across centuries >> without public fanfare or press releases. The current head of operations is Steven Newhouse, son of Donald Newhouse, who is himself the 95-year-old surviving son of the empire's founder, Samuel Irving Newhouse Sr. A man born in a tenement on the Lower East Side of Manhattan in 1895, who quit school before high school, bought his first newspaper while most men his age were still paying off college debts, and died in 1979 owning 31 daily newspapers, Condé Nast, and Random House, with an estate the IRS would spend over a decade and several hundred million dollars trying and largely failing to tax. The Newhouse family has now controlled the most influential [music] glossy magazines in America since 1959. Longer than most of their readers have been alive. And when the last issue of Vogue ever closes its cover, there is a reasonable chance no journalist will manage to get the family on the record about it. Solomon Isidor Neuhaus was born on May 24th, 1895, in a tenement on the Lower East Side of Manhattan. The eldest of eight children born to Meyer Neuhaus, a Jewish immigrant from Vitebsk in the Russian Empire, in present-day Belarus, who had studied to become a rabbi, but arrived in America sickly, unskilled, and temperamentally unsuited for the work that the New World demanded. By 1908, Meyer Neuhaus had departed for Connecticut to live with a [music] sister, leaving his wife Rose, an Austrian-Hungarian immigrant who spent her days walking the streets of Bayonne, New Jersey, selling linens door-to-door to raise [music] eight children in whatever configuration of dignity a linen saleswoman's income could provide. Sam Newhouse, as he would come to be known, >> was 13 years old when his father left. He did not go back to school. Instead, he enrolled in a six-week bookkeeping course at the Gaffney School in Manhattan, then secured a position as office boy [music] for a Bayonne lawyer named Hyman Lazarus, a police court judge and local political figure who ran an operation that was, by the standards of early 20th century New Jersey machine politics, efficiently corrupt. Sam's privately [music] published memoir, A Memo to My Children, recorded his understanding with characteristic directness. Nothing had to be said when I became head of the family at so young an age. Within two years, Sam [music] had been promoted from office boy to office manager. By 16, he was running Lazarus's law practice. And then Lazarus made him an offer that would shape the American media landscape for the next century. Lazarus had accepted the failing Bayonne Times newspaper as payment for an unpaid legal debt, a newspaper so troubled that Lazarus handed it to his 16-year-old office manager [music] with instructions to go down and take care of the paper until we get rid of it, >> and the promise of half the profits if any materialized, on the reasonable expectation that none would. Sam went door-to-door through every shop in Bayonne, selling advertising, helping store owners plan sales campaigns, and turning the city into what one account described as a rash of advertised sales. And when the paper returned to profitability, Lazarus gave Sam a 20% ownership stake, later increased to 50%, >> and a salary of $30,000 a year. Sam had learned the fundamental truth of the newspaper business before he was old enough to vote. Editorial content was the draw, advertising was the engine, and anyone who confused the two would spend their career writing beautiful sentences in the red. In 1922, Sam pooled his savings >> and partnered once more with Judge Lazarus to purchase 51% of the Staten Island Advance for $98,000. In 1924, [music] Lazarus died, and Sam bought out his widow and the remaining 49%, becoming sole owner of a newspaper that would lend its name to an empire. The company that would eventually own Vogue was first called the Staten Island Advance Corporation, with a circulation covering a borough most of Manhattan regarded then, as now, as an afterthought, which suited Sam Newhouse perfectly. Sam Newhouse's acquisition strategy was so consistent it read like a formula typed on a single index card. Find a city with two competing newspapers, buy the struggling one at a bargain, promise both towns you had no intention of merging them, and then merge them. Killing the weaker edition, inheriting a local advertising monopoly, and using the resulting cash flow to fund the next acquisition. In 1932, he bought 51% of the Long Island Daily Press for approximately $750,000. In 1950, he paid $5.6 million for the Portland Oregonian, a record for a newspaper transaction at the time. In 1962, he acquired the Times-Picayune and the States-Item in New Orleans for $42 million. A figure Time magazine noted was more than three times what the Louisiana Territory cost the United States in 1803. In 1976, he bought Booth Newspapers, eight Michigan daily papers, including the Ann Arbor News, the Flint Journal, the Grand Rapids Press, and the Kalamazoo Gazette for $305 million, which was, at the time, the largest sum ever paid for a newspaper company. By the time of his death in 1979, Sam Newhouse owned 31 daily newspapers with a combined circulation exceeding 3 million, the third-largest newspaper chain in the United States after Gannett and Knight Ridder. But the acquisition that none of the business histories quite prepared anyone for was the one he made in 1959. Not for $300 million. Not for $42 million. But for $5 million. In 1959, Sam Newhouse paid approximately $5 million for a controlling block of stock in Condé Nast Publications. The circumstances, as they have been repeated in every biography and business history since, involve his wife. Mitzi Newhouse, born Mitzi Epstein from an upper-middle-class Jewish family on Manhattan's Upper West Side, the daughter of a silk tie importer, had married Sam in 1923 using his honeymoon savings, and she loved Vogue. The line later gave, quoted across decades of biographies, "She asked for a fashion magazine and I went out and got her Vogue." In 1959, Condé Nast published Vogue, Glamour, House & Garden, and Brides. In the same transaction, Newhouse inherited a deal already underway to acquire Street & Smith publications, paying an additional approximately $4 million, which added Mademoiselle, Charm, and various sports annuals to the portfolio. The man who had learned the newspaper business [music] in the back office of a Bayonne lawyer, now owned for $9 million in total, the beating cultural heart of aspirational American femininity. 34 years later, his son would spend $175 million just to acquire Architectural Digest and Bon Appétit, [music] a single deal worth nearly 20 times what Sam had paid for the entire Condé Nast empire. The old man's instinct for price was, even by those standards, in a class of its own. When Samuel Irving Newhouse Sr. died in August 1979, [music] he passed voting control of Advance Publications in trust to his six grandchildren, with his two sons, Si and Donald, as the sole trustees, and the division of duties between those sons was clean and total. Donald would run the newspapers and cable operations, Si would run Condé Nast. Samuel Irving Newhouse Jr., known universally as Si, had been publisher of US Vogue since 1964 and chairman of Condé Nast since 1975. And what he brought to the job was not a businessman's calculating eye, but something closer to an obsessive's consuming love of magazines, art, and the particular kind of talent that could make 200 pages of [music] glossy paper feel like the most urgent thing in the room. Si revived Vanity Fair in 1983, investing an estimated $10 million to relaunch a title folded into Vogue since 1936, [music] and then continued to fund it through the years it lost money before Tina Brown turned it into a cultural event. He bought The New Yorker in 1985 for an estimated $168 million. He acquired GQ, Wired, Architectural Digest, and Pitchfork across four decades of sustained magazine collecting that left Condé Nast as the most prestigious stable of publications in the English-speaking world. But Si Newhouse's tenure will be remembered as much for how he dismissed editors as for how he chose them. Grace Mirabella had run Vogue for 17 years when she learned she was no longer editor-in-chief, not from a meeting with Si, not from a letter, but from a gossip segment on Live at Five, the evening news program, on June 28th, 1988, [music] when her husband telephoned her and said a friend had heard Liz Smith announce that Anna Wintour would be taking over Vogue on August 1st. Mirabella's verdict, "They are in the communications business, but they don't know how to communicate." William Shawn had edited The New Yorker for 35 years >> when Si Newhouse informed him in January 1987 that he was out, and that Robert Gottlieb of Alfred A. Knopf would take his place. The staff response was volcanic. 154 writers, editors, and artists, including J. D. Salinger, John McPhee, Calvin Trillin, and Jamaica Kincaid, signed a letter asking Gottlieb not to accept the position. Fiction editor Daniel Menaker summarized the owner's calculation with a bluntness the biographers all quoted. "Si didn't give a and why should he? He owned the magazine." Si backed Wintour with an interest-free $1.6 million loan to purchase her Greenwich Village townhouse and a reported $200,000 [music] annual clothing allowance. And in her first 9 months at the helm, advertising revenue rose 17%. By 2013, as Si aged out of daily involvement, Wintour was appointed artistic director of all Condé Nast publications, explicitly created to absorb the responsibilities Si himself had previously handled, a coronation that told the industry everything it needed to know about [music] who would actually be making the decisions for the decades ahead. Thomas Maier spent years researching his 1994 biography of Si Newhouse, conducting more than 250 interviews, examining hundreds of pages of court testimony, reading books, newspapers, and periodicals in extraordinary volume. And on the very first page of the resulting book, he recorded the central fact of the entire project. Si Newhouse never responded to a written request for an interview. His brother Donald had, as Maier noted, much the same reaction. Carol Felsenthal's 1998 biography, Citizen Newhouse, was similarly constructed without the cooperation of the family, a Barron's review noting that the book was written without the cooperation of the notoriously reclusive Newhouse family, and indeed nearly never appeared in print at all. The Newhouse family's relationship with publicity has been, since the founding generation, a policy rather than a personality trait. Sam Newhouse Sr. ran his empire with no headquarters, no desk, and no files. He told an interviewer he had none, and his children inherited the same reflexive withdrawal from visibility. In January 1989, Si and Donald Newhouse appeared in federal tax court to contest the IRS's claims over their father's estate, and The Washington Post described the appearance as an uncommon public appearance for the two brothers, as though their materializing in a courtroom was a naturalist event worth documenting. [music] The lawyers who argued the family's case made the secrecy doctrine [music] explicit in their court filings. Obviously, the edge that they have over their competitors [music] in the acquisition of additional companies would be lost if their financial information were made public. Secrecy was a competitive strategy. It was also, in Si Newhouse's case, a profound personal irony. The man who owned the most glamour-obsessed, fame-celebrating publications in America, Vogue, Vanity Fair, The New Yorker, was himself congenitally averse to being seen. He went to bed at 9:00 in the evening. At his cocktail parties, the lights [music] dimmed at 8:00 to signal guests that it was time to leave. He arrived at the office in gray slacks and beat-up [music] loafers, as Politico recorded in its 2017 obituary, at 5:00 in the morning. Bloomberg, writing about the family's Reddit windfall [music] in March 2024, captured the pattern with a single sentence. The Newhouses had watched one billionaire clan after another surpass its wealth, and had barely registered public comment on the record-breaking windfall that Reddit's IPO produced. Michael Grossbaum, whose 2025 book, Empire of the Elite, examined Condé Nast's glittery heyday, identified what he called the central paradox of Si Newhouse's life. A new money Jew who felt excluded from stratified New York society had grasped the unique social possibilities available to the heir of Vogue, using the magazine to access the world from which he felt shut out, while simultaneously refusing, with perfect consistency, to let that world see him back. In 1980, [music] Si Newhouse bought Random House from RCA Corporation for approximately $70 acquiring the publisher of William Faulkner, Truman Capote, and James Joyce, and held it for 18 years, >> during which a source close to the family described the promised editorial synergies between the book house and the magazines with [music] a single word, "Crap." On November 7th, 1997, Si Newhouse's 70th birthday, Bertelsmann's incoming chairman, Thomas Middelhoff, mentioned casually to Newhouse that his [music] company might be interested in Random House if it were ever for sale. Newhouse said, "Give me a week to think about it." 4 months of secret negotiations followed, conducted under code names. Advance Publications was black, Si Newhouse [music] was black no. 1, Random House chairman Alberto Vitale was black no. 2, Bertelsmann was blue. On March 23rd, 1998, in a conference room at the law firm Davis Polk & Wardwell in Midtown Manhattan, Newhouse, in a sweater and chinos while the Bertelsmann team arrived in suits, signed the papers that sold Random House for an estimated $1.4 billion, turning a $70 million purchase into a 20-fold return. There was no applause at Random House's offices that day when the news was delivered. The dark chapter that followed this profitable transaction involved not [music] a failed deal, but a failed workforce. In June 2013, two former Condé Nast interns, Lauren Ballinger, [music] who had worked at W magazine, and Matthew Lieb, who had interned at The New Yorker, filed a class action lawsuit alleging violations of the Fair Labor Standards Act. Ballinger had been paid $12 a day for 12-hour shifts, amounting to approximately $1 per hour. 4 months after the suit was filed, Condé Nast ended its internship program entirely. In November 2014, the company agreed to pay $5.85 million to [music] settle the case, covering approximately 7,500 former interns >> who had worked between 2007 and 2013. Then came June 8th, 2020. Bon Appetit editor-in-chief Adam Rapoport resigned after food writer Tammie Teclemariam posted a 2013 Halloween photograph showing Rapoport and his wife >> in what many described as a Puerto Rican ethnic costume. His wife's caption including the hashtag "boricua". That same evening, assistant food editor Sohla El Waylly went public on Instagram with a second charge. That she had been hired at $50,000 per year, used as a display of diversity in the magazine's wildly popular YouTube Test Kitchen videos, and received no compensation for those appearances. While white editors were paid for theirs. The scandal destroyed much of the goodwill the Test Kitchen channel had built with millions of subscribers. And it forced a public confrontation with a racial pay disparity that the company had, until El Waylly named it, >> successfully kept internal. Condé Nast denied the specific pay allegation, but pledged an equity audit. And the contradiction between the company's aspirational lifestyle brand and its documented workplace practices had, by that point, been entered permanently into the public record. Yet none of the scrutiny reached the family that owned it all, because by 2020, [music] the Newhouse's were already sitting on an investment that would make the Random House sale look like a rounding error. In October 2006, [music] Condé Nast, acting through Advance Publications, had acquired Reddit for approximately $10 Reddit had been founded 16 months earlier in June 2005 by two University of Virginia graduates named Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian. And it was, at the time of acquisition, a small social link aggregation platform that showed no obvious promise of becoming one of the most visited [music] websites in the world. In 2011, Advance spun Reddit out from Condé Nast as an independent subsidiary reporting directly to Advance. At which point, Reddit had approximately 20 employees and 3 billion monthly page views. As Reddit went through successive funding rounds over the following decade, Series A, Series B through Series F, Advance's percentage ownership was gradually diluted. But the company retained its position as the largest single shareholder. And Steven Newhouse served as a non-voting board observer beginning in 2011. By August 2021, Reddit had raised $700 million in a Series F round led by Fidelity Management. Valuing the platform at over $10 billion. On March 21st, 2024, Reddit began trading on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker RDDT, priced at $34 per share. A figure that valued the company at approximately $6.4 billion on a fully diluted basis. By the close of that first day, shares had surged [music] 48% to $50.44. Advance Publications held 42.2 million shares. At closing price on the first day of trading, those shares were worth approximately $2.1 billion. A 5,800% return on a $10 million investment [music] made 18 years earlier in a website that was, at the time of purchase, worth less than several Manhattan studio apartments. Bloomberg described the Newhouse family as the biggest beneficiary of Reddit's IPO. By late 2024, Reddit stock had surged more than 300% from its IPO price. Advance announced plans to establish a credit facility using 7.8 million Reddit shares as collateral, valued at up to $1.2 billion. A move that allowed the family to monetize a portion of the gain without triggering a full sale and the tax consequences that would accompany one. The cable chapter of the family's diversification story had closed a decade earlier. In March 2015, [music] Charter Communications announced the acquisition of Bright House Networks, the cable provider Advance had built through decades of acquisitions into the sixth largest cable operator in the United States. Serving approximately 2 million [music] customers. For $10.4 billion in cash and stock. When the transaction closed in 2016, Advance received approximately a 13% stake in the newly enlarged Charter Communications. Converting the family's cable operating assets into permanent equity in one of the largest cable and internet companies in America. Before the Reddit windfall made that deal look, by comparison, like a conservative play. And with a fortune now measured in the tens of billions, the dynasty was about to face the one eventuality no deal structure had ever been designed to make invisible. Samuel Irving Newhouse Jr. died on October 1st, 2017 at his home in Manhattan, aged 89, after what his family described as a long illness. His brother Donald having confirmed to the press a year earlier that Si had been suffering from dementia. Graydon Carter, who had edited Vanity Fair under Si for 25 years, wrote that Newhouse died in the city he was born in and the one that gave foundation to the empire he built. The estate filing that followed told a story that required expert [music] commentary to make sense. Si's probate documents listed $114 million in assets against $400 million in liabilities. A net position of nearly negative $300 million >> despite Forbes having valued his fortune at $12.1 billion that same year. A probate attorney quoted by the New York Post explained the arithmetic bluntly. If he died with $12 billion, he'd be paying $6 billion in estate tax. The family had learned, during the decade-long IRS battle over Sam Newhouse Sr.'s estate. In which the government claimed $914 million in taxes and fraud penalties, and ultimately collected approximately $48 million. That the mechanics of estate planning were worth mastering with the same intensity one applied to acquiring newspapers. Si's fortune had been moved over his lifetime into trusts and limited liability structures that left almost nothing exposed to the probate process. Entirely within the law and entirely in keeping [music] with a family that had spent a century ensuring its financial architecture remained invisible to anyone who was not a party to it. Donald Newhouse, born August 5th, 1929, was 95 years old when he appeared at the dedication of Newhouse Family Plaza at Syracuse University in August 2024. One of his rarest public appearances, captured in a YouTube video in which the crowd sang happy birthday to a man who had appeared on every Forbes 400 list since the inaugural 1982 edition without once missing it. He remains worth somewhere between $11 billion and $14.9 billion. Steven Newhouse, Donald's son, co-president of Advance Publications, Reddit board member, and former Warner Brothers Discovery independent director, was described by an anonymous source to the New York Post in 2019 as the main decision-maker and really the center of the whole thing. And maintains, by documented family tradition, no public profile worth locating. The Newhouse empire in 2025 comprises Condé Nast, a portfolio of daily newspapers, a 13% stake in Charter Communications, approximately 30% of Reddit, and a cluster of other assets, all held within Advance Publications, which files no public reports, holds no earnings calls, and has no press office in any conventional sense. The dynasty that began in a Bayonne, New Jersey law office in 1911, when a 13-year-old boy walked through the door looking for work, is now in its fourth generation. And the only thing more certain than its continued invisibility is that the family prefers it [music] that way. The Newhouse family has spent over a century making the world more famous while remaining unknown themselves. Now, we want to turn it over to you. Drop a comment below and let us know what you think. Is that level of deliberate privacy the ultimate old money move? Or does owning the magazines that define cultural fame while hiding from that same fame strike you as something closer to a contradiction worth examining? [music] Thanks for watching, and thanks for being part of Old Money Luxury. We'll see you in the next one.

- How Much Money Is Trump Profiting from the Presidency? | The New Yorker: Kind: captions Language: en-US - For months, I've been reading articles and commentary about how much President Donald Trump and his family are enriching themselves off of the presidency. I'm David Kirkpatrick. I'm a staff writer for The New Yorker Magazine. No one I talked to could put a figure on it. No one could tell me just how much he made. And at the same time, I was increasingly puzzled that more people didn't seem to care. That the public, more or less, was shrugging at the way the Trump family was monetizing the presidency. So I set out to try to come up with a number, to try to count for myself, how much money the Trump family was making, more or less, off the presidency. Money that in some way depended on his status as president. [soft music] The problem of a potential conflict of interest posed for President Trump because of his business interests has been there from the beginning. Around the time that he began preparing to run for office, around 2015, the Apprentice was well off the air, the Celebrity Apprentice, it's kind of gimmicky successor, it was also dwindling in the ratings. [people shouting] There's some evidence to suggest that he needed something new to keep his name in the public eye. As part of the backlash against him, a number of people brought lawsuits. He now owes $500 million or so to the state of New York because of their fraud case against him. - I did nothing wrong. There were no victims. The bank loves us. - And he owes another 88 million or so to Eugene Carroll, the writer who sued him for defamation and sexual assault years ago. So arguably he was really in kind of a tight place when he went into the presidency the first time and a much tighter place the next time. I was personally astonished to see just how much his financial position has improved just six months after he came back into the White House. The obvious place to start was Mar-a-Lago. Trump is the first president to sell access to himself and his inner circle for personal profits. That is, this is a private club that people pay to join, and what they get is a chance to mingle with Trump and his friends. Before he first won election, the initiation fee to join the club was a hundred thousand dollars. As of last fall, it was poised to go up to a million. Doing a little bit of math based on the revenue he's disclosed on his personal financial disclosure forms and the cost over time, I concluded that he had made about 125 million in extra profits at Mar-a-Lago because of his presidencies. After that, I looked at his campaign fundraising. Now, campaign fundraising is generally a whole different thing. Laws restrict candidates or politicians from using that money for personal purposes. In Trump's case, those boundaries are a little bit fuzzier. For one thing, he quite conspicuously sells what looks like campaign merchandise through his personal online store. If you're a Trump supporter, you might really believe you're supporting the MAGA movement and its causes when you buy this stuff. But in fact, the money is actually going to the Trump organization and to Donald Trump. We're talking about things like a Gulf of America baseball hat $50, a pair of Trump beer coozies, $18, Trump flip flops, $40. In the last year, he's actually branched out from selling the merchandise through the store to a couple of special lines of products, and that includes 2.8 million from Trump watches, 2.5 million from Trump sneakers and fragrances, and 3 million from an illustrated book called Save America, as well as 1.3 million from the God Bless the USA Bible, also known as the Trump Bible. So all of that came to 27.7 million. In some ways, this is almost like a fashion designer setting up a table in front of a department store to sell knockoffs of their own goods at the expense of their shareholders. So that came to about more than $17 million in income over the last several years. Now, on top of that, there's another way that Trump has found, and that is his legal fees. A political campaign fund for a candidate can't spend money on personal legal fees, but he discovered that if he converted a campaign fund into a political action committee, it could. And he's used his political action committee to cover more than a hundred million dollars in personal legal fees. So all in all, I'm gonna call that a total of about $127.7 million in enrichment through those campaign funds, either in merch or legal fees. So I concluded the spending at his hotels by government agencies, or by influence seekers looking to curry favor probably came out to be a wash. clean and decorate so break down those big daunting tasks into smaller chunks while making it tasty few examples would be start a lot of laundry in the morning dry in the afternoon and fold and keep it away in your organized wardrobe in the evening another example would be breaking down cooking chore into grocery list meal plan weekly meal prep and cooking will never be a chore rather a breeze another one that is my favorite is that i break down all of my youtube work i shoot on one day script and voice over on another and edit on day number three this way i always feel fun and energetic to create new content for you guys and i'm always creative not stressing out to meet the deadline tip number five have a place for everything and have everything in its place life works better when you know where your things are it's very frustrating having to hunt for something you know that you have but can't find and conversely very satisfying when your home runs like a well oiled machine and you can find everything you want when you need it clutter is caused by a failure to return things to where they belong therefore storage should reduce the effort needed to put things away not the effort needed to get them out so take the pain to organize once organize your kitchen your pantry wardrobes organize your house make a place for everything and if something does not have a place please donate when you get home each day take five minutes to put things back in their places if you carry your laptop put it back at your desk if you carried a book with you place it back on its shelf if you are wearing a jewelry return it to its home the same thing goes for your makeup shoes your keys even your handbag make it a part of your i just got home ritual tip number six to have good cleaning products and gadgets invest in tools that make cleaning easier and quicker a stick vacuum is way better than using a broom that sweeps dust from one place to another if you're a gadget freak invest in a robot vacuum it doesn't do a perfect job i would say but it makes house cleaning a whole lot easier and frees up your time also the attachments with the vacuum helps you easily clean sofa or fly screen which otherwise could be a very tough job one of my favorite products is toilet blocks it eliminates the need to clean the toilet bowls on daily basis as a bonus your toilet never builds up that ugly brown ring and smells good always have less cleaning products use multi-purpose spray rather than having one for bathroom one for kitchen one for glass etc less is more also it prevents you from being exposed to various harmful chemicals on daily basis if possible go green mix lemon and water in a bottle and wipe kitchen counters use water and vinegar to clean bathroom surfaces and so on baking soda and vinegar works best for cooktops to clean chimney block drains etc tip number seven create a cleaning routine the key to managing household duties quickly and efficiently is to design an easy to follow routine that includes all the most important tasks i have a morning and evening cleaning routine morning routine includes 30 minutes regular cleaning daily and 30 minutes deep cleaning few times a week having deep cleaning as part of your cleaning routine eliminate the need to wait for diwali to deep clean evening routine includes kitchen cleanup post dinner prep i don't wait to eat dinner to clean up my kitchen as i would get lazy i do it prior and quick 15 minutes to pick up the house my house is never perfect guys it's never perfectly tidy or clean at all times but certainly under control morning and evening rituals help us pay more attention to our kitchens and how we feel and work in them tip number eight don't sit down before all tasks are done in short don't crash on the couch once dinner is cooked don't crash clean as you go as much as you can and whatever cleaning is left do it before you crash on your couch once you crash i bet 99.9 you will feel lazy to get up and clean and you will certainly procrastinate it for tomorrow leaving you a stressful and messy morning so resist that urge to crash rather force clean up then relax tip number nine donate then accumulate keep a donation box the more things you possess the more cluttered your house will get possessions not only clutter your physical space but steals your mental energy too so take time to clean and organize keep stuff that you will really use and sparks joy in you as mary kondo says donate the stuff that do not spark joy there is more joy in owning less than can be found in owning more and it is far better to donate than accumulate tip number 10 keeping floors clean we all know how quickly dirt dust and grime builds up prevention is better than cure use doormats at entrance take your shoes off and ask guests to do likewise too immediately wipe up any spills especially on porous surfaces like marble other than that sadly you've simply got to clean girl so invest in a good vacuum that will suck all the dirt and dust off the floor rather than sweeping or brooming that kind of stirred from one place to the another also having a spinner mop is great as its microfiber mop head is idle for all flows and no bending required though our indian way of mopping is great workout it takes much longer to mop compared to a spin mop mopping the flow every day is a good practice as it adds a lot of positivity and freshness to the room instead of adding disinfectant to the water simply add some sea salt and then do the mopping this will take away much of the negative energy and will raise the vibrations of the room salt is very effective way to get rid of pollution inside your home add one spoon salt to a bucket of water at least twice a week salt has been used since ages by our grandmothers to remove evil eye by others so give it a try tip number eleven clean up spillages immediately this is no brainer clean up spillages immediately rather than getting it dry as dry stains need more time and effort and energy to clean than fresh spills especially if you have kids around this is a very handy tip kids make mess and spill while eating so attend to it then and there and not put it for later as it will take away longer to clean then i know i know we cannot always attend to spillages immediately especially if there are few drops here and there in that case spray a disinfectant using spray bottle five minutes before mopping and mopping the floors would be so easy as the spillages would be moist again tip number 12 use squeegee water vinegar spray to keep bathroom mold away tired of bathroom mold try this mix half water and half vinegar into a spray bottle and once you are done showering simply spray this mixture and leave bathroom open for an hour to ventilate you can even squeegee to take off excess water of glass and the walls vinegar will prevent any further mold formation however it will not magically remove the mould that is already sitting there but after shower try this spray vinegar water mixture and take a toothbrush and thoroughly scrub it once your bathroom is free from mold spraying diluted vinegar will prevent mold from reoccurring tip number 13 keep counters clear clutter starts at the counter top which is why clutter free kitchens you'll notice always have clean and clear counters resist the urge to use the needs so that you stay on top of your clothes and laundry never has a chance to pile up whatever suits you every other day or once a week or every day have a laundry routine start washing machine in the morning dry clothes in the afternoon and put away in the evening few hacks that helped me in my laundry routine i use hangers for clothes that can go straight to the wardrobe post drying fold clothes straight off the rack and do not tuck them away in a laundry basket to be attended to later on as later never comes and clothes keep moving from one room to the another tip number 15 done is better than perfect you don't have to steam mop your flow every night when a quick vacuum and spot clean will do the job to make the bed in the morning time yourself to one minute to make your bed putting off cleaning and tidying your home because you think you don't have time to do it perfectly leave you living in constant chaos so follow the mantra done is better than perfect so don't aim for perfection rather aim to tick off the item of your to-do list as it is way more satisfying than to have it perfectly clean i have a daily five-minute bathroom cleaning routine where i quickly take out the towels flow mats etc from the bathroom next i spray and wipe the toilet with the toilet paper and flush it i clean the sink after i brush my teeth in the morning so it's already clean and i spray   1. @Anonymous-w1r8c [9 likes]: I have never had a maid, over 5 years since I got married and had my kids. I use machines to replace maids. What has helped me is: 
1. Weekly grocery delivery - I haven't gone to the market or store in over 2 years. 
2. Like you said, batch cooking three meals each week 
3. I use a robot vacuum and mop so my floors look mopped everyday 
4. I have a washing machine and a dryer. We wash our clothes by color on Friday. I fold clothes as we watch tv. 
5. We have a dishwasher - we load dishes as we use them, I run it before bathing at night and after bathing I unload the dishes. 
6. I wipe kitchen counters and sink before evening bath (this takes less than 10 mins) 
7. I clean the bathroom every evening as I bath (I have timed myself before and this takes 9 mins)
8. I have an instant pot and crockpot I had brought from Europe. I dump my ingredients in it and by the time we are ready to eat, everything is ready (I use it only for stews and soups). 
I love how you said time with God. Time with God puts everything in perspective.

Don't leave it until later, if there's something that you can be tidying while you're also doing something else, then definitely do it. So for instance, if I'm cooking, I make sure that I have no dead time. Because my boys are that bit older and they can sit up on their own, it just makes sense for me to be cleaning while they're happily playing. And on that note, follow the five minute rule and the one touch method. The five minute rule is exactly as it sounds, if you see a task in front of you and it will only take five minutes to do, just do it now. So for instance, if you have a messy junk drawer and it would actually only take five minutes to sort out, just go ahead and do it immediately, this is a good rule to apply to life in general. And the one touch method is something I also try to live by, and that is just putting an item away with one touch. So for instance if you're brushing your hair, and then you put your hairbrush down somewhere, and then it eventually gets back to where it should be, instead of doing that, just brush your hair and then put your brush back where it belongs, and the same thing goes for when you walk in the door with your coat. Rather than chucking your coat down on the couch and then eventually putting it away or putting it on a coat, just take it off and with one touch put it away where it belongs. My next tip is one that professional cleaners use, and that is to walk into a room and to clean it clockwise, and from top to bottom. So rather than walk into a room and be overwhelmed by how messy it is, run here, there and everywhere to try and clean it as best you can, walk into a room, choose a starting point, and then work your way around clockwise, and always clean from the highest point down to the floor. That's because dust will settle, so there's no point in cleaning the floors if you still have to wipe the surfaces. And when it comes to big tasks like laundry and decluttering, I really like to break those up and do a little bit every day. So instead of having one huge laundry day, I will just do a load every day of the week, so in the morning I'll put on a load of laundry as part of my routine, I'll dry it in the afternoon, and I'll put it away every evening, and it really does feel like less of a burden than a huge laundry day at the weekend. And while we're talking about laundry, I also get so many questions about ironing and my ironing routine, but the truth is, I rarely ever iron, and that is because I fold clothes immediately out of the tumble dryer. As soon as I hear that buzzer go off, I am at the tumble dryer and I am folding them, because I find when I do that, I get so many less creases in our clothes. And my next tip is to read the labels on your cleaning products, and the instructions as well. Now, I know that sounds really simple and very obvious, but be honest, how often do you do that? And some cleaning products require a dwell time, which means they need to sit on your surfaces for one to five minutes to really disinfect properly, so check your cleaning products. My next tip is to always be decluttering. Rather than just doing a huge declutter once or twice a year, keep a basket in your car or in your garage where you can put items that you no longer want or need, and then if you're passing by the charity shop, you can donate them. This is a great way to stay on top of things, and if you ever have 10 minutes in the day, maybe you can find the time to declutter just one drawer. If you do that often and it becomes a habit, it will really keep your house cleaner. And once you have decluttered, have a place for everything, and have everything in its place. And this is straight out of the Marie Kondo book, which I'm a huge fan of, but tidying really is easier if everything has a place to live, so we have invested in some clever storage solutions. We had this little shoe storage built underneath our stairs, because there was just some dead space there. We also love to have gas lift beds, because we can store all of our seasonal clothes and toys under them, we really do try to make the most of the space that we have. My next tip is a great one, and one that I'm really trying to instill in our children, and that is don't leave a room empty-handed, and don't go upstairs empty-handed either. Often there are items that need to go back to the kitchen, that need to go to the dishwasher, so for instance in the morning you're likely to have cups upstairs that need to go back downstairs, and there are always little things on our stairs that need to return up it. We actually invested in a little stair basket as well, so that we can just grab the stair basket and take that up with us. My next tip is to work smart and to get great equipment, you're more likely to clean if you love the products that you're using, so recently I got a robot vacuum as well, and it's been such a game-changer for me. I also love my spray mop, it's really fun to use, and it's a really cool gadget. I know I've shown you it before, but I really love having it, and I always had my favorite cleaning cloth that I knew worked really well, so I decided to buy like five or six of them so that I always have the cloth that I really like to use. And buying new cleaning products so that you clean more is a bit like buying new gym wear so that you go to the gym more. My next tip is to create cleaning routines, and if you don't know where to start, I've made so many videos about my cleaning routines, I have a morning one and an evening one as well, but just look at your day and see if there's anywhere where you can carve out time that you can actually tidy and clean, and again by making it a habit, it won't feel so heavy, and it'll just fit into your life. So for instance, every night when I go to bed, I put the dishwasher on, and then every morning I just empty it, and I kind of don't even notice that I'm doing it, because it is just one of my habits. My next tip is to keep cleaning products near where you're going to use them, and I feel like this tip is a little bit controversial, because a lot of people keep all their cleaning products in one place together, and I definitely have one place where the bulk of mine are, and then if I do a big house clean, I'll take them all with me, but I feel like by having a couple of cleaning products upstairs, like a surface spray and a glass cleaner, I'm more likely to quickly clean our mirrors, or just quickly wipe down our sink or our toilets, so for me that really works, having a few of the products near where I'm actually going to use them. My next tip is to set a timer and speed clean. Now, I've made so many speed cleaning videos in the past on my channel, but it really does work, you will be amazed at how much you can get done in just 10 or 15 minutes, so set a timer on your phone, decide to conquer one room, and you'll be amazed how clean it is in just 15 minutes. This next tip really does work for me, and that is don't sit down. So in the evening, when all I want to do is crash, I make sure that all the tasks are done before I do that, because I know as soon as I sit down on that couch, I will not be getting up and accomplishing very much at all, so I try to do it all before I sit down. Another thing that makes cleaning easier for me is that I treat it like me time, so whenever I'm cleaning, I'm also multitasking, whether I am listening to an audiobook or a podcast, sometimes I like to call my mom and my sister and catch up with them, because I can be cleaning and chatting away at the same time. - What It Takes To Stage A $14 Million NYC Apartment: Kind: captions Language: en [Narrator] When you're trying to sell a place, you want to make potential buyers feel at home. But when you're selling an apartment that's, say, $14 million, that's a 7,200-square-foot triplex penthouse with 3 1/2 bathrooms, skylights, high ceilings, and over 1,500 square feet of private outdoor space, well, that would sell in an instant, right? Not necessarily. That's where Cheryl Eisen comes in. She's the go-to luxury-apartment stager in the New York area. She does more than help sell homes. She sells you on a dream life. The whole purpose of staging is to style and furnish a place for sale, to highlight its selling points for potential buyers, and help them envision themselves living in the space. Cheryl is the CEO of Interior Marketing Group, a New York City-based company that does interior design, staging, and marketing for luxury homes. Her past buyers and clients include Chrissy Teigen and John Legend, Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner, Kim Kardashian West and Kanye West, Bethenny Frankel, and Swedish real-estate broker Fredrik Eklund. The homes she stages? They start at $5 million and go as high as $230 million. The cost of the pieces for a staging is usually about 1% of the listing price of the home. Today, we're going to follow Cheryl and her team as she stages a loft that is expected to sell at around $14 million in Tribeca. - Ecological Footprints: How Your Daily Life Shapes the Planet: Kind: captions Language: en Our planet. A blue oasis in the vastness of space. A world teeming with life from the smallest insects to the largest whales. A delicate balance. A web of interconnected ecosystems. But this precious balance is under threat. And the cause is us humans. We are consuming resources at an unsustainable rate. Our actions leaving a mark on the environment. This mark, the measure of our impact, is our ecological footprint. It's time to understand this footprint and take steps to lighten our tread on this planet we call [Music] home. Imagine the Earth's resources as a giant cake. Each person gets a slice, but some take much larger portions. That's what an ecological footprint represents. The amount of land and resources needed to support our lifestyle. It considers everything we consume and the waste we generate. From the food we eat to the clothes we wear, every choice contributes to our footprint. The larger the footprint, the greater the impact on our planet. Every day we make choices that impact our planet. Driving a car, for instance, consumes fossil fuels and releases greenhouse gases. Choosing a plant-based meal over a meat-heavy diet reduces the land and water needed for livestock. Our homes too play a role. Energy efficient appliances and reducing water consumption all make a difference. Even small changes in our daily routines can add up to a significant reduction in our collective footprint. The future of our planet depends on our actions today. We must recognize the impact of our choices and embrace a more sustainable way of life. This means consuming less, wasting less, and making conscious decisions that benefit both people and the planet. It's about choosing renewable energy sources, supporting sustainable businesses, and advocating for policies that protect our environment. Every step we take towards a smaller footprint is a step towards a healthier planet. Together, we can make a difference. Let's tread lightly and ensure a brighter future for generations to come.

- How do buildings affect the environment: Kind: captions Language: en How do buildings affect the environment? Buildings have a big impact on the environment. The construction and renovation of buildings uses precious natural resources. Then, when in use, buildings are responsible for a significant proportion of all our carbon dioxide emissions. Energy used in the construction and use of our homes and other buildings accounts for around half of the UK's total carbon dioxide emissions. Under the Climate Change Act 2008, we have a legally binding target to reduce these emissions by at least 80% by 2050. Globally, it's a similar picture. Buildings account for around 35% of resources, 40% of energy use, consume 12% of the world's drinkable water, and produce almost 40% of global carbon dioxide emissions. So, what can we do? Producers of building materials like us can ensure this is done in ways so as not to adversely affect the environment. We believe we've performed one of the largest number of life cycle analysis in the UK on our product range. These environmental product declarations, or EPDs, show how our products have been made in a sustainable way, minimizing energy usage, and helping to protect the environment. In addition, many of the products and solutions we produce then go on to help minimize energy use in the buildings that have been built or renovated. Poorly designed and constructed buildings use more energy, increasing the demand on energy production, and contributing to global warming. Reducing energy use in buildings is one of the most important ways to reduce humans' overall environmental impact.

- Ecological footprint: How Many Planets Do We Need in 2026?: Kind: captions Language: en Hi Alex here! What is our ecological&nbsp; footprint? Today I am going to use&nbsp;&nbsp; illustrations to explain what it is and how&nbsp; we fit on pour planet. So, let’s get drawing! This is what we need to provide the resources&nbsp; we use and to absorb the waste we produce: 1.5&nbsp;&nbsp; planets. That means that it takes about a year and&nbsp; a half for our planet to regenerate what we use in&nbsp;&nbsp; a year. The Global Footprint Network calculates&nbsp; every year Earth Overshoot Day and in 2013 it was&nbsp;&nbsp; on August 19th: that was the day when we finished&nbsp; using our resources for the year. Think about it&nbsp;&nbsp; as a bank account: for the first 7 months and 20&nbsp; days we lived on our annual revenue. After that,&nbsp;&nbsp; we spent our capital. Humm. Seems like&nbsp; there is something wrong with that math… Our ecological footprint measures how much&nbsp; land and water area we need to produce the&nbsp;&nbsp; resources we use (things like energy,&nbsp; food, land for settlements, timber,&nbsp;&nbsp; seafood) and to absorb the waste we generate.&nbsp; Our biocapacity is the amount of biologically&nbsp;&nbsp; productive area available to provide the resources&nbsp; we use and to absorb our waste. So we can compare&nbsp;&nbsp; footprint and biocapacity to see if we are well&nbsp; balanced or not. Let’s dig into that a little… I live in Canada. Our ecological footprint in&nbsp; 2007 (published in 2010) was 7.01 meaning that&nbsp;&nbsp; to provide what we consume every year, we need&nbsp; 7.01 global hectares per person (gha/pers). But&nbsp;&nbsp; we live in a huge country and our biocapacity&nbsp; was actually 14.92 gha/pers so if we do the&nbsp;&nbsp; math 14.92-7.01=7.91 so technically we are an&nbsp; “ecological creditor” country. Does that mean&nbsp;&nbsp; that it is okay to consume as much as we do? Well,&nbsp; not really. If the whole world lived like us,&nbsp;&nbsp; we would need more than 4 planets to provide&nbsp; the resources we use and to absorb our waste,&nbsp;&nbsp; because not every nation has so much land, such&nbsp; huge forests and so many other natural resources. The USA has a footprint of 8 and a&nbsp; biocapacity of 3.87, so 3.87-8=-4.13:&nbsp;&nbsp; they are a big “ecological debtor” country. This&nbsp; is actually the case of most developed countries. Among the smaller footprints is the Democratic&nbsp; Republic of the Congo with 0.75. Although it&nbsp;&nbsp; is not an economically rich country at&nbsp; all, it has a biocapacity of 2.76 so you&nbsp;&nbsp; see that it is also an ecological&nbsp; creditor country (2.76-0.75=2.01). The Global Footprint Network published the&nbsp; footprint in low-, middle- and high-income&nbsp;&nbsp; countries between 1961 and 2008. Here&nbsp; is the world’s average biocapacity.&nbsp;&nbsp; What do you notice? It’s systematically&nbsp; decreasing. So the story basically reads,&nbsp;&nbsp; the wealthier people are, the bigger&nbsp; their footprint. When we can afford it,&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s hard to resist I guess! This is confirmed&nbsp; by the fact that 17% of the world’s population&nbsp;&nbsp; consumes 80% of the world’s resources. So the main&nbsp; problem is not the absolute lack of resources;&nbsp;&nbsp; it is the fact that our global consumption&nbsp; is extremely uneven and inefficient. These were footprints for Nations but you can also&nbsp; measure the ecological footprint of an individual,&nbsp;&nbsp; a city, a business or all of humanity to assess&nbsp; our pressure on the planet. I calculated my own&nbsp;&nbsp; personal footprint on the Global Footprint&nbsp; Network website and it is around 5.5. Why is&nbsp;&nbsp; it significantly less than Canada’s footprint&nbsp; of 7? Well, mostly thanks to my lifestyle:&nbsp;&nbsp; I rarely eat meat, drive very little,&nbsp; live in a highly energy efficient house,&nbsp;&nbsp; and so on. You can go and calculate your&nbsp; own if you like, I included the link below. The Ecological Footprint is a really useful way&nbsp; of understanding our relationship to the planet,&nbsp;&nbsp; and how the ways in which we live&nbsp; impact our ability to survive and&nbsp;&nbsp; thrive as a race over time. Try&nbsp; it – you may find it interesting! As usual here are the key points to keep in mind:&nbsp; Humanity uses more resources&nbsp; than the Earth provides.&nbsp; The main problem is not the absolute lack&nbsp; of resources; it is the fact that our global&nbsp;&nbsp; consumption is unevenly distributed and favours&nbsp; only a few. It’s also extremely inefficient.&nbsp; Ecological footprint is a great tool&nbsp; to assess the pressure we put on the&nbsp;&nbsp; planet. It helps us keep the big picture&nbsp; in mind and not get lost in the details. In the next video, we will use the&nbsp; ecological footprint combined with&nbsp;&nbsp; a few other things to create a&nbsp; powerful metaphor describing our&nbsp;&nbsp; sustainability challenges. So stay&nbsp; tuned and thank you for watching.

what the writer says and this is what you're going to sign at close of escrow now if that is not your intent and you are saying well at some point in the future i might use that home either as a primary residence or a second home but initially my intent will be to put a long-term tenant in the property then that would be an investment property classification which means that the lending together with homeowner associations on condominiums many times we'll have upfront dues so we do try to project that into the total cost analysis out of the gate to give clarity around expected cash corrects itself very quickly because those of us that are investors we want to tap into this hot rental market and so we go buy a bunch of property and turn it into rental property or build a bunch of - Renting vs. Buying a Home: The 8.71% Rule: Kind: captions Language: ja 賃貸か持ち家かという話は、 本当によく出てきますよね。 では、 経済的にはどちらが 得なのでしょうか？ 賃貸の場合、 総コストは大家 さんに支払う、 二度と戻ってこな いお金だけです。 例えば、 家賃が月二千ドルなら、 十二ヶ月を掛けて年 間二万四千ドルが、 もう戻ってこない お金になります。 一方、 持ち家の総コストには、 利息と元本からなる住宅 ローンの返済に加え、 年間の維持費などの諸 経費が含まれます。 公平な比較のため、 どちらのシナリオ でも戻らないお 金を計算する必 要があります。 この独自の視点は、 カナダのポートフォ リオマネージャー、 ベン・フェリックス氏がYo uTubeで提唱しました。 彼の動画「賃貸か購入か： 五%ルール」が元です。 動画は下にリンクします。 全ての功績は彼にあります。 しかし、 彼の動画が公開され てから四年が経ち、 最近では住宅ローン金利が ずっと高くなっています。 今は六・五から七 ・五%の間です。 なので、 このトピックは今の 時代に合わせてアッ プデートする必要が あると感じました。 ここで解明したい 主なポイントは、 持ち家のコストです。 それを月単位で出す 必要があります。 そうすれば、 家賃と比較して、 どちらが自分にとって良い選 択肢なのかを判断できます。 理論上、 月々の持ち家コストが同等の 物件の家賃より安いなら、 賃貸より購入すべ きでしょうし、 その逆もまた然りです。 さて、ベン氏によると、 持ち家のコスト、 この月々のコスト計算には三 つの要素があると言います。 それは固定資産税、維持費、 そして住宅ロー ンの返済です。 住宅ローンの返済は、 今日詳しく説明する二つの異 なる要素に分けられます。 これらのコストはすべて持 ち家に関連するもので、 賃貸では発生しません。 まず、 固定資産税につい て話しましょう。 固定資産税は、 所有するすべての不 動産に課されます。 基本的には、 その不動産を所 有することで、 住んでいる政府や州に、 不動産価値の一定の割 合を毎年税金として 納めなければならな いということです。 アメリカでは、 すべての住宅用不 動産における平均 固定資産税率は一 ・一一%です。 今日の例ではこ の数字を使い、 また、 五十万ドルの家を 例に挙げます。 カナダ、オーストラリア、 あるいはヨーロッパのどこか にお住まいの方のために、 今日は皆さんがご自 身の数字を入力でき る特別なツールをリ ンクしておきます。 ですが、この動画では、 先ほどの例で進めましょう。 なので、このような家だと、 毎年五千五百ドルの固定資産 税を支払うことになります。 これが一・一一%です。 そして、 これが二度と戻って こないコストです。 次のコストは、 家の維持費です。 専門家は、 不動産価値の年間一% から二%を維持費と して確保しておくこと を推奨しています。 家がとても古くて、 いつも修理が必要だ と感じる場合は、 もう少し高くなる かもしれません。 計算を簡単にするため、 今日は一%を使います。 五十万ドルの家の 年間の維持費は、 その一%になります。 つまり、年間五千ドルです。 さて、ここまでで、 持ち家で戻ってこな いコストである固 定資産税と維持費 を計算しました。 これは不動産価 値の年間約二か ら二・一一%に相当します。 さて、次に、 住宅ローンの返済コストを 計算する必要があります。 ベン氏はこれを資本コ ストと呼んでいます。 これはつまり、 この家にお金をつ ぎ込むことで、 実際にはどれだけのコス トがかかっているのか、 ということです。 頭金が二十%だとすると、 残りの八十%の家の価値 に対して融資を受ける、 つまりローンを組む ことになります。 また簡単な数字を使うと、 家の価値が五十万ドルなら、 二十%の頭金として 十万ドルを払い、 残りの四十万ドルを、 おそらく三十年かけて返 済することになります。 これが最も一般的な 住宅ローンです。 ここには二つの変動 要素があります。 まず頭金のコスト、 そしてローンにか かるコストです。 これが負債コストです。 よく耳にするのが、 お金には機会費用がある、 つまり、 頭金に十万ドルを払うと、 その十万ドルを株式 市場に投資して、 さらにお金を増やすことが できなくなるわけです。 これは、 かなりもっともな懸念です。 頭金の機会費用 がいくらなのか を計算する必要があります。 これが資本コスト計 算の第一部です。 データによると、 S&P 500の過去三十年 間のインフレ調整後 の年間平均リターン は七・一九%です。 一方、住宅用不動産、 つまりアメリカの過去三十年 間の住宅価格の中央値は、 インフレを考慮した後で、 実際には一・九七% 上昇しています。 ここで意図的に住宅価格 の中央値を使ったのは、 もし商業用や賃貸用の 物件を含めていたら、 不動産のリター ンがもっと高く 歪められてしまうからです。 ほとんどの人は家を借りて、 それを住宅用不動産 と比較するので、 住宅用不動産価格の中央 値を使うべきでしょう。 簡単にするために、 株式の年間平均リ ターンが約七%、 不動産が約二%だとすると、 この二つの差は約 五%になります。 株式は不動産を年間五%上回 っているということです。 その十万ドルは、 もし株式市場に投資 していれば年間五 %多く稼げたはず のお金なのです。 この家に投資していなければ 得られたはずのお金です。 後で実際の数字を 当てはめますが、 まずはこの概念を説明 したかったのです。 これが資本コスト、 つまり住宅ローン返済 コストの第一部です。 アメリカ以外にお 住まいの方は、 世界の不動産リターンや、 ご自身の国の不動産リ ターンをググって、 500や他の株価指数と比 較することができます。 私が調べたところでは、 国にもよりますが、 株式は不動産を大体三%から 五%上回る傾向にあります。 資本コスト計算の第二部は、 利息の支払いがも たらすコスト、 別名、負債コストです。 今日の簡単な計算では、 これを年間二万八千 ドルと仮定します。 ローン残高 に年利を掛けるという単 純な方法を使います。 こうすることで、 どんな家が買えるのか、 そして戻ってこないコスト は何かを把握する上で、 かなり保守的なバッ ファーができます。 住宅ローンを組む際の 重要な考慮事項です。 さて、 ここまでついて きていますか。 資本コストは、 頭金の機会費用、 これは十万ドルの五パーセ ントで五千ドルでしたね。 それに年間の利息支払い二 万八千ドルを加えます。 合計で年間三万 三千ドルが戻っ てこないコストになります。 これをパーセンテ ージで表すと、 三万三千ドルを 家の価値で割ると、 六・六パーセン トになります。 持ち家にかかる隠れた コストの一部です。 これで全ての数字が 揃いましたので、 これまで話してきたこと、 つまり固定資産税、維持費、 そして資本コストをす べて足し合わせて、 非常にシンプルなルール を導き出しましょう。 はい、 ここに計算式が画面に出 ているといいのですが。 もし出ていなかったら、 編集者のリッキ ーのせいです。 コメントで彼に伝 えてください。 まあ、冗談ですが、 彼はこれを見るで しょうからね。 具体的に見ていくと、 固定資産税の一・一一%に、 維持費としての一%を加え、 さらに資本コストの六・ 六%を足し合わせると、 これらの合計は八・七一% という数字になります。 この八・七一% という割合は、 皆さんの月々の 持ち家コストが 一体どのくらい になるのかを、 素早く簡単に把握す るための非常に便 利な目安として活用 できるでしょう。 ここでのルールは、 家の価格に八・七一パ ーセントを掛けて、 それを十二で割ることです。 これが月々の持ち家の 総コストになります。 お住まいの地域に四十万 ドルの家がある場合、 その四十万ドル という数字に八 ・七一パーセン トを掛けると、 年間の持ち家コスト、 つまり三万四千八百 四十ドルが出ます。 これを十二で割ると、 二千九百三ドルになります。 これが月々の持ち 家コストです。 もし、 お住まいの地域で同等 の四十万ドルの家を月 々二千九百三ドル以下 で借りられるなら、 天秤は大きく賃貸 の方に傾きます。 賢明な財務判断を下すた めの重要な比較点です。 皆さんのために、 Googleスプ レッドシートで 便利な計算ツール を作りました。 無料でダウンロ ードできます。 これを使えば、 色々な数字を試せます。 リンクは下にあります。 検討中の家の価格や、 固定資産税、維持費、 頭金などの変数を 変更できます。 計算ツールの最後には、 月々の損益分岐点となる 支払い額がわかります。 住宅ローンの金利 が変わったり、 違う国に住んで いたりしても、 この計算ツールで、 自分にとって賃貸と購 入のどちらが良いか、 ある程度の目安 がわかります。 このルールは非常 にシンプルで、 多くの人に気に入ら れるでしょうが、 同時に批判する人 も多いでしょう。 月々の損益分岐点を評価す るのには役立ちますが、 欠点もたくさんあります。 それに、 家の購入に関す る計算以外の、 話すべき重要な考慮事項 もいくつかあります。 僕が考える最初の欠点は、 人間はしばしば非合理的で、 衝動的だということです。 7ドルのラテを買うのが得策 でないとわかっていても、 つい贅沢してしまう ことがあります。 自己資金には機会費用 があるからといって、 頭金を払わなかっ た賃貸派の人が、 浮いた10万ドルを市 場に完璧に投資して、 その機会費用を最大限に 活かすとは限りません。 投資には規律と完璧な 実行が必要なんです。 賃貸派が頭金分をルーレ ットの黒に賭けたり、 ブラックジャック で21を狙った りしないと誰が言 えるでしょう？ 21の赤？ 何を言ってるか分 かりませんが、 言いたいことは分 かりますよね。 人間は非合理的で、 常に完璧ではありません。 そういうことです。 住宅ローンの支払いは、 ある意味、 強制的な貯蓄方法だ と考えています。 ローン返済で自己資本を 積み立てるからです。 貯金が苦手な多くの アメリカ人には、 非常に良いでしょう。 自己資本形成の話題ですが、 今日、 利息コストはロー ン金利に借入額 を掛けたものだ と話しました。 実際には、 住宅ローンはそうは なっていません。 ローンはいわゆる 元利均等返済ス ケジュールに基 づいています。 ローン期間の初めは、 元本の返済は少なく、 利息を前倒しで多く支 払っているのです。 30年のローン期間 が進むにつれて、 実際には利息の 支払いが減り、 元本の返済が増 えていきます。 この時点で僕が 気づいたのは、 元利均等返済スケジュ ールの変動により、 自己資本コストと 負債コストは常 に変化していると いうことです。 これに加えて、 15年ローンや様々 な種類の変動金利ロ ーンも存在するとい う事実を考えると、 今日のシンプルなルー ルでは考慮に入れてい ない変数がたくさんあ ることになります。 さらに、 30年後には利息コスト は実質的になくなり、 その時点ではほとんどすべ てが自己資本になります。 それは、 今日話しているルールを大幅 に変えることになります。 これも賃貸より購入が優れて いるもう一つの利点ですが、 30年後には、 時間とともに価値が上 がっている可能性のあ る有形資産を実際に手 にしているのです。 賃貸派は毎月家 賃を払い続け、 そのお金は大家の懐 や銀行口座に入り、 その恩恵を受けること は決してありません。 このルールでよく見ら れる三つ目の欠点は、 常に条件が全く同じだと 仮定していることです。 ある年にはインフレが急騰 するかもしれませんし、 実際そうなっています。 あるいは、 住宅ローンの金利が倍に なるかもしれないし、 自己資本の機会費用が大き く変わるかもしれません。 これは間違いなく考 慮すべき点であり、 僕がいつでも修正 できるように計算 ツールを作った理 由でもあります。 最後に、 住宅ローン金利控除 の税制上の優遇措置 についてはまだ話し ていませんでした。 アメリカにいる場合、 住宅ローン金利を所得 から控除できます。 アメリカの標準控除は、 個人の場合、 年間一万三千ドルから 一万四千ドルです。 控除できる住宅ローン金利 がその額を超えなければ、 あまり意味がありません。 もし控除できる住 宅ローン金利が、 例えば一万四千ド ル以上あるなら、 月々の住宅所有コスト全体 を下げることができます。 所得税率区分やローン額、 金利など、 住宅ローン金利と控除には多 くの変数が絡んでくるので、 これは自分で計算して みる必要があります。 税制上のメリットを最大 限に活用するためには、 専門家のアドバイ スも有効です。 家の所有には、 計算とは全く関係のない利 点もいくつかあります！ 一つ目は、 特に固定金利ローンなら、 支払いが予測可能で固 定的であることです。 支払額がほとんど変わらず、 住宅ローンを正確に予 算に組み込めます。 賃貸では大家がいつでも家 賃を吊り上げられます。 もう一つの利点は、 家を所有すると自由 度が高いことです。 改造や改修を加えても、 大家の許可は不要です。 大家が物件を売っても、 立ち退かされたり追い出され たりする心配がないため、 安心感も増します。 住宅ローンを組むも う一つの良い点は、 インフレが実は有利 に働くことです。 少し直感に反するように聞 こえるかもしれませんが、 基本的には、時間とともに、 価値の面で借金の額が 減るということです。 ここでは、 実際には存在しない、 かなり極端な例を挙げます。 仮に50年固定金利 ローンがあって、 50年前に家を買っ たとしましょう。 突っ込まれる前に言 っておきますが、 50年ローンなんて存在しな いことは知っていますし、 40年ローンもただ の都市伝説です。 でも、 この例え話に付き 合ってください。 50年前に50年固定 金利ローンを組んで、 月々の支払いが300 ドルだったとします。 当時、 月300ドルは今 の月2000ド ルのような価値 がありました。 しかし、固定支払額なので、 ローン期間中ずっと月30 0ドルを払い続けます。 そして今では、 皆さんはどうかわ 300ドルは大した 額には思えません。 これは実際の30年ロー ンでも起こることで、 もっと小規模で、 緩やかですが。 家を所有する最大に して最後の利点は、 住宅価値の大幅な上昇 が見込めることです。 僕の近所で今年初めに売れた 家の例を見てみましょう。 1995年に59万9 000ドルで売れ、 最近の1月には323万 ドルで売却されました。 これは明らかに、 アメリカの住宅価 格上昇率の中央 値を三倍以上上 回っています。 とはいえ、 家の所有はコストや 投資だけでなく、 時に純粋な満足感 をもたらします。 引退後の終の棲家がある という安心感も大きく、 特に今、 余裕があるうちに 手に入れることで 得られる安心感は 計り知れません。 賃貸の利点につ いてもいくつか 話しておきたいと思います。 一つ目は柔軟性です。 30年間も一つの家に縛ら れることはありません。 何らかの理由で、 今住んでいる街を来年、 あるいは来月に出た くなったとしても、 契約を早期に解約すれば、 それが可能です！ 次は、税金や維持費、 その他そういった類の費用を 支払う必要がないことです。 賃貸なら、 ただその場所を借りるだけ。 もしかしたら賃貸用の保険に は入るかもしれませんが、 それだけです！ その点では、 生活はずっとシンプルです。 そして最後に、 賃貸なら素晴ら しい共用施設を 利用できることもあります。 特に大都市の大 きな建物では、 ドアマンがいてセキュリ ティ面で安心ですし、 ジムやプールもあります。 バーベキューをしたり、 イベントを開いたり する場所もあって、 そういったものは家を所有し ていては手に入りません。 今日皆さんにお伝えした い最も重要なことは、 賃貸か購入かを 考える際には、 特にご自身の地域で 検討しているなら、 自分で数字を計算する必要 があるということです。 今日の計算ツールと8. 71%ルールがあれば、 金銭面では、 ご自身で状況を判断する準 備がかなり整うはずです。 現時点のアメリカでは住宅ロ ーン金利が非常に高いため、 特にこの短期的な 期間においては、 賃貸の方が家の 所有よりもはる かに魅力的に見 えるでしょう。 もし五年から八年以上同 じ場所に住む予定で、 実際に家を購入す るのであれば、 そちらの方が投資と して最も大きな上昇 の可能性を秘めてい ると僕は思います。 この動画が役に立っ たなら嬉しいです。 もしそうなら、 ぜひチャンネル登録をして、 次の動画もチェッ クしてください。 ここに、 あるいはここに表 示しておきます。 えー、 賃貸か購入かについて、 皆さんの考えをコメン トで教えてください。 次の動画でお会 いしましょう。 ではまた！
