conversation and start to talk to someone other times groups will be very open and there'll be lots of opportunity for you to enter the group and to enter the conversation as well how can you tell the difference how do you know which is an easy group to approach or a difficult group to approach well you need to look at the body language of the people in the group if they are standing close together if there is not much space at all in between the people in the group then that means they are very closed to anyone else entering the group it's going to be very difficult for you to enter the group you eat before the event that way you won't be tempted by all the delicious food that's offered to you and you can focus on your main purpose of going to the event which is to meet people when you start talking to people at a networking event I recommend you start the conversation by asking non-threatening questions what do I mean by non-threatening questions these are questions that are really easy to answer they're not too prying they're not too interrogative in nature you see when you meet someone at a networking event they will be nervous just like you they're likely to be stressed when you start a conversation with them they'll probably be very wary about who you are or what you want what your intentions are they might be worried that you're going to try to sell them something immediately or try to get them to say yes to some kind of request you need to help them relax you need to bring down their defense mechanism you need to get them comfortable talking to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions have you been to this event before how did you find out about this event and the dreaded question what do you do you I know a lot of you are probably thinking that is so boring it is so typical to be asked that question at a networking event but it is extremely effective in getting the other person to talk to you and getting the other person to feel relaxed around you why because they expect you to ask them this kind of question if you start to ask some questions out of left field questions they don't expect they will start to become cautious they'll put up their defense mechanism they won't be able to relax with you and they probably won't want to continue the conversation with you either how can you meet people who can help you in your career if you're not able to talk to them if you're not able to have an in-depth conversation with them so you need to find ways to bring down their natural defense mechanism get them relaxed to get them to talk to you and you do this by asking non-threatening questions and the handshake you give conveys a lot about who you are as a professional I've had bad handshakes I'm sure you have had bad handshakes at some stage in your career as well they definitely make you notice there are definitely memorable but they don't help you to remember that person in a positive way you don't want people to have this reaction of you you don't want people to think this of you any less of you because you gave a bad handshake I want you to be able to connect with people I want you to be able to get people to like you at networking events this is how you can build a relationship with that person networking events are the first point of contact with someone if they don't like you at their first point of contact then you have no hope of developing a relationship with them so it's really important that you give a positive impression and you give a good handshake or the right handshake to convey the right impression there are many different types of handshakes there is the dead fish handshake where the other person has a slightly clammy hand and it feels a little moist there is the queen handshake where the other person offers you just their fingers instead of their entire hand to shake then there is the limp handshake where they offer you their entire hand but there is no strength to their shake you want to use a strong confident handshake so that is exactly the impression people form about you and here's how you do it you need to make sure you have web to web contact when you shake the other person's hand this is the first step you need to wrap your hand around the other person's hand firmly then you need to shake up and down two to three times then you can let go a handshake is generally the only physical contact you're going to have with someone at a networking event so you really want to make it count we haven't talked at all about body language but body language is extremely important for networking events it's extremely important to help you look more confident and look more poised and professional when you meet people I do have a two-part series on my Channel about body language which I will link to in the description below or you can click on this video right here if you want to watch the first part of this two-part series before you go if you values by working toward a collective goal of giving back philanthropy can create a sense of unity and Harmony that may not always be achieved through wealth management alone so why is philanthropy so important to the long-term success of wealthy families Hughes believes that philanthropy plays a vital role in preventing the fragmentation of families over time in many cases wealth can cause divisions and conflicts among family members especially when it's seen as a personal entitlement rather than a shared responsibility philanthropy changes this Dynamic by refocusing the family on a higher purpos a purpose that transcends individual gain when family members work together to make a positive impact on their communities it creates a shared mission that reinforces family values and strengthens relationships this shared Mission becomes particularly important during generational transitions when wealth and leadership are passed out Hugh suggests that families that prioritize family united Hughes argues that philanthropy instills essential values like gratitude generosity and humility in younger Generations he believes that when children and grandchildren are involved in family foundations donor advised funds or philanthropic boards this hands-on experience teaches the Next Generation not only about strategic giving but also about the values that underpin the family's Legacy Hughes points out that it's not enough to Simply give money families must take the time to educate younger members about the why behind their philanthropic choices why does the family choose to support certain causes what impact do they hope to make how do their charitable actions reflect the family's core values Hughes encourages families to create a philanthropic Mission stenta clear set and Social Circles this reputation can become an asset in itself fing to open doors Foster connections and create a legacy that extends Beyond Financial wealth in in this section Hughes also touches philanthropy families cultivate a sense of gratitude for what they have rather than focusing on what they might lack this shift in mindset can protect against some of the negative effects that wealth can have on individual sh as entitled or isolation moreover Hughes argues that families who practice philanthropy develop a deeper sense of empathy for others which can transform their approach to leadership and wealth management philanthropy helps them understand that their resources can be used for the greater good creating a positive ripple effect that extends well beyond the Family itself goals the key is to make philanthropy an intentional and integrated part of the family's overall wealth management strategy in conclusion Hugh sees philanthropy as a crucial tool for preserving family I would be so happy with that." But actually, the emotion that I'm envisioning is being content with that. It's being in Hawaii and saying, "This is enough. This is all I want. If only we had stayed for a couple days longer. If only we had eaten at the nice nicer restaurant last night. How come those people got a better room? How come they got better beach chairs?" And so, you're not actually content with the experience that you're having. I have no desire to be rich in the sense of a big a giant house, flashy cars. I I I have no desire to live that life. I want to be independent. I want to wake up every morning and say, I can do whatever I want today. And I'm using money as a tool to foster that. That's always been my goal. Now, that's very different from saying, "I want a bigger house," or, "I want a house." You know, it's it's it's a goal of independence. And my grandmother-in-law, who had no money, was completely independent. She was beholden to no one else's influence but her own. And she lived an amazing life because of it. There are billionaires, there are deca billionaires out there who are completely beholden to the opinions and the influence of other people. And so there are a lot of people for whom money is a is a financial asset, but it's a psychological liability. It completely controls their identity and who they are. And there's other people who actually don't have that much money. They have no psychological liability. They're in complete control over their personality and their goals. I've I've often used this. It's a very imperfect idea, but I think about if I was on a deserted island, maybe with just me and my family, nobody else could see how I was living. Nobody else could see my house. Nobody else could see my car. It's completely invisible to everybody else. What kind of lifestyle would I live? If nobody else was watching, what kind of lifestyle would I choose to live? And in that situation, myself and I think most people would instantly shift from uh status to utility. I have no interest in status if nobody can watch it. I have interest in utility. >> And so the kind of house that I would want to live in, the kind of car that I would drive would instantly shift from what are other people impressed by towards what is actually going to foster a good life for myself and my family. And they're very different things. You could be you can make a hund00 million a year and feel like you're falling behind if you're chasing other people. There's a great quote from the comedian Jimmy Carr where he says, "Everybody is jealous of what you've got. Nobody is jealous of how you got it. And that's another one of these points. It's very easy for me or or you to look at people who are more successful than we are and say if we had that level of success, I would be happier. Because when we look at them, I can I can see your house. I can see your car. Can see your clothes. I can see the material aspect of your success. I cannot see the hard work and the stress of how you got it and what it took and what it took to get there. So, this is one of the problems with envy and financial envy that we have for other people is that you don't have a good sense of what their life was and what else is going on in their life outside of of money. And this is why some of the studies that are so hard to wrap your head around, there's so hard to even believe that these are true, the studies that show that people who are wealthy are not necessarily as happy as you would think they would be. >> But here's the thing. When I hear that study, I'm like, who cares? Because I just want to be wealthy. You know what I'm saying? Like, like, let them be unhappy. I'll be the one person who's wealthy who's happy. I promise you, >> Or there's a lot of people who say, "Okay, that might be true, but let me experience it myself." >> They're like, they're like, "Let me get there." Because I think the pull is so profound in our heads that if only I had more money, these problems would go away. It seem It seems like, how could that not be true? And I think the people who have experienced it would say, "No, it can be true. Just much less than you thought." As a financial expert with more than two decades of experience of research and writing and teaching this topic, what's the number one thing that keeps people broke? >> It's this overwhelming sense of keeping up with other people and this overwhelming sense of other people have more than I do and they're doing better than I am and they're happier than I am. And that treadmill has has no end there. There's no end to that treadmill where you're going to say, "If only I had this much money, then I'd be content. If only I had a bigger house, then I'd be content." Once you're on the treadmill, it's it goes on forever. And if you're on it, it's almost impossible to feel like you're doing well with money. How can somebody figure out why they're buying something? Because I think most of us mindlessly spend money because in the moment we want that thing or we're bored or stressed or whatever. I think with every dollar that you spend, it's always one of two buckets. And you should ask yourself which bucket this falls into. You can have very little money and have pure financial independence in your head. Let's touch on investing because you have a really great take on this. You said that success in investing has more to do with long-term thinking and avoiding jealousydriven behavior. >> Yeah. >> Why is that so powerful? >> All compound interest. So compound interest is an incredible thing of just you're growing your money over time and it grows at an increasingly higher level and that's how you become. Can you explain that to the person listening who may not know what that is? Cuz I know a lot of people are going to share this episode to people in their lives who may have never ever listened to anybody when it comes to the psychology of money or how to change your behaviors around money. So what is compounding interest? >> So let me explain it very simply. We have we have $100 and we earn a 10% return on our money this year. So in one year we have $110% return. So you earn $10 in profit this year. I'm also going to earn a 10% return next year. So now I earn 10% on $110. So my profit that year is 11. So my profit in year one is $10. My profit in year two was $11. The profit in year three might be 12 or $13. It's growing every year because you are earning gains on your gains. And if and if you extend that over a lifetime over 30 or 40 or 50 years, the returns get absurd. And so the example that I use in my book is Warren Buffett, the greatest investor of all time. He's worth over hundred billion dollars. Nobody comes close to the level of investing success that he's achieved. 99% of his net worth was accumulated after his 60th birthday. >> Say that again. >> 99% of his net worth was accumulated after his 60th birthday. Now when he was 60, he was a billionaire. absurdly successful by any metric, but he became worth over a hundred billion dollars from age 60 to he's 94 today. And so the reason that's the case is that how compound interest works, it's not necessarily about what your returns are, it's about how long can you keep it going. And I think what is so empowering for ordinary people is that if you can be an average investor for an above average period of time, you can achieve absolutely incredible returns. And so a lot of people when investing they look well I can't compete against Wall Street. I can't compete against all these smart hot shots and the hedge funds. They know more things. They can earn higher returns that I can. And so you become discouraged. The truth is that you don't need to do anything extraordinary with your returns. You need to have extraordinary patience. And so if you can be average for 30 years you will end up in the top 1% of investors. My parents uh really personified this. My parents are smart, educated people, but they have no financial experience, no financial education, no financial background, no connections, no information that anybody listening to this doesn't have. Uh, but they have had extraordinary patience and and self-control and discipline. So, they have been investing consistently every month for 40 years. Never sold anything. Every month they invest a little bit of money, leave it alone for 40 years. If you look at their returns, they would literally be in the top of professional money managers without knowing anything, without having any extra information that you and I or anyone listening can't have because they had the one skill that actually mattered which was just patient patience and discipline. And that's all you need. That's the only thing that you need. And you know there's a there there's some very interesting stories of professional investors who I remember hearing the story about this professional investor who in any given year was never in the top half measured against his peers. In any given year you look at this money manager and you're like yeah did okay. Nothing extraordinary. But over 30 years he was he was the best because that's all you need. It's not about what you earn this year. It's about how long can you keep it going for is all that matters. you know, having been $800,000 in debt and having leans on my house, I and clawing my way out of it. So I invest in what are called index funds which are very boring, very basic. You basically own every stock in the universe. Pick a public company. I own it in this fund. Very low cost. And I invest consistently every month, every quarter. And I've never sold anything in any significant amounts. And I hope to do that forever. I hope this is money that I pass along to my kids or charity or whatever it might be. And and that's it. It's as boring and basic as that. It's it's as simple and boring as basic as you can get. The more complicated your investments are, the lower the odds that you can actually stick with them for 10 or 20 or 30 years. And getting back to compound interest, like all that matters is, can you stick with this? Can you keep this going for years or decades? And I think the simpler it is, the fewer levers you have to pull in your portfolio, the higher the odds that you're going to be able to stick with it for a long period of time. >> You keep saying the word patience. >> And I think it's both patience with the things that you're doing, patience with yourself as your life is not meeting your expectations. Yeah. Patience with your emotions when you feel like maybe if you bought that new thing, those new set of golf clubs, it'd make you feel a little less depressed. Patience. Patience. patience for somebody who's never invested. They don't look at their money. They feel intimidated by this. What would you say to them, Morgan? >> One of the most important things about investing in the stock market is that we know historically you can do very well over time. You can make a lot of money in the stock market over time. Like anything else in life, there is a cost of that. >> There there is a fee for doing that. And when I say fees, I'm not talking about fees to your adviser, fees to your broker. The fee for doing well in investing is putting up with a constant never-ending chain of volatility and uncertainty. That's what you're getting paid for. That's the cost of admission is putting up with the fact that I am very confident that the stock market will do well over the next 20 years. But I have no idea when that return is going to come. We might get all of that return next year and then have 19 years of stagnation. We might get 19 years of stagnation and then it all comes in year 20. We have no idea when it's going to come. And enduring that and putting up with that >> is the cost of admission. There are other investments out there that have no volatility. The savings account in the bank, you have no volatility, very predictable what your returns are going to be. Your returns are going to be low. Those are the returns that you deserve. And so if you're willing to put up with volatility and uncertainty and not knowing, that's that's the cost of admission. And historically, that has been a cost of admission that is worth paying. that if you can put up with that over time, 10, 20 years, you can do extraordinarily well. >> You know, Morgan, you make a huge distinction between being rich and wealthy. Can you walk us through the difference and why it matters? >> I think if you are rich, that by my definition, that means that you have the money in the bank to buy the things that you want. You can make your mortgage payment, you can make your car payment, you can pay for your dinners out, whatever you want to do. That's rich. Wealthy is independent. It's money in the bank that you're not spending. It is money. It's things that you have not purchased. It's savings. It's investments that's sitting there not being spent, but it's giving you independence. It's giving you financial independence and psychological independence where you can live your life in your way without chasing what other people what you think other people want out of you. And there are some pretty astounding examples of the two sides of the spectrum. I use this in the book, The Art of Spending Money. The Vanderbilt family was the richest family that the world had ever seen. Uh back in the 1800s, Cornelius Vanderbilt, if you adjust it for inflation, he had something like half a trillion dollars was his his net worth. And some of the other very wealthy families, the Carnegies, the Rockefellers, did a pretty good job at giving most of their money away to charity. The Vanderbilts basically said, "We're going to give it all to our kids and our grandkids and our great great grandkids." And their job is to live the biggest, most ostentatious life that you can possibly imagine. And the biographies that came from there of those Vanderbilt heirs would show, I think without exception that every single one of them was miserable. every single these are you know if you were a Vanderbilt heir in the early 1900s the day that you were born you got half a billion dollars trust fund and the only thing that was ever expected out of you in life was that you spend as big as you possibly can and every single one of them was miserable there's a book called fortune's children written by one of the heirs name is Arthur Vanderbilt and he talks about the history of his of his ancestors and it is a saddest one of the saddest books I've ever read because these people are people who maybe you and I and people listening would look at and say, "You're so lucky. That's I can't believe how lucky you got. You just got a billion dollars the day you were born." And you read it and you say like, "I I would not I would never I would never want that life because they were so they were very rich. They were the richest people in the world, but they had no independence. >> Everything was dictated about even including who they were allowed to marry was dictated by kind of the social circle of of their family. And to me, the most interesting part of the Vanderbilt family is that by this point today, virtually all the money is is gone. There are a couple Vanderbilt heirs that have some money, but the first Vanderbilt heir who got no inheritance, no trust fund, was Anderson Cooper, the CNN Post, who I'm sure most people are familiar with. His mother, Gloria Vanderbilt, was kind of the last person to get a big trust fund. And Anderson Cooper has talked about this that being the first Vanderbilt heir who didn't get much money was probably a good thing. He was the first person in his family in like 150 years who was told you have to go figure it out for yourself. You have to build your own career, make your own money, figure out your own way. And he's talked about this like he's probably the happiest person in his career. Not that he's had a perfect life, but he's he's probably one of the happiest. And I think that is again like if you look at the richest people because the Vanderbilts were the richest people in the world and they had no wealth by my definition. They had no independence. They had no ability to live their own life. I think that's the difference between rich and wealthy. >> It all comes back to what are you actually using money for? And the clearer you are about the drivers for why you're doing it, it changes what and how you're doing it. >> Yes. And it's so clear in my head like, would you rather earn $70,000 a year and your kids admire you? You've have a great marriage. You have barbecues with your friends and you sit and laugh and you're in good health. And would you rather have that life or make a million dollars a year and you're on your fifth divorce, your kids don't talk to you anymore, you're in terrible health, you're being sued by all of your business partners? It's when you frame it like that, it's the most obvious thing in the world. like would you rather have a good life and less money or more money and and a worse life? If you frame it like that, it's obvious. But still, even if you agree with that framing, it is so normal and natural to say, "Okay, but I'd still rather make a million dollars a year." Like the pull is still always there. >> Well, cuz you think you're going to be the exception. >> Everybody does. >> Everybody does. So, if you're in your 20s and you're just getting started, you haven't been a big saver, you're just got your first job out of college, Morgan, what is your advice in terms of getting good with money? >> A really simple tool that I I came to this by realizing that most people's who are making financial mistakes and have a problem with money, it's not because of the lack of intelligence, it's it's ignorance. And for most people, they actually have no idea how much money they're making or spending. And if you ask them like, "How much money do you make per month?" And how much money do you spend per month overall? Most people in that situation either couldn't tell you or they would give you an answer that's false. And I think the the very basic such boring advice that goes the longest is check your bank account balance every single day. It takes every day. Every day it takes 10 seconds. It's not that hard. And just to give yourself some sense of how much money is coming in and how much is going out, just be cognizant of it. I've never, now people don't do this anymore, but back in in a different generation, balancing your checkbook was always a big thing. Most people don't do it anymore, but I remember for my entire life since I've been a teenager, I've never had any desire to never need to do that because I know my bank account balance down to the dollar every day since I've been 16 or 17. And I think just having that awareness of what's going in and what's going out, it's the most basic advice. But for people who are new and starting out, nothing goes further than that. It's not a lack of intelligence, it's ignorance. You just have to become aware of what you're spending and how much is coming in and how much is going out. That's the very first thing. >> And what's the next thing? >> The other thing is financial independence exists on a spectrum. It's not black and white. So most people would say, we talked about this earlier, why save $20? That doesn't seem like it's going to make any difference whatsoever. So, I'm not even going to save. Every dollar that you save is a piece of your future that you own. Every dollar that you save is a little bit marginally more comfort that you're going to have if you get laid off, if you have a medical emergency, if your car breaks down, every single dollar is getting you to that place. So, if you view independence as black and white, either I'm filthy rich or there's no reason to do it, then I understand why you would say, "Why even save?" When you view it as every dollar that I save is a piece of my future that I control, it's a night of sleep in the future that's going to be a little bit better. I think the desire to save becomes even more. And you don't feel like it's idle money, like you're saving money and it's just sitting there not being used. It can give you pleasure today, right now. And I think some of the best financial decisions I've ever made was what I didn't do. I almost bought a house in my early 20s that I wouldn't have been able to afford. that would have locked me down to a region of the country that I didn't want to stay in. That would have done all kinds of things that in hindsight would have been terrible. And the freedom, the independence that I had by renting earlier in my life of getting up and going, moving to where the jobs were, I look back at that in hindsight as being an absolutely wonderful thing. >> Well, you know, as I listen to you, what I'm realizing it's also a matter of expectations. >> Yeah. If you expected to be able to afford a house in your late 20s or early 30s, then of course you're going to think you failed, >> right? >> But if you shift your expectations and say to yourself, if I'm patient, markets go up and down, >> inflation goes up and down, >> mortgage rates go up and down, if I am patient, I will be able to figure this out. And when the timing is right and when I can afford it, I will find the right house for me. >> Yes. We live a my wife and I live a good life, but not a not a very materially flashy life. So, we don't need that much to kind of check the boxes in our life. And so, I feel like that can be a a superpower for your savings. If if you need $10,000 to be happy, but I only need $1,000 to be happy. Like, I'm getting way more out of that savings than you might. And so, once you realize that that gap between expectations and reality is like the feel the super like supercharging your savings, then it has a totally different feel. And so this is especially true when I was younger where saving $100 or $1,000 that was that made an extraordinary difference in my life. How do you switch the focus though from this obsession of making more to understanding the importance of saving? >> I' I've always viewed it as again back to independence. And if you are earning a high salary, but you need that money >> to to pay your bills this year, you have no independence whatsoever. It's an astounding story. It's a guy named Ronald Reed. Ronald Reed came from the most humble, if not impoverished background that you can imagine. And even as an adult, he spent his entire career as a janitor and a gas station attendant. And when he died, he left millions of dollars to charity. You have to go out of your way to think about it all the time because it's not normal and natural. Nothing in psychology, I think, is more powerful than keeping your expectations in check and being gracious for what you already have. There's a great quote from Stephven Hawking, the great scientist, who of course had ALS and uh was had absolutely no control over his body. He could not even speak. He was in a wheelchair for his almost his entire adult life. And he gave this interview with the New York Times several years before he died. And he said, "Everything else since then has been a bonus." >> And it just made him in that dealing with that tragedy made it much easier for him to exercise gratitude for what he did have. So, that means it's a professional team who will manage who will help you with managing your estate in a very professional manner, bringing in uh governance structures, robust process, and helping you to uh customize the portfolios and the investment uh management process, etc. So, it's an office set up by the family in a very Okay. shorter or a nutshell to say. And uh so, typically family offices are set up by uh the entrepreneurs or business owners who have kind of seen or uh created significant wealth, where they also want to uh maintain a distance between their operating business and their wealth or the personal wealth, which probably will be a family inheritance, right? So, it's a multi-generational wealth, which they would like to distinguish from their operating business and diversify. And again, family offices initially used to be a wealth management kind of an alternative form. But I would say there's a wealth creator space now. Okay. So, the the second gen and the third gen is looking at family office as a platform where they can help creating the wealth in a different form, diversifying or looking at other investment opportunities, which itself can be uh highly, you know, growth-oriented. And also um there is this concept which we all have learned and heard about uh the uh the third generation usually kind of Yes. you know, uh completely uh destroys the wealth. Yeah. So, family office stands very tall and quite uh helping these families to safeguard the wealth across generations. So, that's a very important role. Yeah. And one of the reasons mainly families set up or consider having a family office is this. Okay. That the next generation also Yeah. has this enduring legacy. So, what are some challenges you face in terms of scaling a family office? Okay, that's an interesting question because it's very difficult to scale a family office for two reasons. One is um you need to get the right talent when you say family office you are looking at people who have done that, who have been there for at least 15-20 years, who have got the expertise. Um who can handle a large family's requirements, a UHNI family's requirement. So, we need to get the right talent and who will also stay for long term because one thing which one of our very first clients and investor told us that um clients not investor, clients told us that I don't want to see a different different person. I have to see the same family. That is why I'm signing up with a family office. So, that's very important aspect for us. So, the right talent and a long-term association with these. So, retention of talent hence becomes very important, very difficult also, but that's an important aspect. And second of course is the cost associated with all of this because whatever we are doing, the services, whatever we we offer, all has to be top notch. It has to be high-quality service. So, hence you need good quality people, which comes with a cost. So, if I'm looking at an investment advisor who's got 15 years, 20 years, then obviously the cost is very high because in that front we are competing with the private banks and the you know other firms in the market in the industry. So, it becomes very competitive when it comes to our package pay packages. So, cost is another challenge for getting the right talent and retaining them. Especially in these kind of market scenarios, it's extremely hot at this point of time in the BFSI sector. Okay. Hiring is very difficult for us. Getting the right talent. Even if you hire, whether they will whether they will continue with us for long term is a big question. So, these are two challenges which we see in scaling family offices. Okay. So, in that scenario, single family office again has this challenge in a slightly I would say a next level because in a single family typically you would have four to seven average. If it's a family with 2,000, 3,000 crores of assets, you would have minimum of three to four. Maybe three senior and one could be a junior just for doing the operational day-to-day work. But if it's slightly larger above 5,000 crore, they will definitely have a seven, 10 members because some would be focused on investment, some could be on the other banking transactions, etc. payments. And then some would be on the real estate or whatever the other things which the family wants this office to handle. So, there again the challenge what we see is especially in private investments for example, okay, every private investment is a unique opportunity. Now, you are within the seven, 10 people, you may not have the expertise to analyze a company which is coming from all these sectors. Or the person may not be capable to do the legal diligence also and the technical diligence and take care of the documentation. So, again that is a challenge, right? You have a good lawyer who only takes care of certain aspects of it. But when it comes to private investment, maybe he or she is not equipped. So, again you'll have to outsource. So, that's an additional cost to you. Okay. Whereas in a multi-family office because we're handling different clients, at any point of time we have multiple clients, there are different expertise which is built in. And you have lawyers who understand the various the variety which is available which is possible, right? And that is a cost average which we can do. Yeah. Yeah. That is something which is very important and we felt that after being with the banking system for like 15, 20 years, we said let us do something which is more client aligned and in that process we will have long-term association with the families, multi-generational relationship and that is when we thought of setting up a family office and this is in the year 2013 and that is when SEBI gave came up with this RIA license, registered investment advisors Okay. in India. So, we were one of the first ones to apply for the RIA license and we started as an investment advisory firm with RIA license and then also have another entity created for execution because once you give advice, clients also want us to execute it seamlessly. So, we become the power of attorney holders and execute these transactions for them. Then if you have to create a trust or give them legal services, you need that support. So, there are multiple requirements and all of this are made in-house. - ANYONE can JUGGLE to 100 using this Technique!: Kind: captions Language: en Learn the perfect juggling technique. Quickly get to 100 juggles. Say it. I hear you ask, "Weren't you born with these spectacular skills?" No. When I was born, I couldn't even sit. I only learned that when I was 6 months old, and I learned to juggle when I was 15. It took me a whole 15 years to learn. Actually, I only started practicing when I was 15. Once I started practicing, it didn't take me all that long to get to 100 juggles. Let me show you how you can do it, too. I'll also show you how you can walk and juggle at the same time. There are many styles of juggling. Some people juggle with their laces. Some like to juggle with a back spin. Some like to use just one foot. And I have a friend who juggles like this. So, which one of these is the correct style? They're all correct. There's no wrong way to juggle. But I personally like to juggle like this. A simple alternating foot juggle with no spin. It just looks nicer and I like nice things. Now, if you're starting out for the first time, you're probably doing something like this. It feels silly and you look silly. But don't worry, you're still a spectacular footballer. Let's just fix your form. Keep your feet closer together around the same width as the ball. Bend the knees a little bit. Arms apart, neck down, chest slightly down. Pretend to be a vertical turtle. This pose has a lot more balance than this pose. Let's first master the single juggle. Hold the ball. Drop it from around the height of your thigh. Then we'll tap it up once and catch it. We will drop the ball directly at our center like that. Now, because the ball's at the center, we have to bring our foot towards the center as well when we juggle. And we're going to apply an upward force right at the center of the ball. This way, the ball doesn't spin. If it's slightly off center, it's okay. It'll still be under control. But if we hit it way off center, ball's going to fly away. We're going to swing our lower leg. So, instead of keeping our entire leg stiff, we're going to swing our lower leg forward. I like to touch it at the middle of my toes. I like to squeeze my toes. I keep my toes straight, but my foot is bent at the ankle. The force upwards has to be gentle, but not too gentle where it doesn't even lift. With practice, you'll get an understanding of how much force you actually need to apply. Now, if you manage to drop it, tap it so that it goes straight up and you catch it. Congratulations. You've just done a perfect single juggle. Now, keep repeating this exercise until you're comfortable with your one foot. And once you're comfortable with one foot, practice the same thing with your other foot. Once you're comfortable with both, now we're going to do two juggles. When doing the second juggle, some of you might come across this problem. After the first juggle, you just can't seem to get your other leg up to do the second juggle. This is because you're thinking of the technique and your brain is doing all these calculations. By the time you decide to do the second juggle, it's too late. The ball has already dropped. But don't worry, you're still a charming footballer. You have to train yourself to be able to lift your feet both times. Just force yourself to do that second juggle. It's fine if the technique is not perfect. So, keep practicing till you get those two juggles. You can keep a stick or a line like this in between yourself for reference so that you know to move your foot to the center. And once you get the two juggles, congratulations, you've passed the second stage. By the way guys, I know that you've been requesting the 1v one video. I am still working on it. I want that video to be as concise as possible. So, I'm putting a lot of effort behind it. If you are impatient, you can check out my full dribbling course at www.bballerskills.com where we show you many types of dribbling and we cover 1v1 there as well. Notice that when I juggle, the ball is very low down and my touches are very soft. But when you first start, your touch won't be that soft. You find that the ball is going higher and that's totally okay. With regular consistent practice, your touch will start getting a lot more refined. The secret to getting to 10, 50, 100 juggles is in the effort of trying to break your record every single day. That's how I did it. The technique that I just showed you should be a soft suggestion at the back of your mind. Your focus should be on trying to break your record every single day. Now, if you're serious about getting to 100 juggles, please, can you do this? Comment your current juggling record right now. And every time you break your record, please reply to your own comment to update your record. Let's see how many juggles you can get to in one month. Now, in your pursuit to break your record every single day, you will find that even though you know the technique perfectly, the ball is still going all over the place and you're running around like a squirrel. But don't worry, you beautiful footballer. This happens because currently your muscles are just not strong enough. But with consistent daily practice and when you rest and recover, the next day your touch is a lot smoother than the day before. If you want to learn juggling very quickly, let's say 1 month's time, I suggest that you train between 30 minutes and 1 hour every single day. And in that period, you have to try and break your record. So if your record is two today, tomorrow you have to try and get three in that one hour. If you don't, it's okay. Try again the following day. And if you keep repeating this process, you'll keep breaking your record and your muscles will start getting stronger. If the ball keeps spinning backwards and you don't want it to spin backwards, this happens because you are applying the force at an angle. If I hit the ball straight up and at the center of the ball, there won't be any spin. But if I am slightly angled, then I will slice the ball and the ball goes backwards. Let me show you how you walk and juggle at the same time. It's actually almost the same technique. Instead of moving our foot upwards, we'll just move our foot slightly forward like that. And as I keep walking, I like to keep my balance on my heel. When I walk and juggle, I don't walk like a normal person. I walk on my heels. It helps me with the balance. As always, my friend, I really appreciate you supporting me in my journey towards becoming a full-time football content creator. So, if you enjoyed this video or found it helpful, please let me know. Let's go out there and break our juggling records. The bite.

- Why Manhattan Condos Are Selling At A Loss: Kind: captions Language: en Over the past year, 1 in 3 condo owners in Manhattan who sold sold at a loss. Now, with the election of Mamdani, there are reports that the wealthy could flee even more, going to the suburbs or places like Florida or Texas. But the real reason why Manhattan real estate has been flat over the past decade, and what the wealthy are really doing is a bit more nuanced. I think prices got too high and they've had to come down. So of course people lost money. And you're reading about $100 million transactions, but those are largely detached from everyday New Yorkers. Condominiums have often been looked at as a way for overseas investors to park money. The individual ROI you get on an investment unit is is trivial. To be perfectly honest. If we pan out and look at the Manhattan market over ten, 20 years, what's happened with prices over that time? Did did people make money, especially when you compare it to some of the hotter markets. Yeah, I think Manhattan has pretty much been flat. I don't think that people have cashed in, sold their apartments and made money. That just hasn't happened. Investors track prices per square foot to compare properties. In 2015, one square foot of space cost over $1,500 in Manhattan. By 2025, prices had fallen to around 1100 per square foot, according to Redfin data. That's still quite high compared to the rest of the country. This is like a townhouse in the sky, and it's got a beautiful outdoor space. And the maintenance here, the monthly costs are $10,000 a month. Asking price is 9.325 million. Long term. Yeah, I mean, you'll probably make a little bit of money, possibly depending on what you buy, but I don't think people buy to do that. I mean, they want to buy something that maintains its value, and hopefully when they sell it, they can make a little bit of money. But I don't think it's for that. It's not for flipping purposes. Today, 72% of condos sold in Manhattan are selling under list price. There's only so much of a demand pool that exists out there for these properties. You take places like 57th Street, 57th Street became Billionaires Row. Now, if you go look at these tall skyscrapers, where's your demographic? They're billionaires all over the world. You've had a significant decline over the years in foreign capital coming in. It's still coming in, but not in droves that it was, you know, back in the day. You know, most people care about mortgage rates. They have to get a mortgage to buy their home. We're fortunate in that a lot of people are able to buy their homes with cash. And that's why the market didn't decline as much as the rest of the country. Since the Fed pivot in 2022, pushing rates higher, what we are seeing is a tremendous amount of affluent buyers coming into New York City, and the affluent aren't relying on interest rates. They're relying on cash. Most Manhattan condos are purchased all cash. It shows you that we are in such a discretionary market. This is not America. You know the rest. They say if you leave New York City, you have to live in America. And I think that's true because this is a different environment. Buyers who need a mortgage to purchase in Manhattan are looking at rates over 6%. This leaves many of them waiting on the sidelines. I would equate the last three years of low activity solely due to interest rates. Most consumers who rely upon obtaining financing require a home were priced out of the market because the price appreciation, along with a record level debt service, just made it cost prohibitive for them. And that's actually why rents went up so much because people couldn't afford to buy. The losses in Manhattan condos may be one reason that even the wealthy are choosing to rent. First time home buyers. Today, the average age is 40 years old. That's you know, it's heinous that that's what we've come to the buying side. I'm seeing more of that. People in their early 30s, and they're getting a little bit of help from their parents as well. Condo buyers pay transaction costs, maintenance fees and property taxes, all of which eat into returns. So New York City, our closing costs are tremendously high. And part of that is to disincentivize flipping of properties. You have brokerage commission between 5 to 6%, and you pay New York City transfer tax of 1.425%. You pay New York State transfer tax, and a lot of buildings have exit fees as well. You know, you're talking north of 10%, and that's before you get to paying any gains taxes. So you have to make a decent return in order to even just cover the exit fees. You know, from a return on investment. I think there's probably more upside over the next ten years than the last ten years. With elevated interest rates, we're going to see a lower level of production of condominiums, especially luxury condominiums, which is going to make them more expensive. New York is more dependent than ever on the very wealthy. The top 1% of earners pay more than 40% of all income taxes. The average sale price for Manhattan real estate is nearly $2 million. Most condos sold sell for over $1.5 million. Tell us about who those luxury buyers are. A lot of them are in New York. You know, there are New Yorkers who are going, you know, going from uptown to downtown, empty nesters, some of that younger families moving from places, you know, from California to New York, a little bit of that, not so much international. The luxury market has done so well this year, and I think people made a lot of money. Wall Street calls it the K-shaped economy. Lower income Americans are cutting back while the wealthy are doing most of the spending. I'm shocked by the level of luxury buying and commitment to the city. We were hearing about this seismic shift post Mamdani, and that didn't happen. In fact, we're seeing the opposite. New York City Mayor elect Mamdani promises to tax the rich and freeze the rent. You're looking at 3 to 4 years of zero rent increases for rent stabilized tenants. That's about a million rental units. There's about 1,200,000 rental units that are open market. And so what that's going to cause landlords to do is push expenses on the buildings they control as an entity push rents higher for open market tenants. 19% of New Yorkers plan to leave the city over the next five years. The top factors housing affordability, crime and safety, living, space, taxes, amenities and job opportunities. That said, 55% of New Yorkers poll before the election plan to stay. So I think people buy things depending on their situation. Am I moving here? Am I having a baby? Is somebody pass away and then they decide what they're going to do. And that's really real estate in a nutshell. People don't go home. It's a good time. Let me invest my money. Typically they don't do that. Even in Manhattan. They buy because they have a need. So can you afford it? Does it make sense? Is it the home of your dreams? Does it meet your needs? Is it close to your kids schools? Can you get to the subway? Those are the questions you ask yourself. And is it what you want? If not, maybe rent for a little while and decide a little bit later.

- New York Homeowners PANIC as Property Taxes Rise Overnight 2026: Kind: captions Language: en Your home is your sanctuary, but in 2026, it has become a massive liability you can no longer afford. While you slept, New York lawmakers shifted the financial burden of the entire state onto your doorstep. The tax bill arriving in your mailbox this morning is not a mistake. It is a calculated strike against your savings. If you think your fixed rate mortgage protects you, you are dangerously wrong. Escalating assessments and hidden levies are draining bank accounts across the Empire State. You must understand these new rules immediately. One missed detail could trigger a foreclosure process that moves faster than ever. The new reality of New York property taxes. The year 2026 marks a turning point for every homeowner from Buffalo to Monttoque. For decades, property taxes in New York were seen as a predictable if painful cost of living. But the system has undergone a fundamental shift. The New York State Department of Taxation and Finance along with the Office of Real Property Tax Services has implemented a series of aggressive collection models designed to fill massive budget gaps. In New York City alone, the expense budget for 2026 has climbed to an staggering $115.9 billion. After accounting for all state and federal aid, the city faced a gaping $ 35.2 billion hole. To fill it, the state has turned to the only reliable source of revenue left, your home equity. This is no longer just about the tax cap. While you may have heard that property taxes are capped at 2%, that is a dangerous halftruth. In 2026, local governments and school districts are finding sophisticated ways to bypass these limits, often leaving you with a double-digit increase that seems to happen overnight. The state's constitutional property tax cap restricts levies to 2.5% of the average full market value, but the way market value is calculated has been weaponized. As property values were inflated by the market trends of the last few years, the state is now catching up on its assessments, leading to what many are calling the assessment explosion. Who made these decisions? The current crisis is a collision of state level mandates and federal tax policy. The primary driver on the federal side dates back to the Trump administration's tax cuts and jobs act of 2017. This legislation placed a $10,000 cap on the state and local tax salt deduction. For years, New Yorkers were hit twice. They paid high local taxes and were then unable to deduct those payments from their federal returns. In 2026, while new federal reconciliation bills have adjusted these caps to $40,400 for some, the phase down for higher earners and the complexity of the new brackets have created a tax trap for the middle class. On the state level, Governor Hatchel's 2026 executive budget and the resulting legislative sessions have shifted more responsibility to local municipalities. By mandating new environmental standards and social programs without providing full funding, Albany has forced towns and counties to raise property taxes to stay afloat. The decision makers at the New York City Council and the various county boards of supervisors across the state have been forced to choose between cutting essential services or overriding the tax cap. In 2026, almost all of them are choosing the latter. The impact on seniors and retirees. No group is more vulnerable in 2026 than New York seniors. If you are living on a fixed income, your home is likely your greatest asset, but it is also the one being targeted. The school tax relief or STAR program has seen significant changes this year. While the state has introduced an automatic upgrade to the enhanced STAR exemption for those turning 65, the income limits have not kept pace with inflation. For 2026, your 2024 adjusted gross income must be $110,750 or less. If you made even $1 more, perhaps by selling a small amount of stock to cover a medical bill, you lose the enhanced benefit entirely. This all or nothing approach means a senior could see their school tax bill jump by three or $4,000 in a single cycle. For someone relying on social security, this is a financial death sentence. We are seeing a record number of retirees being forced to short sell their homes or take out high interest reverse mortgages just to pay the tax collector. The dream of aging in place is being replaced by the reality of being taxed out of your own neighborhood. Number 10, automatic interest on late payments. Under New York Real Property Tax Law, RPTL section 924A, the interest rate for late property tax payments is set at 1% per month. This sounds manageable until you do the math. If you miss your January payment by December, you owe an additional 12% in pure interest. In 2026, many homeowners are finding that their autopay systems through their mortgage escrow failed to update after a reassessment. Most people ignore this because they think the bank is handling it. However, if your escrow account has a shortage, the bank might not pay the full amount on time. The state does not care whose fault it is. They apply the interest immediately. This leads to a cascading debt. If you owe $10,000 in taxes, a one-year delay adds $1,200 in interest alone. This debt is often reported to credit bureaus, dropping your score and making it impossible to refinance your home to pay off the tax bill. It is a silent trap that catches thousands of New Yorkers every year. Number nine, the star credit maximum ceiling. The school tax relief star program is the primary way New Yorkers save on their school taxes. However, in 2026, the state has enforced a strict maximum savings ceiling. Under current regulations, the star savings amount cannot exceed the savings of the prior year by more than 2%. Meanwhile, school districts across the state are raising their actual tax levies by 5, 7, or even 10%. Why is this a risk? Because most homeowners believe STAR will always cover a significant portion of their bill. In reality, as school budgets grow, the percentage of your bill covered by STAR is shrinking. You are paying the difference. This leads to an effective tax hike that is much higher than the headline numbers suggest. If your school tax is $6,000 and the district raises it by 10%, you owe an extra $600. But if your star credit is capped, you are on the hook for nearly the entire increase. Over 5 years, this gap can cost a family an additional $5,000 that they never budgeted for. Number eight, unpaid water leans hit taxes. In 2026, a new enforcement trend has taken hold in New York. The absorption of municipal utility debt into property tax leans. If you fall behind on your water or sewer bills, many New York municipalities are now legally authorized to levy those unpaid amounts onto your property tax bill. This is governed by local town and village codes under the authority of the general municipal law. Most people ignore water bills thinking they can just pay them eventually. But once that debt is moved to the tax role, it becomes a priority lean. This means it must be paid before any other debt and it carries the same heavy interest and penalties as unpaid property taxes. If you don't pay that water tax, the city can move to foreclose on your home. This has become a major issue for landlords whose tenants didn't pay the utilities. The landlord ends up losing the building because of the tenants unpaid $500 water bill. It is a legal record that stays with the property and can prevent any future sale or transfer. Number seven, the residential redistribution trap. New York City's property tax system is divided into four classes. Class one includes one to three family homes. In 2026, the city had to balance a massive budget gap. Initially, class one homes saw a market value increase that would have triggered a massive tax spike. To prevent a total collapse, the state caps class shifts at 5% per year. However, the 20.63% interim tax rate set for class 1 in 2026 is the highest it has been in decades. Why is this happening? Because commercial properties class 4 are struggling with high vacancy rates in the work from home era. Since the city cannot squeeze more money out of empty office buildings, they are forced to shift the burden to residential homeowners. Even with the 5% cap, the cumulative effect of these annual shifts is devastating. You are essentially paying a subsidy to keep commercial real estate afloat. This leads to a long-term decline in your financial stability as more of your home's potential profit is eaten away by the city's need for cash. Number six, local law 97, carbon fines. If you live in a condo or a co-op in New York City, you're now facing local law 97. This climate legislation set strict carbon emission limits for buildings. In 2026, the first major wave of compliance reports is due. Buildings that exceed their carbon caps face a civil penalty of $268 per metric ton of excess carbon. For the average building, this can translate to a fine of 50 cents per square foot every single month. Most homeowners think this is a building problem, not a homeowner problem, but co-ops and condos pay these fines through special assessments. You will see a new line item on your monthly maintenance bill that could be hundreds of dollars. These fines are not taxdeductible and do not add any value to your home. If your building board fails to file the report by the May 1st deadline, the fines start immediately. This can lead to a legal record of non-compliance for your building, making it nearly impossible for new buyers to get a mortgage to buy into your complex. Number five, the 2% cap override. New York's general municipal law, section 3C, created the 2% tax cap, but the law contains a massive loophole. A municipality can override the cap if 60% of the governing board votes to do so. In 2026, we are seeing a KN gene of overrides across the state. From Livingston County to Nassau, local boards are passing local laws that explicitly authorize them to exceed the 2% limit. Most people ignore these board meetings thinking the state will protect them. The reality is that the state provides the mechanism for the override. Once the board votes, there is no appeal. You might find your property tax increasing by 5%, 8%, or even 12% in a single year. This leads to a rapid erosion of your home equity. In just 3 years of overrides, a homeowner can see their annual bill increase by several thousand. This is government enforcement at its most direct, and it is happening in 2026 because local officials feel they have no other choice. Number four, state- funded cyclical reassessments. New York has introduced an assistance program under Title 9YCR subpart 2013 to encourage local governments to reassess properties on a cyclical basis. This sounds like a good thing, keeping values accurate, but in 2026, many towns haven't reassessed in 20 years. When they finally do, the catch-up hike is massive. If your home was valued at $200,000 in 2005, but is worth $600,000 in 2026, your assessed value, AV, is about to triple. Even if the tax rate stays the same, your total bill will skyrocket. Most people misunderstand this, thinking their taxes only go up if the rate goes up. The AV is the real engine of the tax bill. The state is paying your town to do this because they want more revenue. The long-term impact is a sudden permanent increase in your cost of living that can wipe out your retirement savings in a few short years. Number three, the federal salt deduction trap. The state and local tax salt deduction was a cornerstone of middle class home ownership in New York for generations. After the Trump era cap of $10,000, New York homeowners were hit with a massive increase in their effective tax rate. In 2026, we are in the middle of a complex federal transition. While some caps have been raised to $40,400, these new benefits are being phased out for anyone with a modified adjusted gross income over $500,000. While that sounds like a rich person's problem, in many parts of New York, a dual-income family, like a teacher and a nurse, can quickly approach these thresholds. Furthermore, the 1% annual indexation of the cap is not keeping up with the 8% tax hikes at the local level. You are being bracket crept into a position where more of your income is being taxed by both the state and the federal government. This leads to a loss of financial liquidity making it harder to maintain your property or save for your children's education. Number two, the new foreclosure collection model. For years, New York municipalities would sell tax leans to private investors who would then try to collect the debt. In 2026, a major shift has occurred with legislation like Bill A09116. The state is moving toward a model that prohibits the sale of tax leans to private entities, but in exchange, it has created a more aggressive state-run collection program. The new law establishes a homeowner protection fund, but it also mandates that every municipality adopt a strict model collection program. This program prioritizes fast foreclosure to clear the titles of delinquent properties. If you fall behind in 2026, you won't be dealing with a private company that might offer a payment plan. you will be dealing with a state agency that is legally required to move toward foreclosure. This has accelerated the timeline from years to mere months. If you ignore a court notice today, your home could be owned by the county by the end of the year. This is a total loss of your most valuable asset and it can happen because of a single year of unpaid taxes. Number one, the taxable AV redistribution trap. The most financially damaging risk in 2026 is the taxable assessed value AV redistribution. This is the hidden formula that determines how much of the $ 37.98 billion levy you personally owe. In 2026, the state has allowed for the redistribution of the tax burden from classes 3 and four utilities and commercial onto class one homeowners. Why is this number one? Because it is a structural change that you cannot appeal. You can challenge your individual assessment, but you cannot challenge the class share. If the city decides that homeowners must pay a larger share of the total budget because commercial offices are empty, your taxes will go up even if your home's value stays the same. This is the ultimate drain on your savings. It is a mandatory transfer of wealth from families to the government to cover for the failures of the commercial real estate market. In 2026, this redistribution is expected to cost the average New York homeowner an additional $1,500 to $4,000 per year, every year indefinitely. This is not a fine you pay once. It is a permanent increase in the rent you pay to the state for the privilege of owning your home. It destroys your credit score if you can't pay. It ruins your financial stability and it makes New York home ownership a high-risisk gamble. What is happening right now in 2026? As we stand in the first quarter of 2026, the pressure on New York homeowners has reached a breaking point. We are seeing a mass exodus of middle-class families and retirees moving to states with lower property tax burdens. The tax department is currently reviewing comments on dozens of new rules that will further automate the collection of petroleum taxes, property percentages, and inventory records. The goal is clear. The state is building a high-tech surveillance and collection system that leaves no stone unturned. The no tax on tips and other middle class tax cut proposals at the state level are often revenue neutral, meaning if they cut one tax, they must raise another. Frequently, that other tax is the property tax as it is the hardest to avoid. You can stop working for tips, but you cannot easily move your house. Right now, the New York City Council is considering new legislation to lower the class shift cap even further. But this is a double-edged sword that could lead to higher rates in the long run. The panic you see in the headlines is not an exaggeration. New York in 2026 is a state where your home is under constant financial siege. The lesson for every homeowner is simple. You can no longer afford to be passive. You must check your property assessment every year during grievance day. You must understand your star status and ensure your income remains within the thresholds. Most importantly, you must have an emergency fund specifically for tax increases. The era of 2% predictability is over. We are in the era of the $10,000 mistake. A single missed payment, a single missed assessment challenge, or a single year of ignored board meetings can cost your home and your future. The system is designed to favor the state, not the citizen. You must be the one to protect your sanctuary.
