- This Is The RICHEST Family That OWNS NEW YORK...: Kind: captions Language: en When you think of New York wealth, you likely picture Wall Street bankers in tailored suits trading stocks. But true New York money doesn't just trade the market. It owns the market. It owns the skyline, the media, and the political machine. We are talking about the dynasties that control Hudson Yards, the global beauty industry, and the information flowing to your screens every second. Today, we count down the 12 richest families in New York. From old money publishing barons to the undisputed king of financial data. This is how absolute power operates in the Empire State. Number 12, the Durst family, the kings of concrete. To understand the architecture of New York real estate, you have to look at the foundations laid by Joseph Durst. He arrived in America in 1902 with exactly $3 sewn into his coat. He started as a tailor, but he realized quickly that in a city growing vertically, the people who make the clothes don't get rich. The people who own the buildings do. He purchased his first building in 1915, establishing the Durst Organization. Over the next century, his descendants, notably Seymour and Douglas Durst, transformed that initial investment into a gargantuan portfolio of prime Midtown Manhattan real estate. They did not just buy land, they hoarded it, operating with an incredibly long-term horizon that publicly traded real estate investment trusts simply cannot afford. The Durst family protects its immense wealth through extreme financial conservatism. The real estate industry is notoriously cyclical, filled with highly leveraged developers who go bankrupt the moment interest rates rise. The Dursts famously use very little debt. When recessions hit and other developers are forced to liquidate, the Durst family swoops in, flushed with cash, and buys up distressed assets for pennies on the dollar. Their absolute pinnacle of power was executed at ground zero. Following the tragedy of 9/11, the reconstruction of the World Trade Center site was mired in political gridlock. The Durst organization stepped in, purchasing a massive equity stake in One World Trade Center and taking over the management of the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere. While the public knows the name due to true crime documentaries, they misunderstand the business. The family exised Robert Durst decades ago to protect the institutional integrity of the empire. Worth an estimated 11.2 $2 billion. The Dursts dictate the physical skyline, operating as the quiet landlords of the global elite. Number 11, the Hurst family, the information overlords. William Randolph. Hurst inherited a massive fortune from his father's silver mining operations in the late 1800s. But he knew that raw commodities were vulnerable to market shifts. He pivoted into media, building the largest newspaper chain in the United States and effectively inventing the sensationalist yellow journalism that once shaped American foreign policy. Today, the Hurst Corporation is a sprawling diversified conglomerate overseen by his descendants, including William Randph Hurst III. The true genius of the Hurst wealth is the legal fortress built to protect it, the Hurst family trust. This ironclad structure completely prevents any single heir from selling off pieces of the empire to fund a lavish lifestyle. The trust ensures that the core assets which now include massive stakes in ESPN, hundreds of magazines, and lucrative television stations remain intact, compounding value across generations. While legacy print media struggled in the digital age, the Hurst family executed a brilliant invisible pivot. They didn't panic. They moved into B2B financial and medical data. Today, they own Fitch Group, one of the top three credit rating agencies in the world. They essentially rate the debt of sovereign nations and mega corporations. When people hear the name Hurst, they think of opulent castles or glossy fashion magazines like Harper's Bazaar. But the underlying math of their $21 billion fortune tells a different story. The magazines are just the glamorous storefront. The true untouchable wealth is generated by the highly specialized, incredibly boring subscription data services that banks and hospitals absolutely must buy to function. They traded the printing press for the server farm. Number 10, the Ze family, the financial ghosts. William Ze Jr. inherited a modest publishing company and transformed it into Ze Davis, the undisputed king of niche enthusiast magazines. If you read PC magazine or Car and Driver in the 1980s, you were putting money directly into the Ze family's pockets. William understood that advertisers would pay a massive premium to reach highly targeted, passionate audiences rather than the general public. The Ze family then executed one of the most perfectly timed exits in history. In 1994, right before the internet annihilated the print industry, Ze Jr. sold his empire for $1.4 $4 billion. His three sons, Daniel, Robert, and Durk Ze, took that immense liquid capital and protected it by entirely removing themselves from public operations. They formed Ze brothers Investments, pioneering the modern, ultra- sophisticated family office. The Ze brothers didn't just passively invest in index funds. They became the ultimate apex predators of Wall Street. They operated as seed capitalists, providing the initial billions to launch the careers of legendary hedge fund managers like Bill Aman. They outsource the stress of trading to the hungriest managers while keeping the lion's share of the profits. With a combined net worth currently estimated at over $21 billion, the Ze brothers are essentially financial ghosts. They rarely give interviews and do not operate a consumer-facing company. What the public misunderstands is that the smartest wealth in New York often decides to completely disappear. They morphed from a publishing house into an invisible capital machine. Number nine, the Lauren family. The architects of aristocracy. Ralph Lifchitz, a kid from the Bronx, started his career working out of a single drawer in the Empire State Building, making wide neck ties out of leftover rags. He legally changed his name to Ralph Lauren and founded a brand that didn't just sell clothing. It sold the mythology of the American aristocracy by blending the aesthetic of old money New England prep schools with the rugged romance of the American West. He built a global fashion empire. The Ralph Lauren Corporation is a publicly traded behemoth. But Ralph Lauren protected his family's absolute power by instituting a dualclass share structure. While Wall Street mutual funds and retail investors can buy class A shares, the Lauren family fiercely controls the class B shares, giving them the overwhelming majority of voting rights. No activist investor or hostile corporate raider can ever force Ralph Lauren out of his own company. Ralph Lauren's ultimate power move was entirely psychological. He democratized the aesthetic of the elite. He understood that people don't buy a polo shirt because they need cotton. They buy it because they want to signal status. He licensed his brand across homegoods, fragrances, and restaurants, creating a closed loop lifestyle ecosystem where consumers can sleep, eat, and dress inside the Ralph Lauren illusion. With a family net worth of over 12.7 billion, he built his empire by selling permanence. He engineered a timeless classic aesthetic that is utterly immune to the volatile trend cycles of high fashion. He isn't selling garments. He is selling access to an exclusive country club that anyone can join for the price of a sweater. Number eight, the Ross family, the Metropolis makers. Steven Ross began his career as a tax attorney on Wall Street, organizing syndications for affordable housing. He quickly realized that the real money in New York wasn't in practicing law. It was in utilizing complex government tax incentives to develop physical properties. He founded related companies in 1972, initially focusing on government subsidized housing before aggressively expanding into ultra luxury development. Ross protected his wealth by turning related companies into a fully vertically integrated fortress. They don't just build skyscrapers and walk away. They manage the properties, finance the debt, and own the operating businesses within them. By controlling the entire life cycle of a building, related companies is insulated from the devastating boom and bust cycles that regularly wipe out traditional developers. Steven Ross executed the most ambitious, high-risk real estate play in modern American history, Hudson Yards. He convinced the city to let him build a $25 billion, 28 acre luxury metropolis suspended entirely over a working commuter railard on the west side of Manhattan. He literally manufactured a brand new ultra exclusive neighborhood out of thin air, shifting the geographic center of gravity for New York's corporate elite. Commanding an estimated net worth of 11.6 billion, Ross is far more than a simple landlord. He owns the Miami Dolphins and is a massive investor in luxury lifestyle brands like Equinox and SoulCycle. Hudson Yards is not just real estate. It is a physical consumer funnel. Ross builds the luxury condo, owns the gym at the base of the tower, and curates the restaurants, capturing every single dollar his wealthy tenants spend. Number seven, the Tish family, the civic syndicate. The Tish dynasty began when brothers Lawrence and Preston Tish bought a small winter resort in New Jersey in 1946. They used the cash flow from that single property to embark on a legendary decadesl long acquisition spree. They bought hotels, gained control of Lowe's theaters, and eventually consolidated their holdings into Lowe's Corporation, a massive holding company that operates like a miniaturized version of Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway. The Tish family protects its wealth through extreme diversification. Lowe's Corporation controls CNA Financial, Boardwalk Pipelines, and Lowe's Hotels. This is a classic hedging strategy. if the hospitality sector crashes, the energy and insurance dividends keep the family office flooded with liquid cash. While their corporate acquisitions were brilliant, their cultural power move occurred in 1991 when Preston Tish bought a 50% ownership stake in the New York Giants. The NFL is the ultimate American cartel. by owning half of the city's premier football franchise. The Tish family cemented their legacy in the emotional tribal heart of New York's civic identity. If you walk through New York City, you will see the Tish name plastered across the NYU School of the Arts and massive hospital pavilions. They are recognized as elite philanthropists. But what people misunderstand is the brutal, highly efficient corporate machinery required to fund that philanthropy. Their billions aren't made in glamorous tech startups. They are extracted from the highly regulated sectors of commercial insurance and natural gas transport. Number six, the New House family, the private pundits. Samuel Irving. New House started his career as a teenage clerk, eventually taking over a failing newspaper in New Jersey. From that single asset, he built advanced publications. His sons, Sai and Donald New House, inherited the empire and expanded it, [music] turning Conde Nast into the absolute global authority on high culture with titles like Vogue, Vanity Fair, and The New Yorker. The New House family operates with a terrifying level of absolute privacy. They are one of the largest privately held media companies on Earth. Because they do not answer to public shareholders, they can sustain massive short-term losses to maintain the cultural dominance of their magazines. They control the narrative without ever becoming part of it. While the media praised their print magazines, the family quietly executed a brilliant digital move. In 2006, they acquired a massive early stake in Reddit, [music] and they hold a multi-billion dollar stake in Charter Communications, one of America's largest broadband providers. They don't just own the content, they own the pipes that deliver it. When people think of the New House family, they picture the Met Gala and high fashion. But Donald New House, holding an estimated net worth of 15.8 billion, relies on a different engine. The true financial heavy lifting of advanced publications isn't done by Vogue. It is done by their massive network of boring, hyper local regional newspapers and lucrative investments in broadband internet. Number five, the Lauder family. The vanity monopoly. Estee Lauder began her empire in the 1940s, cooking up skin creams in her uncle's kitchen and relentlessly pitching them to high-end beauty salons. She pioneered the gift with purchase marketing strategy, fundamentally altering the beauty industry. Her sons, Leonard and Ronald, took that hustle and transformed the Estee Lauder companies into a global monopoly of vanity. The company is publicly traded, but the Lauder family utilizes a dualclass share structure to maintain absolute control over the voting rights. This structure allows them to execute a relentless acquisition strategy, purchasing independent brands like MAC Cosmetics, Clinique, and Tom Ford Beauty without seeking permission from Wall Street. The Lauder family's ultimate power move was their complete subjugation of the New York art world. Leonard Lauder promised his $1 billion collection of cubist masterpieces to the Metropolitan Museum of Art. This wasn't just a tax write-off. It was a permanent installation of the family name into the highest echelon of American cultural aristocracy. The general public assumes that selling makeup is a frivolous industry. In reality, the Lauder family benefits from the lipstick effect. During recessions, when consumers cannot afford new cars, they still purchase high-end cosmetics as a cheap psychological boost. Worth a combined $20 billion, the Lauers don't just sell face cream. They sell heavily fortified recessionp proof escapism. Number four, the Murdoch family, the global kingmakers. Rert Murdoch inherited a modest Australian newspaper, but his ambitions were global. He systematically conquered the British tabloid market before invading New York. He purchased the New York Post, expanded into television with Fox, and acquired the crown jewel of global finance, the Wall Street Journal. The Murdoch empire is shielded by the infamous Murdoch family trust. This complex legal structure was designed to maintain absolute familial control over News Corp and Fox Corporation. It dictates exactly how the voting shares are distributed among Rert's children, setting the stage for the most publicized corporate succession battle in history. In 2019, Rupert Murdoch executed the trade of the century. He sold 21st Century Fox to Disney for $71 billion. He cashed out of the entertainment business at the absolute peak of its valuation, retaining only the highly lucrative, politically untouchable assets of Fox News and live sports. The public views the Murdoch simply as media executives. But that is a massive underestimation. Operating from their pen houses in Manhattan, the Murdoch, worth over $ 24.2 billion, are political kingmakers. They do not just report on elections. Their networks actively dictate the conservative political narrative of the entire English-speaking world. Number three, the Simons family, the quantum codereers. The late Jim Simons was not a traditional Wall Street trader. He was a brilliant MIT mathematician and a cold war codereaker for the NSA. In the late 1970s, he looked at the financial markets and realized they were not driven by fundamental business value, but by massive chaotic data sets full of hidden patterns. He founded Renaissance Technologies, employing physicists and astronomers instead of finance majors. Renaissance Technologies protects its wealth through the legendary Medallion Fund. It is widely considered the most successful hedge fund in human history. To protect its algorithm from becoming diluted, Simons ruthlessly closed the fund to all outside investors. In 1993, only employees are allowed to invest, creating a perfectly sealed financial ecosystem. The Medallion Fund generated annualized returns of roughly 66% before fees over three decades. By charging exorbitant fees to their own employees and kicking out all external capital, the firm built a legal money printing machine. They completely decoupled their wealth from the traditional Wall Street hustle. Jim Simons proved that traditional investment analysis is irrelevant noise. Managing a family fortune that exceeds $ 32 billion, the Simons family demonstrated that ultimate financial power isn't held by the loudest trader. It is held by the quietest mathematician utilizing supercomputers to harvest [music] billions from human emotional panic. Number two, the Ko family, the private monarchy. Fred Ko invented an efficient method for refining crude oil into gasoline. His sons Charles and David Ko built Ko Industries into a sprawling diversified behemoth. When David Ko passed away, his widow, Julia Ko, inherited his 42% stake, transforming her into the richest woman in New York. The Ko family protects its wealth by remaining fiercely private. Ko Industries generates over $125 billion in annual revenue, but because it is not publicly traded, it never has to file a quarterly earnings report. It is completely immune to the hostile takeovers and activist campaigns that plague public companies. While Ko Industries is headquartered in Kansas, Julia Ko's power move is absolute dominance over New York high society. She didn't just inherit cash. She deployed it strategically, donating $75 million to healthcare in Manhattan and executing massive transactions on billionaires row. When the public hears Ko Industries, they picture oil refineries and Midwestern industrial grit. But Julia Ko, [music] commanding a net worth of over $81.2 billion, controls her empire from the glittering heights of Park Avenue. The true power of the Ko fortune is its sheer terrifying liquidity, allowing the New York branch of the family to operate as an untouchable financial monarchy. Number one, the Bloomberg family, the information Imperium. In 1981, Michael Bloomberg was fired from investment bank Salomon Brothers, but he left with a $10 million severance check. He realized that Wall Street traders were essentially flying blind, manually calculating bond yields with paper and calculators. [music] He used his severance to build the Bloomberg Terminal, a dedicated computer system that delivered realtime financial data directly to the trader's desk. The Bloomberg Terminal is the ultimate unbreakable subscription monopoly. It costs roughly $24,000 a year per user and it does not offer discounts. Traders cannot cancel their subscriptions because the terminal's built-in messaging system is the primary communication network for global finance. If you do not have a Bloomberg terminal, you simply do not exist on Wall Street. It is an entirely inelastic product. Bloomberg's ultimate flex was buying the political machine, self-funding three successful campaigns for mayor of New York City. For over a decade, he essentially ran the financial capital of the world, both politically and financially. Sitting on a personal net worth of $12 billion, Bloomberg is not a media mogul. He sells the speed of information. The old New York families built their wealth on concrete and steel. But Michael Bloomberg built the central nervous system of global capitalism. He doesn't need to trade stocks because [music] he owns the digital infrastructure that makes those trades possible. He is the undisputed king of the empire state. - This Is The RICHEST Family That OWNS MANHATTAN...: Kind: captions Language: en Manhattan isn't just a city. It is a vault. Behind the neon lights of Time Square and the glass cliffs of Billionaires Row, the island is not owned by corporations, but by dynasties. These are the families who don't pay rent. They collect it. They bought the land before the skyscrapers were built, and they wrote the zoning laws that allowed them to rise. From the invisible family that owns the ground under Saks Fth Avenue to the dynasty that rebuilt the World Trade Center, we are tracking down the true landlords of New York. Number 10, the Resnik family, the steady hand. Most people assume that to win in New York real estate, you have to be loud. You have to put your name in gold letters on the front of the building and fight with the tabloids. But the Resnik family proves that silence is often more profitable than fame. Founded by Jack Resnik in 1928, this family built an empire on a simple, unglamorous principle. Hold on to everything. In the roaring 20s, while others were speculating on wild stock market returns, Jack Resnik was buying small Bronx walk-ups. He understood early that in a city with finite land, the person who holds the deed holds the power. While other developers were flipping properties or leveraging themselves into bankruptcy during the 1980s and the 2008 financial crashes, the Resnik stood still. They focused on acquiring prime office space and residential towers and simply never letting them go. This buy and hold strategy is the real estate equivalent of Warren Buffett's philosophy, but applied to steel and concrete. Today run by Jonathan Resnik, Jack Resnik and Sons controls over 5 million square ft of commercial space. Their portfolio includes the iconic 1 Seapport Plaza, a glimmering tower that defines the South Street Seapport skyline. But their genius isn't just in the glass towers, [music] it's in the dirt. They bought land in neighborhoods like Hudson Square and Tribeca decades before they became trendy, effectively futureproofing their fortune. When they acquired 315 Hudson Street, the area was a manufacturing district, a place for printing presses and warehouses, not ad agencies and tech [music] firms. Today, thanks to the reszoning and the migration of creative industries like Google and Disney, to the area, that boring warehouse space has transformed into some of the most expensive office square footage in the city. Their wealth is estimated in the low billions, but their influence is instability. In a city of gamblers, the Resnik are the house. They carry low debt, maintain high occupancy, and weather the storms that sink their flashy competitors. They remind us that the first rule of Manhattan real estate isn't buy low, sell high. It's buy low and never sell. Number nine, the Rose family, the residential rulers. If you have ever lived in a luxury rental in New York, there is a high probability you have written a check to the Rose family. For over 90 years, Rose Associates has defined how wealthy New Yorkers live. Founded by brothers David and Samuel Rose in the 1920s, this family took a different path from the office obsessed tycoons, they looked at the skyline and realized that while people might work in glass boxes, they want to sleep in limestone fortresses. The family's history is tied to some of the most ambitious housing projects in the city's history. They were the original developers of huge swaths of the Upper East Side, bringing high-rise luxury to neighborhoods that were previously dominated by town houses. They were also heavily involved in the management of Stivosent Town Peter Cooper Village, a complex so large it has its own zip code and its own power grid. Today, under the leadership of Amy Rose, one of the few women to lead a major dynasty, they manage over 14,000 units. But their power move was the transformation of downtown. They saw the financial district, which used to be a ghost town after 5:00 p.m. and bet heavily on turning old office buildings into luxury apartments. One of their crown jewels is 70 Pine Street. This building is an art deco masterpiece originally built as the headquarters for CIT GO and later AIG. It stands as the third tallest building in lower Manhattan, topped by a stunning limestone spire that resembles a snowcapped mountain. When the Rose family took it over, they didn't just repaint the walls. They executed a massive complex adaptive reuse project, turning 66 floors of offices into ultra luxury rentals. They didn't just renovate a building. They rebranded a neighborhood by adding a high-end market, a Michelin starred restaurant saga on the top floors, and hotel style amenities. They proved that Wall Street could be a place to live, not just work. The Rose family wealth is harder to track than public companies because they often operate as managers and partners. But their footprint is undeniable. They control the most intimate space in the city, the place where you sleep. Number eight, [music] the Zechondorf family, the luxury kings. There is rich and then there is Zechonorf rich. This family is responsible for creating the modern standard of the ultra luxury condo. If you see a building where a penthouse sells for $100 million, the Zeonorfs probably built it. The family dynasty began with William Zeondorf Senior, a man so flamboyant he owned the land the United Nations stands on today. He was a larger than-l life character who would travel in a limousine with a license plate that simply read WZ. But he famously lost his fortune, leaving the family to rebuild from the ashes. The modern fortune was solidified by his grandsons, Arthur and William Lie Zeondorf. They redeemed the family name by making one of the boldest bets in real estate history. Their masterpiece is 15 Central Park West. When they built this limestone tower in 2008, critics said they were crazy. The site was formerly the Mayflower Hotel, a slightly run-down property on a very expensive block. They spent billions to acquire the land and build a pre-war style building from scratch, hiring the legendary architect Robert AM Stern. They didn't want glass. They wanted limestone, marble, and permanence. They traveled to the same quaries that supplied the Empire State Building to ensure the facade had the correct shade of New York beige. The gamble paid off. 15 Central Park West, nicknamed the Limestone Jesus by brokers for its miraculous ability to command high prices, became the most successful residential building in the history of the world. It broke every sales record. Hedge fund managers, celebrities like Denzel Washington and Sting, and foreign oligarchs fought to get in. The building generated over $2 billion in sales almost immediately. One penthouse famously sold to Russian billionaire Dimmitri Ribolovv for $88 million. At the time, the most expensive transaction in New York history. The Zechonorfs didn't stop there. They went on to build 520 Park Avenue, another billionaire silo catering to the global elite. Their power lies in their rolodex. They know exactly what the top 0.01% of the world wants, and they build it. While other developers worry about affordable housing mandates, the Zechondorfs are worrying about whether the marble in the lobby was quarried from the same mountain as the Pantheon. Number seven, the Fiser family. The Quiet Giants. The Fiser brothers are the submarines of New York real estate. You don't see them coming, but they carry massive firepower. The dynasty began in 1915, founded by Martin Fiser, who started building brick homes in Queens. But as the generations passed, the ambition scaled up vertically. By the midentth century, the Fisers were building massive office towers in Midtown Manhattan. Their portfolio is a list of corporate fortresses. 299 Park Avenue, 6053rd Avenue, and 1,345 Avenue of the Americas. These are not vanity projects. They are the engine rooms of corporate America. These buildings house the headquarters of major banks, law firms, and hedge funds. If you walk down 6th Avenue, you are walking in the shadow of the Fiser family. The Fiser family's wealth is protected by a strategy of extreme liquidity. Unlike other developers who leverage themselves to the hilt, the Fisers are known for keeping massive cash reserves. When the market crashes, they don't panic, they go shopping. This conservative financial management has allowed them to survive the Great Depression, [music] the 1970s fiscal crisis, and the 2008 recession without losing a single major asset. But their real influence is political and civic. The family has deep ties to the city's unions and political infrastructure. Perhaps their greatest contribution to the city isn't a skyscraper, but a ship. Zachary Fischer, one of the second generation brothers, personally saved the USS Intrepid from the Scrapyard in the 1970s. He poured millions of his own money into converting the aircraft carrier into the Intrepid Sea, Air and Space Museum, which now sits on the Hudson River. Kenneth Fischer and Winston Fiser are major players in the city's philanthropic and civic circles today. They also run the Fiser House Foundation, which builds comfort homes for military families. They don't just own the buildings. They sit on the boards that decide where the subway lines go and how the city is zoned. Their status symbol isn't a building. It's a 150 ft yacht often seen docked near their properties. But in the boardroom, their status is confirmed by the fact that they manage over 10 million square ft of prime Manhattan real estate. They are the landlords of the establishment. Number six, the Malcolm family. The keepers of the icon. Owning a skyscraper is impressive. Owning the skyscraper is a different level of power entirely. The Malcolm family controls the Empire State Building. For decades, the ownership of the Empire State Building was a tangled mess of leases, subleasases, and feuding partners. The building was technically owned by a syndicate of thousands of small investors, while the land was owned by others, and the lease was held by partners that included the legendary Harry Helmsley and [music] later Donald Trump. But the Malcin family, led by Anthony Malcin, fought a yearslong war to consolidate control and bring the building public. This was not a polite boardroom negotiation. It was a siege. When the Helmsley estate wanted to cash out after Leona Helmsley's death, Anthony Malcin proposed consolidating the building into a publicly traded company. In 2013, they launched the Empire State Realy Trust, ES SRT, A R E I T, that trades on the New York Stock Exchange. This move was controversial. Many of the old school investors, some of whom had inherited shares from their grandparents, sued to stop it. They argued that the IPO would dilute their ownership and that Malcolin was prioritizing his own control. But Malcolin forced the IPO through, valuing the portfolio at over $4 billion. The genius of the Malcolin family was realizing that the Empire State Building isn't just office space. It's a tourist trap. The observation deck alone generates over $100 million a year in pure profit. It is one of the most profitable tourist attractions in the world, effectively subsidizing the office space below. While other landlords worry about vacancy rates, the Malcans are worrying about the line at the ticket counter. Anthony Malcin has since modernized the building, retrofitting it to be eco-friendly and attracting tenants like LinkedIn and Shuttertock. He took a dusty aging icon and turned it into a tech campus in the sky. The Malcolm wealth is tied to the stock market now, which gives them liquidity that other families lack. But their status is immovable. As long as that building stands at 34th and 5th, the Malcon name is etched into the skyline. Number five, the Ruden family. The tech landlords. The Rudens are the bridge between old New York and [music] new tech. Founded by Samuel Ruden in 1925, the family motto has always been, "We don't sell, they build, they buy, and they hold." This philosophy has amassed them a portfolio of 16 office towers and 18 apartment buildings. But under the leadership of Bill Ruden, the family pivoted. They looked at their portfolio of aging office buildings and realized that the future wasn't in law firms. [music] It was in technology. They were the first major landlords to fully embrace the wired building concept. In the mid 1990s, when the internet was still a novelty, they took an empty building at 55 Broad Street and pumped it full of fiber optic cables and broadband capacity. They launched it as the New York Information Technology Center. This single building helped launch Silicon Alley, proving that tech companies could thrive in Manhattan, not just in California garages. They also developed the Greenwich Lane in the West Village, a luxury condo project that replaced the old Saint Vincent's Hospital. This was a highly sensitive project. St. Vincent was a beloved institution famous for treating Titanic survivors and AIDS patients. The community was fiercely opposed to its demolition. The Rudens navigated the political minefield with grace, preserving historic facades while building ultramodern residences behind them. This project alone was a billiondoll windfall, selling out to celebrities and CEOs who wanted the charm of the village with the amenities of a hotel. Their greatest asset, however, is 345 Park Avenue, a massive slab of prime real estate that serves as their headquarters. The Ruddens are also the fixers of New York. Bill Rudden is often the first call the mayor makes when the city needs help from the business community. They chair the association for a better New York, ABNY. They are the diplomats of the real estate world, smoothing over the cracks between the public sector and private wealth. While other families fight in court, the Rudens are usually cutting ribbons. Their net worth is estimated at over $5 billion, but their social capital is priceless. Number four, the Spire family, the Lords of Rockefeller. If you want to understand ambition, look at Jerry Spire. He didn't just inherit a portfolio. He bought the heart of the city. Tishman Spire is one of the largest real estate owners in the world, but their crown jewel is Rockefeller Center. In 1996, Jerry Spire and his partners bought the Rockefeller Center complex for $1.2 billion. At the time, people thought he was overpaying. The complex was aging, the retail was struggling, and the underground concourse was dingy. Saw what others missed. He realized that Rockefeller Center was the town square of the world. He gutted the retail, brought in high-end tenants, and reopened the observation deck, top of the rock, which had been closed for decades. He turned a prestigious address into a money printing machine. By reconnecting the underground concourse and revitalizing the plaza, he increased the value of the property exponentially. But the Spire story also has its scars. In 2006, they led a group that bought Stivent Town for a record-breaking $5.4 $4 billion. It was an aggressive bet that they could convert middle-class apartments into luxury rentals. It failed. The 2008 crash hit and in 2010 they defaulted on the debt, handing the keys back to the lenders. It was one of the largest real estate defaults in history. Yet, the family bounced back with incredible resilience. They are the developers behind the massive MetLife building [music] which they bought for $1.7 billion and major projects in Long Island City. Rob Spire, the current CEO, has continued the aggressive expansion. They are not just landlords. They are global asset managers. They raise money from pension funds and sovereign wealth funds to buy [music] entire neighborhoods in Shanghai, London, and Brazil. But in New York, their power is visible every December when they light the Christmas tree. That isn't a public park. That is the Spy family's front lawn. Their estimated personal net worth is in the billions, but the assets they control exceed $60 billion. They are the family that corporate America pays rent to. Number three, the Leaf family. The cities within cities. Most developers build buildings. The leafracks build zip codes. The Leafra organization led by Richard Lerrack is one of the largest private landlords in the United States. Their wealth is conservative, massive and rooted in the idea of scale. The family fortune was built by Samuel Lfrack, a man who famously said, "I serve the mass, not the class." While the Zechondorfs were building for billionaires, Lefrack was building for the middle class. His signature project was Lafra City in Queens, a massive complex of 20 apartment towers housing over 15,000 people. It was a city within a city with its own security stores and infrastructure. But the family's greatest coup was Newport in Jersey City. [music] In the 1980s, Richard Lefrack looked across the Hudson River and saw abandoned rail yards, rotting peers, and industrial decay. He bought hundreds of acres for pennies on the dollar. People thought he was insane to try to build a luxury community in what was then a desolate part of New Jersey. Today, Newport is a gleaming skyline of its own with office towers, high-rise apartments, and malls. It is effectively the sixth burrow of New York City, and the Lefrack family owns almost all of it. They built the roads, the sewers, and the parks. They created a tax base where there was none. In Manhattan, they are major players as well, owning Gateway Plaza in Battery Park City. Richard Lfrack's net worth is estimated at over $6 billion. But the liquidity of the family is legendary. Because they tend to hold assets for decades and carry low debt, they are insulated from the market cycles that destroy other developers. The leftrack strategy is simple. Buy land by the mile, not the foot. Number two, the Goldman family. The invisible monopoly. You have probably never heard of Saul Goldman. You have probably never heard of his daughter, Jane Goldman. And that is exactly how they want it. But if you walk down any street in Manhattan, you are likely walking past a building they own. Solo Management, the firm controlled by the Goldman family, owns the largest portfolio of privately held real estate in New York City. We are talking about over 400 properties. Saul Goldman was a legendary dealmaker in the 1960s and 70s. He was a compulsive buyer. He would buy anything. Ground leases, town houses, office buildings, parking garages. He didn't care about the condition of the building. He cared about the land underneath it. At the height of his power, he owned more of New York than anyone else. When he died in 1987, his empire was plunged into a massive legal battle. His aranged wife sued for a larger share of the estate, leading to one of the most expensive divorce/probate battles in New York history. Eventually, his daughter Jane took the reigns. She has quietly grown the fortune into a behemoth. The Goldman portfolio is unique because it isn't just skyscrapers. [music] They utilize a powerful tool called the ground lease. For example, they own the land under the Grammarcy Park Hotel. Recently, when the hotel operator stopped paying rent, Solo evicted them. They also own the ground under the flagship Saks Fifth Avenue store. They own the dirt that other billionaires build their towers on. This ground lease strategy is brilliant. They let other people spend the money to build the building and manage the tenants. The Goldman's just collect the rent for the land. It is arguably the safest, most passive income stream in the world. Jane Goldman is the only female billionaire running a real estate firm in America with a net worth estimated at $3 billion personally, but the family assets are worth exponentially more, likely in the tens of billions. They are the invisible hand of Manhattan. They don't need to put their name on the building because they own the pavement you're standing on. Number one, the Durst family, the spire of power. At the top of the food chain sits the Durst family. They are the synthesis of everything we have discussed. The scale, the history, the tragedy, and the ultimate symbol of power. The Durst organization, currently led by Douglas Durst, controls over 13 million square ft of premier Manhattan office space. The dynasty began in 1915 with Joseph Durst, a tailor from Austria who arrived with $3 in his pocket. But it was his son, Seymour Durst, who began the aggressive assembly of land in Midtown Manhattan. Seymour was famous for his patience. [music] He would secretly buy up small brownstones and delies one by one over the course of 20 years until he owned the entire block. He was also a civic agitator, famously installing the national debt clock near Time Square to shame the government into fiscal responsibility. This patience allowed them to build their corporate monuments. one Bryant Park, the Bank of America Tower, and four Times Square, the Conde Nast Building. These buildings transformed Time Square from a Sidi district into a corporate hub. The Bank of America Tower, in particular, set a new standard for green skyscrapers, being the first in its class to achieve lead platinum certification. But their status as number one is cemented by a single asset, One World Trade Center. When the Port Authority was struggling to rebuild the World Trade Center site after 9/11, they needed a private partner who had the money, the muscle, and the credibility to get the job done. They called the Dursts. In 2010, the Durst organization acquired a 10% equity stake in the property and took over the management and leasing. By putting their name on the Freedom Tower, they effectively became the landlords of the Western Hemisphere's tallest building. It was the ultimate status signal. The family wealth is estimated at over $8 billion, but their story is also one of darkness. The family was haunted for decades by the saga of Robert Durst, the estranged brother of Douglas, who was accused and eventually convicted of murder. The HBO documentary The Jinx exposed the family's internal rifts to the world. It showed the brutal reality of dynasty dynamics. Douglas had to protect the business from his own brother, eventually testifying against him. Yet, despite the tabloid scandals, the Durst business machine never faltered. They continue to build, lease, and dominate. They own the skyline's most visible peak. When you look at New York City from a plane, the first thing you see is the spire of one world trade. And that spire belongs to the Dursts. They are the family that proves that in New York, land is thicker than blood. While hedge funds rise and fall and tech billionaires move to Miami, these dynasties remain anchored in bedrock. They remind us that the true power in New York isn't about the money you make this year. It's about the land you hold forever. Who do you think has the most influence? Let us know in the comments. And don't forget to like and subscribe to Global Status for more investigations into the world's elite.

That said, many details and nuances could not be independently verified by us. We also contacted all major Murdoch family members to request statements confirming the main aspects of the story, but none responded. [Music] It's early Sunday morning and young Rupert Murdoch is still in bed. He's 21 now. He's always been a bit of a loner, but now at Oxford, he's coming out of his shell. He's socializing and dabbling in left-wing politics, even joining the Labor Party. But what he really wants more than anything is the respect of his father. Sir Keith Murdoch is an Australian newspaper man. He dreams of creating a national news chain in Australia. So he skillfully cultivates political influence to bypass media regulations. So Keith burns with ambition. He hoped his son would too. But Rert never did well in school. He preferred to skip class to go gamble on horse racing. All his life, Rupert has yearned for his father's approval, but now he'll never have the chance to earn it. I was sleeping in one Sunday morning when my tutor came in and woke me up, told me that my father had died. Any memory I really have of it is one of um complete shock. >> Rupert flies back to Australia as fast as he can, but he misses his father's funeral. Then he finds out that his inheritance is significantly diminished. Sir Keith established family trust back in the 1930s. Family trusts are commonly used to manage assets and pass on wealth to future generations. But Sir Keith's death is sudden and unexpected, and his estate carries a serious amount of debt. To pay this off, Roert's mother had to sell off half of his father's estate. Now Rupert is furious. He's been cheated. Instead of the empire his father was working to build, he inherits just a single local newspaper. The news of Adelaide. He takes it and does what his father never could. He builds a newspaper empire. By the time he's 40, Rupert has gone further than his father could have dreamt. He founded the first national newspaper in Australia, The Australian, and soon he used that platform to help catapult a politician into the prime minister's office. He's conquered Australia. Next up, the rest of the world. This is Checkers, the British Prime Minister's 600 Hector County estate located 50 km from London. It's afternoon. Prime Minister Margaret Thatcher is sitting down to eat. Thatcher wants to keep this lunch a secret. For the rest of her life, she'll deny it ever happened, but notes in her official archive tell a different story. Sitting across from her is Rupert Murdoch. In a few months, Rupert will turn 50. He's easily one of the most powerful figures in Australian media. His company, Newscore, dominates about twothirds of the newspaper market in the country, and it's gone global, acquiring newspapers in the UK and the US. Now, he sets his sight on a new prize, the Times of London, one of the oldest and most respected newspapers in the world. Acquiring the Times would give him control over 40% of the British press, a monopoly, according to UK law. That would mean legal scrutiny. possibly hoping to avoid that scrutiny. Rert is now having lunch with the prime minister. Back in Australia, he established a successful formula. First, acquire a newspaper and make it successful through populist journalism. Then, leverage that success to obtain more properties. Next, gain control over a prestigious newspaper. Respected broad sheets tend to reach a different audience, powerful people, including politicians. Speaking directly to people like this means a chance to influence policy through endorsements and editorials. It can even mean having an impact on elections. For a newspaper mogul intent on growing an empire, that kind of influence could be worth its weight in gold. In the UK, Rupert applied his formula on a bigger stage. His towhold was News of the World, a weekly left-wing tabloid newspaper he acquired in 1969. A year later, he added a daily, The Sun. Both papers became known for headlines like these and for a willingness to focus on sex, drugs, and scandal. The Sun even introduced a new daily feature, a topless page three girl. It broke with tradition on Fleet Street, home of the British press. We were tearing up the old ways and inventing a new form of journalism, a new way. We were the on turbula of Fleet Street and proud to be so. >> Sales began climbing. A decade later, The Sun was Britain's bestselling tabloid. The new approach also earned Rert a nickname. That strat of society called him the dirty digger. Now Rupert is making his case to Thatcher for why he should be allowed to take over the Times of London. He just wants to make improvements to the paper. He says he'll be a hands-off owner. Later on, he'll guarantee this with a written promise. Afterward, he thanks her for the lunch with this handwritten note. He calls her my dear prime There's no evidence that Rupert and Thatcher made a deal. Thatcher carefully avoids any suggestion of this, even in her own documents. Her official record of the event states she did no more than wish him well in his bid. But this official record doesn't even cover the entire meeting, just the salient points. And in accordance with Thatcher's wishes, it was kept confidential. No one outside of the prime minister's office was to know about her lunch with Rupert. This has led some to speculate that over lunch, Rupert Murdoch and Margaret Thatcher may have arrived at an understanding. What is certain is that within weeks the Monopolies Commission decides not to look into RER buying the Times. Soon he owns the paper. At the same time, Britain is stuck in a recession. Thatcher is deeply unpopular. But in April 1982, Britain goes to war with Argentina over the Faulland Islands, and Rupert's tabloid, The Sun, goes all in on supporting it. As Britain's bestselling tabloid, The Sun reaches a huge portion of the British public, and many believe its aggressive support for the war hugely boosted Thatcher's popularity. Some have even suggested this might have saved her prime ministership. Rupert applies his formula on the other side of the Atlantic, too. His tabloid New York Post helps deliver the longshot candidate at Cootch to the New York City's mayor's office. >> I wouldn't be in a position to say I was mayor of New York City if Rert hadn't uh endorsed me. He gave me credit developing. >> Then the Post gets behind Ronald Reagan's presidential candidacy. >> The endorsement o of the New York Post of Ronald Reagan was crucial. Reagan carried New York State in the 1980 election. >> During the Reagan administration, Rubert's drive to expand faces fewer barriers. At the time, American regulations prohibit owning a major newspaper and a television station that share the same market. Under the Reagan administration, the Federal Communications Commission temporarily waved these regulations for Roupert. Only American citizens can directly own more than 20% of a TV station. So, Rupert applies for citizenship. In September 1986, he receives it quickly enough that some claim his application was fasttracked. With these roadblocks out of the way, he's free to buy a handful of television stations. Then, he buys a movie studio, 20th Century Fox. A month after receiving his American citizenship, he combines these new assets and launches the first new major broadcast television network since the 1950s. Fox's shows are a bright, edgy alternative to straight lace broadcast television, and Living Color presents sketch comedy with a black hip hop inflected perspective. The Simpsons and Married with Children reinvent the family sitcom as dysfunctional and even racy. And then Reagan eliminates the fairness doctrine, which had required broadcasters to give equal time to both sides of controversial issues. Many see this as paving the way for partisan media and eventually a channel that will change the American media landscape forever. Thank you very much for your attendance at such short notice. uh you have the uh press statement I think which tells you uh everything and that >> on January 30th 1996 Rupert Murdoch announces the Fox News Channel. His network TV station also called Fox has been on the air for a decade. Now he's finally making the leap to cable. Rupert's news station will be challenging the 400lb gorilla of 24-hour news CNN. To pull it off, he hires this man. >> I'd like to thank uh Mr. Murdoch for the kind words. Thank you all for coming this morning on short notice. I know that you're good journalists. Therefore, >> his name is Roger Als. Rupert hired him to build Fox News from scratch. They have a lot in common. Conservative politics, an instinctively anti-establishment point of view aimed at what they see as liberal elites. It's a crystal clear vision for Fox News. The network will cater to an overlooked audience, older, white, less educated, church-going, rural, and most of all, conservative, so-called middle America. The two men will reportedly joke that they've uncovered a whole new market, half the country. Ills was media adviser to presidents Richard Nixon, Ronald Reagan, and George HW Bush. He knows the target demographic well. He's also a controversial figure in the media landscape just weeks before the debut of the Fox News Channel. He left a job at NBC after reportedly hurling an anti-Semitic slur at a coworker. And he's known as a magician of the divisive power of media. For Fox, he's later quoted as saying, >> "It's not enough that conservatives like us, but that liberals hate us." >> Fox News Channel, fair and balanced. >> When Fox debuts, the studio looks cheap, thrown together, but it's undeniably different. It's colorful and deliberately edgy. Over and over, the station's slogan is repeated, fair, and balanced. It worked, and ratings soared. By 2002, Fox News had become the most watched cable news channel in America. For many conservatives, it became the only channel and its base was always expanding. In 2007, a study found that when Fox News entered a local media market, it pushed viewers to the right and made them more likely to vote Republican. This was called the Fox News effect. If a Republican politician wanted to reach their base on the national stage, it was said the only road went through Fox. >> Republicans originally thought that Fox worked for us. Uh, and now we're discovering we work for Fox. Newscor's power and influence now spanned several continents, but Rupert himself was aging. He had always considered his empire a family business, and like his father, he had considered his legacy. >> My two sons are very interested in having major careers, and I'm doing what I can to train them, give them opportunities, and see what mistakes they make and what triumphs they have. >> But which of his children would one day take control? On that point, Rubert could be mattlingly hard to read. You have three adult children, Lachlan, James, and Elizabeth, each of whom is now a very accomplished media executive in his own right. At the same time, you have reportedly told your biggest outside shareholder, Prince Wed, quote, "I'm going to stay on as CEO until I'm 130." >> So, as >> I'm more realistic than that, really. >> Rupert Murdoch raises children to be media moguls. He pushed them to be part of his empire. >> We knew that dad loved the time he had with us and he wanted us to be part of his world. And I think that as a child you have to be part of that world in some ways if you were going to be engaged with him. >> One day, one of them would take over the family business. But which one? According to observers, the children were constantly trying to figure out where they stood with their father. Who did he favor at any given time? Family life revolved around succession. Each child grew up trying to prove themselves worthy. Elizabeth Murdoch is often described as bright, capable, and like her father. With his help, she purchased a pair of television stations in California. About a year later, she sold them for a $12 million profit. Soon, she'll be climbing the corporate ladder at her father's satellite TV station, BK SkyB. She's tough, ambitious, and has her eye on the top job. Now he's working on his father's newspapers in Australia. He's young, rich, and a budding celebrity. know, the day-to-day. >> Prudence is Rupert's only child from his first marriage. She met Rupert while interviewing him for one of his Australian newspapers. Anna's on the News Corp board of directors. She's mother to Elizabeth James and Lachland and she helped raise Prudence. As they grow up, Anna worried about what the competition to succeed their father was doing to her children. >> Your sons, are they planning to go into the newspaper business? >> I think so, and so is my daughter. I mean, I can see um lots of arguments along the way. Who knows what will happen. >> It wasn't always easy. Rupert's work always came first. >> Newspapers are in his blood. He's a good Australian businessman who's come here. I'm going to show you how to do it. >> And it never ever stopped. >> It is awful sometimes and it is lonely and you are cut out of it. I don't think I change it for anything at all. For decades, Anna has been patiently waiting for him to retire so they can enjoy their golden years together. On that day, he would name his successor. Win or lose, the children would all be free of their burden of succession. Anna could enjoy life with her husband, free from the burden of news court invading every second of their lives. But instead, in 1998, everything changed. By now, Rupert is 67. He still hasn't retired, and he's met someone else. Her name is Wendy Deng. She works at one of Rupert's TV stations in China. She's been acting as his private translator when he visits the region, and she's half his age. Rupert insists nothing is going on, but Anna is sure he's having an affair. Either way, he swiftly cuts Anna out of his personal and professional lives. At first, Rupert and Anna announce that their separation will be amicable. Anna will remain on the News Corps board, but then in Newscore's annual report, Anna reads that she's retiring. Then she claims he gave her a direct order. You get off the board. But Anna has one last card to play. Legally, she's entitled to have the assets Rert has created since they've been married. But she wants something else. Anna sees what competing for succession is doing to her children, and she wants to make sure Rert can never toss them aside like he has her. To protect them, she wants to ensure that all four will inherit a share of the family business. So, she sacrifices half of what she's entitled to in the divorce. In exchange, Robert agrees to restructure the family trust. His four children will each control a single vote within the trust, while Rert will control four. But when he dies, his votes will disappear. Elizabeth, James, Lachland, and Prudence will control the trust assets equally. These assets have been valued at more than 7 billion. But there's an even bigger prize. They include almost half of the voting stock and two of the most influential media companies in the English-speaking world. The voting power will also belong to Rupert's children equally. These companies decide what millions of people read, hear, and watch on a daily basis. Managing a huge empire is quite the challenge.
