In the early 2000s, the Murdoch Empire was arguably at its peak, reaching five continents and 3/4 of the world's population. One of the companies controlled by the shares in the Murdoch family trust, NewsCore, owns media properties all over the world. The other is the most profitable cable news channel in America. In time, it'll become much more. James will later say that with a trust, the idea was that it would incentivize us to cooperate. What actually happens is very different. And at the heart of it is Fox News. This plane is flying from Sydney to New York. Inside it, Lachlan Murdoch is brooding. A few months from now, Lachlan will describe the events that follow to a reporter for New York Magazine. Our description is largely based on that reporting. Lachlan is now 33 years old. 5 years ago, his father promoted him to deputy chief operating officer of News Corp. As deputy COO, Lachlan is responsible for running the New York Post, the publisher Harper Collins, and 35 TV stations. Roupert is still head of the company, and he shows no signs of slowing down, but he's now 75 years old. One way or another, he'll have to step down soon. It's an open secret that Lachlan is winning the race for succession. At this point, his sisters are out of the running, and his brother James trails behind. Lachlan has long seemed to have an advantage. He's very much his father's son, sharing his conservative politics and deep love for Australia. In 1997, Rert told an interviewer that of his children, Lachlan was first among equals. But something isn't right. Lately, he's in conflict with Audrey Als, the father of Fox News. As one of Rupert's most trusted executives, Ills is largely allowed to do what he likes. This includes pushing his ideas for the TV stations Lachlan is in charge of most recent idea is a show called Crimeline. For financial reasons, Lachland turned it down. According to New York magazine, Als went to Rupert and told him about Crimeline. Rupert told him, "Do the show. Don't listen to Lachlan. Lachlan feels undermined by Rupert and it isn't the first time. Now he's heading back to America. He and his father are going to have a talk. In New York, Lachlan and Rupert discuss the situation over lunch. Rupert listens. He offers suggestions. Maybe changing the organization chart. But Lachlan knows his father isn't going to change. He wouldn't ask him to. Still, if things stay as they are, their relationship could seriously deteriorate. Something begins to dawn on Lachlan. He's used to thinking about himself in relation to the company and to his father. It's just difficult to sort of uncouple your life and your identity from the company, he thinks. Then he makes the decision. I have to do my own thing, he says. I have to be my own man. And with that, he steps down as deputy COO and moves back to Australia with his family. He remains on the newspore board. But in the race for succession, he's no longer the leader. Suddenly, James is. By this time, James is a long way from his years as a rebel. Back in 1995, he co-founded the hip-hop label Rockus Records. But then, Roert bought the label, dragging his son into the family business. He's been busy proving himself ever since. In 2007, he receives a major promotion to running Newscore operations through Europe and Asia. Then, something strange happens. James personality reportedly seems to change. He'd always been energetic, analytical, offbeat. At work, he was known as technocratic and given to business jargon, but now he seems brash, cocky. Some think he's emulating his father, and he's reportedly assembled a loyal team in London. To many, it looks like a rival power center in the company. No one knows exactly how Rert sees this, but to some observers, he seems proud and maybe a bit threatened. In 2010, Rupert proposes something unusual. family therapy. At a retreat at the Murdoch family in Caven Station, Australia, a specialist will help him and his adult children work on how they treat each other. Months later, news of the retreat was leaked to Vanity Fair. The magazine describes how the siblings came together to help James become a better leader and improve his relationship with Rupert. James later described something very different to The Atlantic. According to him, the therapist first met with everyone individually. Then at a session with the whole family, everything supposedly fell apart. According to James, there was gaslighting, accusations, strategic maneuvering. Even in family therapy, each of the Murdochs were advancing a personal agenda. James describes it as a car crash that left the family in even worse shape. Afterward, Elizabeth supposedly offers to create a family constitution. According to The Atlantic, James helps her. Together they create a draft including principles like we commit to undertake active dialogue with each other at all times and to relentlessly communicate openly with trust and humility and we will be vigilant of and defend against divisiveness either between us or that which could infiltrate from without. In February 2011, Elizabeth, James, Lachland, and Prudence reportedly all signed the document. But their newfound resolve to stick together is about to be tested like never before. This is Rupert Murdoch's war room. Rupert is fighting to contain the biggest scandal in Newscore history. Elizabeth is advising him and also providing emotional support. According to reporting, she thinks the best public relations move would be to let someone in the family take the fall. And her brother James is in charge of newspore operations in Europe and Asia. It should be him. Rupert slept on the idea. He agrees. Then he sends Elizabeth down the hall to fire her brother. From 1996 to 2001, Elizabeth was managing director of the British satellite TV network B SkyB, which later changed his name to Sky. She worked hard to position herself as Rupert's successor. Then in 2000, she abruptly quit. In one version of the story, she let Rupert know by sending him a fax. The exact reason is unknown, but many believe that within the family, Elizabeth had long been hitting the glass ceiling. Years later, James would reportedly save Rupert. He is a misogynist. After quitting the family business, Elizabeth was successful on her own terms in a way none of her siblings had been. Her production company, Shine, produced hit TV shows like The Biggest Loser and Master Chef. In early 2011, Rert bought Shine for 673 million, bringing Elizabeth back into the fold. He also asked her to join the News Corps board. She said yes, but quickly changed her mind, perhaps to distance herself from the massive scandal erupting around Newsore. On July 4th, 2011, an article in the Guardian blew the lid of a long simmering scandal. Reporters from Rupert's beloved tabloid, News of the World, had allegedly been hacking the phones of people they reported on. According to the Guardian, this included Millie Dollar, a teenager who had been abducted and murdered back in 2002. The scandal snowballed. The victims of phone hacking were said to include deceased soldiers, people killed in the London bombing in 2005, and a huge parade of celebrities, even the royal family. This had all happened before James took over as CEO. But he was linked to the scandal nonetheless, and he scrambled to contain the damage. His efforts reportedly included firings, internal changes, and a PR campaign. >> Practices of certain individuals did not live up to the standards and quality of journalism that we believe in and that I believe in. and that this company believes in. >> But then Rupert came to London and things got worse. He spoke to reporters. He met with the parents of Millie Dollar, the murdered teenager. None of this was coordinated with James. Rupert seemed to be undermining his son and CEO at every turn. Testifying before parliamentary committee, he continued to act on his own repeatedly. It even looked like he was throwing James under the bus. >> As for my comments, Mr. chairman of my statement which I believe was around the closure uh of the News of the World newspaper. >> Before you get to that, I would just like to say one sentence. >> This is the most humble day of my life. >> Thank you. >> That is the first I've heard of that. >> Elizabeth follows her instructions and walks down to James' office. She tells him that both she and her father think he should step down. Years later, James will describe his feelings in extended interviews with The Atlantic. He's stunned at Rupert's cowardice. He tells Elizabeth to make their father do the dirty work himself. Elizabeth and James' relationship will never be the same. For years, the two siblings will barely speak. Elizabeth will eventually call the episode one of her life's biggest regrets. James denies knowing the phone hacking had been a widespread problem, but his own employees claim under oath that they told him about it. The parliamentary committee concludes that at minimum, James has shown an astonishing lack of curiosity. The scandal forces Rert to shut down News of the World, the tabloid that established him in the UK. To contain the fallout, he also splits his empire in two. Print media remains part of a reconstituted News Corp. While television and film become 21st Century Fox. James takes most of the blame for the scandal. Rert relocates him to New York where he's made deputy chief operating officer of News Corp. It's spun as a promotion, but James was running operations in Europe and Asia completely on his own. In New York, he reports to the COO He's back under his father's watchful eye. Four years pass. Rupert is now 83 years old and he's still the head of his companies. It's lunchtime in the corporate dining room at Fox News headquarters. Rupert Murdoch is having lunch with Jared Kushner, Ivanka Trump, and her father. Right as their food hits the table, Donald Trump shares his big news. He's running for president. Rupert's eyes reportedly stay on his soup. He says only that Trump will have to be ready to take some hits in the press. The two men have known each other a long time going all the way back to New York in the 70s and 80s. Roupert is close friends with Jared Kushner. They've even vacationed together. Roupert is politically conservative and in 2011 Fox News was the platform for Trump's false birther crusade in which he repeatedly claimed that President Barack Obama lacked an American birth certificate. But according to an unconfirmed account by author Michael Wolf, who has interviewed Rupert extensively, Rupert supposedly considers Donald Trump quote, "a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] idiot." When the time comes for the first Republican primary debate, New York Magazine claims he instructs the moderator, Megan Kelly, to make things hard for Trump. >> You've called women you don't like fat pigs, dogs, slobs, and disgusting animals. >> But when Fox hosts continue to support other candidates and mock Trump, he skips the channel second debate. Trump's base and Fox's core audience are largely the same people. Without him, the second debate on Fox gets about half as many viewers. Trump may be a [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] idiot in Rupert's mind, but he seems to be crucial to Fox's ratings. The question is, can Rupert afford not to back him? It's a major turning point for Fox News. As the 2016 Republican presidential primaries wind down, Trump was starting to look inevitable. By now, the support of the Murdoch media had become crucial to Republican candidates. But Trump flipped this around thanks to social media. He had his own loyal audience. If Fox didn't support him, they might just go somewhere else. Thanks to alternatives like Breitbart and One American News, Fox was no longer the only conservative media outlet in town. So Rupert seemingly pivoted. ProTrump articles began popping up in the editorial section of the Wall Street Journal, a Murdoch paper. The covers of the New York Post began celebrating Trump's politically incorrect stances. And Fox News featured hours of proTrump programming in its prime time lineup. At Fox, Trump was now the guy. According to at least one Fox anchor, the new rule was don't bash him. The anchor is quoted saying, "We've thrown in the towel." In November 2016, Trump won the presidency of the United States. >> Right away, Rert begins visiting the White House. He's frequently on the phone with the new president, sometimes weekly. It's the culmination of Rupert's outsized ambition. He's come a long way from his secret lunch with Margaret Thatcher. He now has a direct line to the White House. Over the phone, Trump reportedly says to him, "Rupert, Rupert, you love this shit." >> Soon, Fox's lineup changes. It seems more closely aligned with Trump's politics with proTrump personalities like Tucker Carlson and Sean Hannity filling prime time slots. Trump often gets up early and watches Fox, then seemingly tweets about what he's seen. Commenters begin to notice a feedback loop developing. Some note that at times it's hard to tell where policy positions come from. Trump or Fox. There was a time when Fox drove Republican politics. Now it's becoming something totally new. A TV network that to many looks inseparable from the White House. Fox is arguably more powerful than ever. And it's still unclear who will follow in Rupert's footsteps. More than a year earlier, this is the Lamb's Club in Midtown Manhattan. James Murdoch is flying to Indonesia today, but he's made time for lunch with Lachlan and the COO of News Corp before he heads to the airport. Years from now, he'll discuss the events that follow in his interview with The Atlantic. James is 42. It's been 4 years since the phone hacking scandal, and its effects are still being felt. Still, the succession race may be coming to an end. When the time comes, reports say James will be the CEO of 21st Century Fox. Lachlan will be given a ceremonial title. But then something unexpected happens. Lachlan breaks the news. He's returning to the US to run 21st Century Fox. Lachlan, who quit years ago and hid in Australia while James toiled in the company and took the blame when things went wrong. Lachlan is going to be his boss. At this point, James has been in the family business for almost 20 years. He's paid his dues, but behind his back, his father's been making plans for his brother to return. In fact, he'd never fully given up on bringing his favorite son back. >> I hope and have said from the day he went back there that he's going to come back here. >> James does not want to report to his brother. He gets up and heads to the airport. While he's gone, he ignores messages from Rert and Lachlan. Rumors about him resigning whip through the company. By the time he returns, Rupert has changed his tune. James will still become CEO. Lachlan will be chairman. In reality, he sets his sons up as co-leaders. Both are expected to sign off on every major decision, but according to several accounts, the arrangement is a disaster. The brothers are unable to work together. In the fall of 2018, James spots a way out. A deal is beginning to take shape. Disney will buy nearly all of 21st Century Fox's assets, but not Fox News. James is enthusiastic. The deal could give him cover for exiting the company. Lachlan, on the other hand, is reportedly enraged. He's relocated his family from Australia to be head of a top tier media property and his father wants to sell it out from under him. In his interview with The Atlantic, James describes events coming to a head. Over dinner at the Grand Mercy Tavern in New York, Lachlan blows up. He rants about the deal, makes threats, and finally storms out. On the way out, he tells Roert that if the deal goes through, you will not have a son. Then he looks at James and you won't have a brother. But less than a year later, it happens. Disney buys 21st Century Fox. Rupert's children receive an enormous payout from the family trust, about $2 billion each. James steps down as CEO of 21st Century Fox, and exits the family business. Lachlan becomes chief executive of Fox, and along with Rupert, co-executive chairman of Fox and News Corp. The father and his favorite son are left running the Family Pistons together, just as Rupert always dreamed. The fight for succession seems to finally be over, but the real battle is about to begin. November 3rd, 2020. Election night, Trump versus Biden. Americans are glued to their screens. One by one, voting ends in each state. Millions of ballots are counted, and projections begin to take shape for the winner in each of the 50 states. Those projections are influential. They can shape public perception of who's going to win. >> Just before 11:20 p.m. Eastern time, Fox News makes a bold call. >> He's calling Arizona for Joe Biden. That is a big get. >> It's a risky move. Arizona is a hotly contested state in the presidential election, and data is still coming in, but being first to call the state could be a journalistic coup for Fox. It's only prudent for the election desk to get approval before taking such a risky move. Much later, a Murdoch biographer describes the decision this way. The Fox higher-ups sign up on a call one by one. The decision eventually reaches Lachlan, then Rupert. Will you approve announcing such a huge loss for Trump? >> [&nbsp;__&nbsp;] him, >> Rupert reportedly says. Fox made the projection. The Trump team is furious. They barrage Fox with demands the call be reversed. Fox refuses, but it seems Fox's audience is also furious. In the days that follow, ratings plummet. Then Fox pivots. >> Tonight, every American should be angry. You should be outraged. You should be worried. You should be concerned at what has happened in the election. >> The Dominion software system has been tagged as one allegedly capable of flipping votes. How, for example, did scenile hermit Joe Biden get 15 million more votes than his former boss, rockstar crowd surfer Barack Obama? It gets behind Trump's false narrative about the election being stolen. None of the higherups at Fox seem to believe those claims. Rupert reportedly calls them really crazy stuff. Still, internal communications show that many inside the network believe that Fox needs to get back in its audience's good graces to recover in the ratings. It's possible that echoing whatever Trump says will do that, but that will both cost Fox and the Murdoch family dearly. After Fox promotes the Stop the Steel narrative, Dominion Voting Systems, a voting machine company, sues the network for defamation. Fox settles out of court for almost $800 million. The Murdoch Empire skews politically conservative, but the Murdoch children seem to vary. As Fox News transforms under Trump, experts say their political differences become more stark. Lachlan has long been known as conservative. But according to his brother James, something changes. He now seems more partisan and reactionary, even willing to consider farright ideas like white nativism. Commentators have long seen Elizabeth and Pru as more liberal than the rest of the family. They've never said so publicly, but as the family business drifts rightward, private communications obtained by journalists seem to indicate a willingness to push back against positions like climate change denial. James' politics have always been harder to pin down. But just before leaving the family business, he begins to give hints of where he stands. Back in 2017, neo-Nazis marched in Charlottesville, Virginia. They carried torches enchanted blood and soil, and Jews will not replace us. There are violent and even deadly clashes with counterprotesters and police. When asked about the events, Trump seems to partially defend the neo-Nazis. You had some very bad people in that group, but you also had people that were very fine people on both sides. >> In fairness, this was taken out of context at the time. He's talking about other protesters who were supposedly at this rally. In the same speech, Trump explicitly condemns neo-Nazis and white nationalists. In any case, Fox News defended him. >> I think the president made a strong and clear message condemning all hate groups. >> James was disgusted. As CEO, he wanted to release a statement to his employees making his position clear. But he couldn't face the push back he assumed he'd get from Lachlan and their father. But his feelings became clear when an email he wrote to colleagues was leaked to the press. In it, he wrote, "I can't even believe I have to write this. Standing up to Nazis is essential. There are no good Nazis." James saw this as a nonpartisan matter. He didn't necessarily mind catering to conservative viewers. He did want to at least nudge the company towards accountability and higher editorial standards, but he believed Rert would never allow him to change the company's direction or decouple it from Trump. The battle for succession seemed resolved. It was Lachlan, but all four siblings still had a vote on the family trust that made their opinions on Fox's political orientations especially relevant. If James, Elizabeth, and Prudence decided to change Fox's direction, all they had to do was wait for Roert to pass away. At that point, they would outvote Lachlan. That very prospect would ignite the Murdoch family's final battle. It's 2020. This is Australia's black summer. Bushfire season has raged out of control, reducing 19 million hectares of land and 3,000 homes to ash. 33 people are dead. Most consider the cause to be at least partially due to climate change, but not the Murdoch properties. They call it arson. >> So, let me deal with the issue head on. Does climate change cause these fires? No. >> So, that's the key, the drought. And if the drought can't be blamed on climate change, you can't blame the fires on climate change, especially when so many are deliberately lit. >> James is still on the News Corps board, but in January, he releases a statement. He and his wife don't just believe in climate change. They're particularly disappointed with the ongoing denial among the news outlets in Australia given obvious evidence to the contrary. According to James, the rest of the board is so angry that he's pressured to resign. And he does, cutting his last remaining tie to the family business. Except for the trust. Speculation runs rampant. The media begins to report a rift in the family. Some ask if James is willing to contradict the company's climate change deniialism. What will he do when Rupert dies? Will he try to drag the family business towards the political center? According to The Atlantic, around this time, James' wife, Catherine, saw a kind of conspiracy theory begin to take hold between Rert and Lachlan. Rert had always been known as conservative, but not particularly partisan. His newspapers had supported Thatcher and Reagan, but also the center-left British Prime Minister Tony Blair. In 2008, he and the rest of the family were even have said to have backed Obama. But some believe that in the Trump era, he had grown more partisan. If this was true, and if James, Elizabeth, and Pru were planning to drag the network to the left when he died, that made them a threat. This growing mistrust between the Murdochs sent them on the road to the courtroom in Nevada. The trial that followed would be held in secret. But in 2025, two New York Times reporters obtained nearly the entire record of the trial, including a huge number of private messages between the Murdochs that were introduced as evidence. The direct quotes that follow are based on that reporting. According to the Times, after the Disney deal, Lachlan repeatedly tried to buy his siblings out of their shares of the trust and cement his own control. His attempts failed. During the Black Summer, Rupert sent him a message of encouragement. Been thinking a lot overnight about the deal with James at all. benefit of getting him out probably outweighs other considerations. Around this time, some observers believe Elizabeth, James, and Prudence were becoming a united front. When Rupert died, they would sort things out. With three votes on the trust, they could afford to wait. Soon, they would realize just how unprepared they were. It's April 9th, 2023. Succession is playing on HBO. The show is based on the Murdoch. The series is now in its fourth season. The main character, family patriarch Logan Roy, is modeled on Rupert. So far, the show has been heavily based on real life Murdoch lore. But in this episode, the show diverges sharply. Attention, major spoilers ahead. Skip until here if you want to avoid them. >> It is very, very bad. >> It seems very bad. I'm so sorry to call you like this. >> Can you put him on? >> Suddenly, and unexpectedly, Logan Roy dies, but it happens offcreen. The viewer experiences Logan's death through the reaction of his children who find out via a phone call. According to the New York Times, Elizabeth Murdoch is watching, and she feels panic rising inside her. Logan Royce children are completely unprepared. No one is in charge. Chaos reigns and eventually they lose control of the company. It all hits way too close to home. Elizabeth's father is now old, very old, 92. In 2018, he tripped and fell while on Lachland's yacht, suffering life-threatening injuries. In 2022, he was hospitalized with CO. Someday soon, it will just be Elizabeth, James, Lachland, and Prudence running the family's trust. The fate of the Murdoch companies will be in their hands. But can they work together? Elizabeth is deeply shaken. So is Mark Dero, her representative on the family trust. He sees the episode as a grim warning. I thought this is exactly exactly the thing we should be avoiding because that is chaotic. So Devou writes what he calls the succession memo. In it, he poses questions intended to prepare Elizabeth, James, and Prudence for what's to come. When they have equal say in the direction of the empire, could James accept Fox News as it is? If not, can someone buy him out? On the other hand, if he and Elizabeth and Prudence want to move Fox News to the center, would Lachlan accept it? But Rupert is still very much alive, and he's making plans to stop this very thing. It's early December 2023. Rupert is here to speak to his daughters. A few months ago, he finally retired. He retains only a ceremonial title, Emirus, chairman of the Fox board, but he still controls the trust, and he's about to make big changes. He's called a special meeting of the trust board. It's in 2 days. Before he pulls the trigger, he wants to see where Elizabeth and Prudence stand. At 4 p.m. he meets with Prudence. Rupert has talking points prepared. These companies are my legacy. I put everything into them over my life. The Murdoch companies are protector of the conservative voice in the English-speaking world. Then he comes out with it. I love each of my children and my support of Lachlan is not intended to suggest otherwise. But these companies need a designated leader and Lachlan is that leader. Gradually, Rupert reveals his plan. He, Lachlan, and a team of lawyers and executives have been working on it in secret for months. They call it Project Family Harmony. According to reporting by the New York Times, the Murdoch family trust is controlled by a single entity called Crudin Financial Services. Crudin is run by six managing directors, Elizabeth, James, Lachlan, and Prudence. Each one have a managing director with a single vote. Roupert has two directors with two votes. Rupert can never be outvoted, but when he dies, his votes will disappear. His four children will control the trust equally. So the plan is to create two new trustees, Crudin 2 and Crudin 3. Rert would control one, Lachlan the other. When Rupert dies, control of both would pass the Lachlan. Lachlan would have more power over the trust than even Rert ever had. Even working together, James, Elizabeth, and Prudence would be completely disenfranchised. But there is a catch. Legally, Rupert couldn't change the trust unless his changes were in good faith and in the best interest of the beneficiaries they affected. So, the argument is this. James, Elizabeth, and Prudence are planning to change the political orientation of Fox News. If they did that, ratings would drop, shares would become less profitable, and the trust would suffer. With Lachlan in total control, Fox would remain profitable, benefiting everyone. When her father is finished, Prudence is lost for words. All she can manage is right. Later he explains the plan to Elizabeth. Her response is more straightforward. You are completely disenfranchising me and my siblings. You've blown a hole in the family. Rupert leaves London without either daughter's support. Later, Elizabeth texted Lachlan that Project Family harmony was a massive gut punch. Lachlan wrote back, "Hey Liz, I really appreciate your support. Today is about dad's wishes and confirming all of our support for him and for his wishes. It shouldn't be difficult or controversial. Love you, Lachlan. The special meeting happened over Zoom. Roert read out the changes from a prepared script. The next day, he formally filed the changes to the trust. Later on, details would emerge that suggested the changes had a single target, James. In Project Family Harmony documents, he was called a troublesome beneficiary or just troublesome. The real goal was to stop him at all costs. Later, Rupert sent James a copy of his script from the Zoom call. A note said, "James, still time to talk. Love, Dad. P.S. love to see my grandchildren one day." Ironically, with these changes, Rupert has started the very rebellion he was trying to avoid. It's September 16th, 2024. Wu County Courthouse. Immediately after Rupert filed the changes to the trust, Elizabeth, James, and Prudence formally objected. They're inside these SUVs. Half an hour later, the second wave with Rupert and Lachlan arrives. Today is their first day in court. The descriptions that follow are based on records of the trial obtained by the New York Times. Rupert is the first in the family to take the stand. He's 93. His voice is sometimes faint, but he still seems perfectly lucid. He's asked about his motivation in changing the trust. He says, "If there's uncertainty about the management, the public will feel it inside. The company would feel it." Ensuring Lachland succession will eliminate this uncertainty once and for all, everyone will know where they stand. I just felt sure, very certain, that if these things weren't settled, there would be trouble, and the trouble would be damaging. The opposition lawyers question him. Isn't Project Family Harmony really about preserving Rupert's legacy by keeping Fox News on the political right? Rupert tells him there's no difference between the two. If Fox changes its political orientation, its audience will rebel again. Lachlan will keep things as they are, ensuring that the network and that the family's trust shares remain valuable. That benefits the whole family. His whole case rests on this argument. The changes are only intended to benefit shareholders. But then he keeps going. Fox News cannot fall into the wrong hands. It will be a disaster if it does. And the one at fault is James. Because of him, Elizabeth and Prudence will have to choose between their brothers. if one day they all share control over the trust. After his testimony, Rupert is excused from court due to illness. But James suspects another reason, cowardice. On day three, Elizabeth takes the stand. Rupert's lawyer paints her, Prudence, and James as spoiled billionaire children determined to thwart the man who had made them wealthy beyond most people's wildest dreams. Hasn't Elizabeth been fortunate to have become a multi-billionaire thanks to her father's enormous generosity? Elizabeth replies, I wouldn't call it generosity, but his enormous success. Yes. She is grateful to her father, though. All her life, he had mentored her in media and business. I feel like I was taught at the feet of the master. The lawyer hones in on a meeting that took place a year before. James, Elizabeth, Prudence, and their spouses had all met for dinner at Clarages, a London hotel and restaurant. Beforehand, the siblings had met in a private conference room for 90 minutes. They caught up, talked about the succession memo and plans for Rupert's death and complained about Lachlan and their family. Or so they said. Before the trial, a piece of evidence had come to light. In his diary, Prudence's representative to the trust had written about the brother's activities and the meeting at Clarages. In it, he writes that the three are planning to get rid of Lachlan. Rupert's lawyers paint a picture of a full-blown conspiracy. Elizabeth's lawyer develops a competing image. Rupert and Lachlan are the ones who've been scheming. It's Project Family Harmony that has torn the family in two, not some supposed conspiracy by Elizabeth, James, and Prudence. He asks Elizabeth to describe how Rupert explained his plans to her. She describes feeling angry and betrayed. "What is your relationship with your father like today?" the lawyer asks. "Absent, "What was it like before the December 6th special meeting?" "Unbelievably close. In the afternoon on day three, James testifies. Rupert's lawyers seek to portray him as a resentful son plotting his revenge. A puppet master scheming to undermine his brother. James admits they talked about outvoting Lachlan when their father died, but he insists they never discussed a coup. The lawyer asks about the dinner where Lachlan had essentially disowned him because of the Disney deal, and James is surprised to find himself crying. Later on, he chokes up when discussing his relationship with his father. You know, there have been a lot of hurtful things over the years. He tells the court, "We worked together very closely for a long time. It's hard." But he's direct when asked about identifying as the troublesome beneficiary. He calls the label garbage. They're making stuff up or believing things that are being told to create some sort of boogeyman that is a justification for stealing stuff they didn't want to buy. Day four, Lachlan is called to the stand. A question hangs over the trial. Is he the target of a plot by his siblings or is he targeting them? He insists that evidence of a plot against him has been piling up for years. He says thousands of stories in the media have quoted anonymous sources close to James, confirming his plans to ou Lachlan. One unauthorized biography of Lachlan quotes an anonymous source. Lachlan gets fired the day Rupert dies. As Lachlan sees it, quotes like this clearly come from people close to James. Taken together, they show why Lachlan and Rert had to act. But under cross-examination, cracks begin to appear. Lachlan can't approve that the anonymous sources come from James. In fact, he's changed his mind. Now he thinks they're people close to Elizabeth. But it's revealed that the source of the biography quote is much closer to home. It's a friend and adviser of Lachlan's who spoke to the author for more than 14 hours, and Lachlan had approved it. At least some of his evidence of a plot had come from within his own camp. Lachlan is forced to walk back other things, too. And he admits that changing the trust wasn't Rupert's idea. It was his. On December 7th, the probate court commissioner filed his report. It was unambiguous, even harsh. Rupert and Lachlan had acted in bad faith. Item by item, he dismissed the supposed evidence of a sibling plot. The Clarage's meeting had amounted to no more than a casual and inco conversation. Lachlan's testimony had been misleading. Statements he had made about a failed corporate merger were an example of Lachland Murdoch's lack of cander with the parties and on the witness stand. Ultimately, the changes to the trust were a carefully crafted charade. Their motivation was political. To ensure that Rupert's companies continue to be alternative conservative voices in media after he dies, Roert and Lachlan immediately appealed. According to the New York Times, their chances of success were slim. James, Elizabeth, and Prudence intentions remained unknown. Time seemed to be on their side, though. When Robert died, they would have the power to reorient the Murdoch companies. Behind the scenes, things may not have been so cut and dried. According to the reporting by the New York Times in February 2025, the balance of power seemed to shift. Rupert and Lachlan's appeal nudged forward with a state judge requesting oral arguments, and The Atlantic published a profile of James. In it, he criticized his father and brother and shared sensitive details of the sealed court case. Rupert and Lachlland's lawyers pounced, requesting sanctions against James, and a whole new trial. Outside of the trial, Rupert seemed more powerful than ever in some ways. Trump is once again president. Former Fox employees pepper the administration, including Trump's cabinet. Roupert has resumed his visits to the White House, though his relationship with Trump isn't without turbulence. In July 2025, Trump even sued Rupert in his paper, the Wall Street Journal, for its claims about a body note he allegedly wrote to Jeffrey Epstein. As the appeal seemed to progress, the Murdoch headed back to the negotiating table. In September 2025, a deal was announced. Elizabeth, James, and Prudence would sell their shares of their empire for $1.1 billion each. The current Murdoch family trust will be dissolved and a new one created, including Lachlan and Rupert's two daughters with Wendy Deng, Grace, and Kloe. In a way, both sides could claim victory. Elizabeth, James, and Prudence were finally severing their ties with an empire they increasingly seem to disagree with politically and doing so with an impressive payout. Lachland would succeed his father as head of the family business, and Rupert, after decades of intrigue and infighting, has finally secured his legacy. In Lachlan's hands, Fox News and News Corp, seem likely to stay reliably conservative. They will remain what Rupert has always envisioned, a family business. But the family itself may never be the same.

The first three steps to planning any great event is understanding your guest count, your budget, and then your location and season. And each of those sort of impacts the other, so your guest count will then impact your budget, which will also impact the spaces that you're gonna look at to host your party. More and more people are looking to have events in unique spaces and I love that vibe, but what they don't realize is if you rent a beautiful, empty space, you're bringing everything in. It comes with plates, flatware, glassware, china, bartenders, and if you love the ambience, then you already are saving so much money on decor and rentals. Most candles come in white but they also come in every color of the rainbow. And when buying candles, you wanna buy high and low. This way we're playing with shape, size, and texture, which really sets the ambience for a great party. The flower of the moment that is the most affordable but also making a comeback is the carnation. You can buy a lot of them, and make a big wow moment without breaking the bank. When hosting a party, you don't want to play server and host. A station is a great way to do that, because you can do all the preparation in advance, and then guests can help themselves as they want. - Asian frugal habits that saves me thousands every year 💸: Kind: captions Language: en It's not about the takeaway coffee. It's about the habit. The habit of getting a takeout drink, which often comes with a sweet pastry on the side. And a few blocks from the cafe, we see a cute dress and decide to splurge with a matching scrunchie to go with it. All this shopping gets us hungry. So, we pick up some takeout on the way home and decide to get an Uber because we deserve it. This on repeat is no longer a habit, but it's a lifestyle. an expensive one at that. And some might say, what is the point of working so hard when you can't even treat yourself? And to that I say, maybe we're just conditioned to think that splurging on ourselves is a form of self-care. But actual self-care looks like working towards a life that truly enriches us. And maybe these little splurges are distractions along the way. Growing up in Korea, we lived very frugally, but I was still happy as I could be. Now in my mid-30s, I've come full circle and still embrace many of those same habits. These small everyday decisions might seem insignificant on their own, but it does add up over time. But more importantly, I found that living a lowmaintenance lifestyle comes with a piece that outweighs anything I can buy. So, I don't mind skipping the coffee. I never received any kind of gifts growing up. Not for my birthday or Christmas or any kind of holiday. And some people might hear that and think that I had a really sad childhood. But honestly, the opposite is true. My parents always try to gift us some kind of experience to remember these days. I remember for my 10th birthday, we spent the whole day just kind of walking the pier and we had fish and chips. I still remember to this day and I feel like these memories always stay with us way longer than any gift ever could. And I feel like these special occasions and holidays are no longer about spending time together and it's all about buying things for each other, all about the marketing, the sales, the limited editions and it's just really exhausting. So my husband and I, we don't gift each other anything. And instead, for these special occasions, we just like to spend a whole day doing our favorite things together. And one of the reasons, too, is because I feel like we have everything that we need. And if we want something, then we'll put it on our wish list and go and buy it ourselves. So there's really, really no pressure to go and buy something just because it's a specific day to prove our love to each other. It's just something that I don't deal with anymore. Thinking back, I feel like I only started eating out when I moved to the States. It was because it was like a cool thing to do with my friends and I always worked in the restaurant industry. But growing up, my mom always cooked for us. I remember she would always pack our lunches and my dad's bento box. And even when we were traveling, she would bring this gigantic rice cooker with us everywhere we went and she would cook for us. I definitely appreciate her more for doing that because it's a lot of dedication and time and effort that goes into it. But I know because she did that, we were able to save so much money. You guys know that for this year, I really wanted to cook more at home. I feel like I am slowly adjusting my habit and mindset around it. definitely takes a lot of creativity and a lot more planning, but honestly, I feel like the savings is so much worth it. So, for example, my husband and I, if we go out on a date to this bin, uh we will spend probably around €50, but if we cook at home and I could buy best of the ingredients, then it would probably be around less than €20 together. So, that's my biggest motivator. I would say 90% of the time I cook at home and 10% of the time we go out and try new restaurants. I am generalizing of course, but I feel like Koreans are very healthconscious people. I know a lot more people now in soul, they're going to Pilates and going to weight training and going to gyms and things, but I feel like from a very young age, we're told to have kind of a healthy lifestyle and integrate it into our lifestyle. For example, we always like to stretch in the mornings. We like to drink a lot of herbal teas and we have fruit for dessert and there's a lot of nice hikes in Korea. So on the weekends we would come together with our friends and family and go hiking. And these habits are kind of second nature to me. I've always kind of abided by these rules. And instead of getting these really expensive gym memberships, class passes, and having these really expensive hobbies just so that I could be active, I find it so much easier to just integrate it into my lifestyle and I can do it for free. Something that I never understood are those really nice aesthetic jars or containers. I guess the container store in general. I mean, it does look really nice when everything is displayed out in the open and everything is matching, but it's so expensive to buy these things where you could just get it for free from the grocery store. And I think the key is to buy something that's already in a really nice container. So after you use the item or eat it, then you can just wash and use the jar. A little bit of dish soap, just soak it overnight and it just comes right off the next morning. And I have tons of jars and I use it for everything because I buy a lot of loose leaf tees and buy things in bulk and they're really really handy to have. And this isn't just for glass jars. Obviously, you can repurpose everything and I think it's just being smart. You can save the wrapping paper from the original packaging. You can use the rice water to water your plants. You can even save those little yogurt containers and use it for when you're waterc coloring. You can even save your kitchen scraps and make broth with it. I mean, there's just infinite ways. You know, I think before you throw something away, it's a really good rule of thumb to ask yourself, can I use this for something else? And this over time can save you tons of money. Okay, this kind of goes with my previous point, but I always say everything has a first life and a second life. First, of course, I try to use up every little bit of it. And I'm definitely cutting the toothpaste in half and getting every little bit because that's money wasted if you don't use it. Then that toothbrush becomes a scrubbing tool. The old t-shirt becomes a rag. The paper bag becomes a trash liner. You get the point. I love a simple minimal home, but just dedicating one or two drawers for these little things that you could use for DIY purposes, it really isn't going to hurt anyone. And in Asian bath, you're actually losing money if you throw away something that's perfectly usable. I don't really know if this is an Asian thing or just because my husband and I grew up having to be very resourceful, but we really do not like to use other people, professionals to do something if we can do it ourselves. I cut my own hair, my husband cuts his. I groomed the dogs. We're doing all the home renovation ourselves and we're also making some of the furniture. So, we're definitely DIY kind of people. We live in an information age. We can literally Google anything or YouTube it and try it for ourselves. To me, this is way less stressful than having to manage other people to do it or having to pay someone else to do it. [Music] I don't know if you guys know, but before YouTube, I was a financial counselor. And when I was going over people's finances, I couldn't believe how many people just live off of their credit cards. And I understand that sometimes it's necessary to kind of propel you forward in life, but it is a really slippery slope and also it's not sustainable over time. The credit card culture isn't so common in Asia and I grew up always thinking that I have to pay off all my debts because that was something that my dad really drilled in me. I use credit cards because I can get the hotel points and the flight points and everything. And we have a mortgage, but it was pretty much the same as what we were paying for rent. But other than that, we don't have any lingering debt, especially from overspending on something that we couldn't afford. Like I said, it's not about the few dollars we save by living this way. Refinance&nbsp; simply means changing the structure of a loan&nbsp;&nbsp; so it consumes less cash flow. lower&nbsp; rates, longer amortization period,&nbsp;&nbsp; consolidate from high interest rate to low&nbsp; interest rate. Right? Sometimes the fastest way&nbsp;&nbsp; to improve cash flow isn't paying off a loan. It's&nbsp; simply restructuring it. Pocket the difference. When you have&nbsp;&nbsp; assets you can use as collateral, you get better&nbsp; interest rates. Maybe it's even a loan against a&nbsp;&nbsp; brokerage account or again against your cash value&nbsp; life insurance. The second R is renegotiate. Means&nbsp;&nbsp; questioning what you've been tolerating. Interest&nbsp; rates, fees that no longer reflect reality.&nbsp;&nbsp; Every dollar you overpay, it's a dollar lost.&nbsp; Renegotiation alone can create immediate returns&nbsp;&nbsp; that boost your bottom line without risk.&nbsp; There's no delay. I worked with a dentist&nbsp;&nbsp; that got a streamline on an existing mortgage.&nbsp; Meaning he just got to lower his interest rate&nbsp;&nbsp; with the same company without having to pay&nbsp; all the closing costs of a refinance. See,&nbsp;&nbsp; good credit, good cash flow, good collateral, and&nbsp; good connections. Credit, cash flow, collateral,&nbsp;&nbsp; and connections. Those are the four C's to&nbsp; renegotiation. Now, the last R. Reallocate&nbsp;&nbsp; means looking where your money is sitting idle.&nbsp; If you're earning 3% on savings while paying 8%&nbsp;&nbsp; on a loan, you've got cash flow every single&nbsp; month that's being lost. Paying off that loan,&nbsp;&nbsp; it's not being conservative. It's a guaranteed&nbsp; tax-free return that will increase your cash flow.&nbsp;&nbsp; Just had someone at my cash cabin experience&nbsp; where they're getting less than 2% on their&nbsp;&nbsp; Edward Jones account and paying 22% on credit&nbsp; cards. Easy choice. Let's save the money. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; your mortgage lender doesn't care about your car&nbsp; loan, your car loan company. They don't care about&nbsp;&nbsp; your business line of credit. Nobody's looking at&nbsp; all these together. Coordinated team looks at all&nbsp;&nbsp; of it and says, "Which loans do we refinance?&nbsp; Which fees do we eliminate or renegotiate?&nbsp;&nbsp; And where does money create the most cash flow?"&nbsp; That's the interest leak. It only gets found&nbsp;&nbsp; when someone sees the full picture. So, here's&nbsp; your test. Pull up your loans. Breach one ask.&nbsp;&nbsp; Can I refinance at a lower rate or better terms?&nbsp; Is there something I've been tolerating I could&nbsp;&nbsp; renegotiate? Is there idle money somewhere&nbsp; earning less than what the loan's costing me?&nbsp;&nbsp; If you haven't asked all three questions about&nbsp; your loans, you're likely losing money with&nbsp;&nbsp; money that's going to the institutions instead&nbsp; of to your pocket. So, let's go to investments.&nbsp;&nbsp; Most people think investing means putting money&nbsp; in the stock market and hoping it goes up.&nbsp;&nbsp; That's one-dimensional. The best investments&nbsp; do three things at once. They create cash flow,&nbsp;&nbsp; they build equity over time, and they provide real&nbsp; tax advantages. Again, when you have a coordinated&nbsp;&nbsp; team, that becomes easy because it's within reach.&nbsp; Trying to do too much on your own takes time, and&nbsp;&nbsp; it steals your energy. If your financial person's&nbsp; chasing returns without considering your taxes,&nbsp;&nbsp; your cash flow, or what your business really&nbsp; requires, they're really going for their piece&nbsp;&nbsp; without seeing your whole picture. See, I want&nbsp; your dollar to do more than one thing. I want to&nbsp;&nbsp; add more options of the future, more choices, and&nbsp; less risk. A coordinated team makes that possible&nbsp;&nbsp; because they see how your investments connect to&nbsp; your tax strategy, how your insurance, and your&nbsp;&nbsp; cash flow all impacts it at once. Plus, only 8%&nbsp; of managed funds outperform index funds. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; private funds have the opportunity to far out&nbsp; earn the stock market, but they also have a higher&nbsp;&nbsp; chance to lose everything. So, where do you have&nbsp; the most control? Well, lowering fees, admin fees,&nbsp;&nbsp; legal fees, right, with retirement plans,&nbsp; expense ratios with investments, 12b1 fees&nbsp;&nbsp; with mutual funds and fiduciary fees, which is&nbsp; that percentage you pay to your financial adviser&nbsp;&nbsp; who all they did was collect the assets.&nbsp; They don't actually invest the money.&nbsp;&nbsp; They hand it to someone else. This 1 to 2% in&nbsp; fees can be 20 to 50% of your overall return&nbsp;&nbsp; cuz compound interest also has compounding&nbsp; costs. And those small percentages, they add up.&nbsp;&nbsp; So that's the investment leak. Your test, look&nbsp; at your biggest investment outside your business.&nbsp;&nbsp; Does it create cash flow, equity growth,&nbsp; tax advantages? If it only does one,&nbsp;&nbsp; you're stuck in a one-dimensional investment.&nbsp; Then think, do you know your fees and the&nbsp;&nbsp; impact on your funds or your mind if they go down,&nbsp; right? Do you have influence or knowledge? If not,&nbsp;&nbsp; you might be gambling your future and paying hefty&nbsp; with those fees. Finally, insurance. Nobody wakes&nbsp;&nbsp; up says, "Let's talk insurance, but I'm going to&nbsp; put more money in your pocket." This one's sneaky&nbsp;&nbsp; because people either have too much of the&nbsp; wrong kind or not enough of the right kind.&nbsp;&nbsp; Your insurance person, they sold you a policy. Did&nbsp; they coordinate your deductibles with the amount&nbsp;&nbsp; of savings you have? Are there places you can more&nbsp; effectively cover certain risk through something&nbsp;&nbsp; like a captive insurance structure? It's basically&nbsp; setting up your own insurance company. It's like a&nbsp;&nbsp; CC Corp. And in the tax code, it's either 831A&nbsp; or B. It just simply says, "Hey, I'm going to&nbsp;&nbsp; cover these things on my own." And then you can&nbsp; raise deductibles or replace insuranceances.&nbsp;&nbsp; And if you don't have a claim, you get to pocket&nbsp; the money. Plus, you get to invest it like an&nbsp;&nbsp; insurance company. Now, it's going to require&nbsp; an attorney or CPA, and you want your insurance&nbsp;&nbsp; professional to collaborate. But did your attorney&nbsp; look at an asset protection combined with your&nbsp;&nbsp; current coverage to protect what you built? Did&nbsp; your insurance person talk to that attorney?&nbsp;&nbsp; This is where a lack of coordination, not&nbsp; having a family office, cost people big time.&nbsp;&nbsp; See, insurance, it's not just protection, it's&nbsp; permission. When you're properly protected, you&nbsp;&nbsp; make bolder decisions. You invest with confidence.&nbsp; You sleep at night. When you're not protected,&nbsp;&nbsp; you play small. You hoard cash just in case. That&nbsp; fear cost you more than any premium ever would.&nbsp;&nbsp; That's the insurance leak. And it only&nbsp; gets plugged when your insurance person,&nbsp;&nbsp; your attorney, and maybe even your CPA are&nbsp; in the same conversation. So your test,&nbsp;&nbsp; if something happened tomorrow, how&nbsp; much insurance would you get today?&nbsp;&nbsp; Probably as much as you can, right? We don't hate&nbsp; having insurance. We just hate paying for it.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, how you design it makes a difference.&nbsp; See, when we put all these four together,&nbsp;&nbsp; taxes, interest, investments, insurance, each area&nbsp; on its own might be two or 3%. Might even hardly&nbsp;&nbsp; notice it. But together, that's 10%, 15%, even&nbsp; 20% of money that's rightfully yours in someone&nbsp;&nbsp; else's hands. - 7 Signs Someone is Secretly Wealthy: Kind: captions Language: es-MX La mayoría de los millonarios y multimillonarios son completamente invisibles. Probablemente estén sentados junto a ti en Starbucks, y ni siquiera sabes que tienen siete, ocho o quizá hasta nueve cifras en el banco. En mi opinión, hay siete señales reveladoras que delatan si alguien es rico en secreto o no, así que si sabes qué buscar, podrás encontrarlos. Sinceramente creo que si logras adoptar algunos de sus hábitos, podría ser la clave para construir una verdadera riqueza por tu cuenta. La primera señal que vamos a ver es que la gente rica evita a toda costa hablar de las tres E. Ahora, estas tres E se refieren a ganar, a lo caro y al exceso. Por la primera E, que es ganar, nunca hablan del dinero que están ganando porque para ellos, si alguien siempre está hablando de dinero y de cuánto gana, demuestra que esa persona todavía está en la etapa de querer probar algo. Los verdaderamente ricos no tienen nada que probar porque en secreto ya saben que tienen el dinero y no hay necesidad de decírselo a nadie. La segunda E: no presumen su última compra cara. Mientras otros publican bolsas de diseñador o relojes de lujo en redes, los ricos en secreto callan sus adquisiciones. Porque si alguien habla constantemente de cuánto costó su carro, de lo caras que fueron sus vacaciones, es una clara señal de que estiraron su presupuesto para poder pagarlo y están tratando de maximizar cada dólar que gastaron obteniendo algo de fama en redes sociales o quizá contándoselo a sus amigos. Y la tercera E es que evitan vivir en exceso, al menos en cuanto a su imagen externa. La gente rica entiende que los verdaderos lujos en la vida son tener opciones, comodidad y libertad, y no tener las cosas más ostentosas. Si alguna vez te encuentras con una persona que habla de cuánto gana, cuál fue la compra más cara que ha hecho recientemente, o simplemente vive en exceso y lo presume, puedes apostar que esa persona probablemente no es rica. ¿Te crees mucho por tu Range Rover? Pues sí. Al menos yo tengo carro. ¿Tú siquiera tienes uno? Quédate aquí. ¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora? ¡¿Quién es el plebeyo ahora?! Eso pensé. Okay. Entonces, eso significa lo contrario. Los que son callados, son ricos en secreto, y creo que si puedes adoptar este tipo de actitud en tu vida, podría ayudarte a generar más riqueza. Porque la energía hipotética que gastarías en las apariencias, o en impresionar a otros, podría redirigirse a construir tu patrimonio neto real. La señal número dos de que alguien es rico en secreto: conduce un auto confiable, de cinco a más de diez años, ya pagado. No manejan autos ostentosos como BMW de alta gama, Porsches, Rolls-Royces o Lamborghinis. Eso es porque los ricos saben lo brutal de la depreciación, así que tirar quinientos a ochocientos dólares al mes en la mensualidad de un carro por algo que pierde valor cada día es un retorno de inversión muy negativo. Y si haces cuentas, podrías invertir de seis mil a nueve mil seiscientos dólares anuales en su lugar, y en diez años con rendimientos compuestos, eso bien podría valer más de cien mil. Además, un auto de cinco a más de diez años ya perdió la mayor parte de su valor por depreciación. Las curvas de depreciación de autos varían, pero se aplanan drásticamente tras cinco años. Esto significa que la mayor parte de la depreciación ya ha ocurrido, por lo que el valor se estabiliza. Si quieres ahorrar dinero en cuanto a carros, creo que tiene mucho sentido comprar un carro usado con unas treinta mil millas o quizá de tres a cinco años de uso. Ese suele ser el punto ideal entre valor y depreciación, pero si solo quieres ser una persona rica que pasa desapercibida, quizá optarías por algo más viejo. Pueden ver en esta gráfica que estos son los modelos de los carros, y el "aquí" que se indica en la gráfica es el punto de inflexión alrededor del año seis o siete, y es cuando la depreciación realmente se desacelera. Los ricos en secreto entienden que un auto es solo transporte. Prefieren ese dinero extra en inversión, evitando pagos de auto y destinándolo a activos que generen riqueza. En otras palabras, prefieren ser ricos que solo parecer ricos y al mismo tiempo deber un montón de dinero por un carro. La señal número tres de que alguien es rico en secreto es que el dinero que sí gastan lo invierten en cosas que la gente normal ni siquiera consideraría. gastan su dinero en cosas poco comunes, por ejemplo, para tener más tiempo, pueden tener un pasatiempo de coleccionables raros, o gastan dinero en pequeños lujos o comodidades de las que no te enterarías a menos que, por alguna razón, pudieras pasar veinticuatro/siete con ellos todos los días. Hablemos primero del tiempo. Una persona rica entiende que el único recurso finito en todo el mundo y en la vida es el tiempo. Por lo tanto, podrían comprar su tiempo de vuelta y están dispuestos a gastar una cantidad desproporcionada de dinero en las cosas que pueden devolverles ese tiempo. En viajes, podrían volar en privado, evitando seguridad, saliendo cuando quieran y saltándose el tedioso abordaje y desembarque. Para la comida, un chef privado les hace las compras y cocina saludable, ahorrándoles incontables horas semanales. Y las tareas del hogar, también las delegan. Para eso, seguro tienen mucha ayuda. Y en todos estos casos de ahorro de tiempo, realmente no tendrías idea de que alguien gasta dinero en estos servicios a menos que pudieras observar a esta persona por un período prolongado. La siguiente categoría de gasto en la que una persona rica podría derrochar, es en pasatiempos poco comunes o caros como coleccionar arte raro, vinos finos, recuerdos deportivos o quizá hasta tarjetas de Pokémon. En lugar de mostrar su riqueza como una persona normal, prefieren hacerlo con una apreciación por el lujo y artículos raros. Poseer algo que cientos de miles ya tienen no es lo suficientemente genial para ellos. Necesitan sentirse únicos; cuanto más raro el artículo, más codiciado. En mi opinión, es naturaleza humana. Cubiertas las necesidades básicas, los ricos ya no compiten con otros. Están tratando de diferenciarse por completo de todos los demás, y una de las formas en que lo hacen es coleccionando bienes raros o quizá comprando su tiempo de vuelta, que es su activo más... eh, valioso. Otra parte de la naturaleza humana es suscribirse a mi boletín gratuito llamado Hump Days. Ahí publicamos noticias de negocios dos veces por semana, los miércoles y domingos. Y si quieres ser rico en secreto algún día y disfrutas los videos de aquí, asegúrate de suscribirte. Es completamente gratis en la descripción de abajo. Perdón, tenía que meter el comercial sutilmente. Bueno, continuemos. La señal número cuatro de que alguien es rico en secreto, es que son conscientes de su imagen. Ya aludí un poco a esto en la primera señal, pero la gente rica entiende que, por lo general, la gente siente envidia de lo que no tiene. Incluso si tu mejor amigo está feliz por ti el noventa y nueve por ciento de las veces que tienes éxito, siempre habrá un pequeño pensamiento en su mente, quizá en el fondo, de "¿Cómo sería si mi vida fuera como la de ellos?" o "¿Por qué no pude ser yo?". Esa es la naturaleza humana. Así que, para no incomodar a nadie, evitan hablar de cualquier cosa que pueda llevar a alguien a entender, por deducción, cuánto dinero podrían tener o cuán exitosos son. Si le preguntas a una persona rica cuál es su patrimonio neto, es probable que diga alguna frase como: "Me ha ido bastante bien", o "Ahí la llevo", o quizá, "Ciertamente no soy la persona más rica que conozco". Nick, no puedes pagarlo. ¿Tu familia es realmente rica? Cómodamente. Eso es exactamente lo que diría una persona súper rica. Y de hecho, esta es una historia real que me pasó. Estaba comiendo con un individuo que vale más de nueve cifras, o sea, al menos cien millones de dólares de patrimonio neto. Y cuando hablaba de sus amigos en la comida, todos multimillonarios, soltó algo en la conversación muy casualmente. Fue algo como: "Soy pobre en comparación con todos mis amigos". Para mí, fue una locura escuchar eso porque me demostró que a medida que subes en la escalera de la riqueza, siempre habrá alguien más rico con quien compararte. Digo, eso es cierto para casi todos en el mundo a menos que seas Elon Musk, claro pero sigue siendo una locura pensar que alguien en el Top 400 de Forbes, por ejemplo, todavía podría compararse con personas que están más arriba en la lista. La comparación está tan arraigada en la naturaleza humana. Estaría dispuesto a apostar que hay al menos una persona en la lista del Top 400 de Forbes que piensa que no es lo suficientemente rica, aunque racionalmente, están perfectamente bien. La gente rica quiere ser cuidadosa con su imagen externa también por razones de seguridad. Si son constantemente ostentosos, o le dicen a la gente cuál es su patrimonio neto, solo están atrayendo atención negativa y resentimiento de otros, lo que podría comprometer su seguridad. La quinta señal de que una persona es rica en secreto es que hace preguntas detalladas sobre compras de menos de cien dólares, pero no pestañea ante gastos grandes y necesarios. Esto es bastante común. Podrías encontrar a una persona rica pasando de veinte a treinta minutos investigando la mejor batería portátil de veinte dólares en Amazon. Leen reseñas, se clavan viendo videos de YouTube, pero de repente, los verás soltar quince mil dólares en un techo nuevo sin pestañear. La diferencia es que la compra de la batería portátil es un gusto, mientras que el techo era una necesidad. Probablemente hay quince opciones diferentes de baterías portátiles que hacen lo mismo, así que la persona rica se toma su tiempo para encontrar el mejor valor por su dinero. El reemplazo del techo de quince mil dólares, por otro lado, es una necesidad, y especialmente si el reemplazo es urgente, simplemente gastarán el dinero para acelerar esa reparación. Personalmente, creo que esto es más un hábito arraigado de alguien que se hizo rico por su propio esfuerzo. Este pequeño hábito de no gastar de más en compras pequeñas a lo largo del año puede sumar un gran ahorro en el transcurso de trescientos sesenta y cinco días. Sin embargo, la gente rica entiende que los grandes gastos necesarios ocurrirán, y cuando esas cosas surgen, tienen que actuar rápida y decisivamente. La señal número seis de que alguien es rico en secreto: ha dominado la espera. Mientras los demás compran cosas al instante, la gente rica investigará una compra por meses, esperará la oferta perfecta o, a veces, decidirá que no necesita ese artículo. También entienden que la mayoría de las ofertas no son reales. Y si esperas lo suficiente, casi todo se pone en liquidación. Si te pones a pensar en lo que es una oferta, es literalmente un descuento inventado basado en un número inventado. ¿Y quién establece ese número? Generalmente es el proveedor o el vendedor de dicho artículo. Una persona rica en secreto podría ser muy frugal y orientada al valor, no le importará esperar, especialmente si eso significa obtener algo que realmente ama, en lugar de algo con lo que solo está conforme. Entonces, una buena lección aquí es que si estás pensando en una compra grande, esperes al menos de siete a treinta días, realmente tienes que hacer una pausa y preguntarte si de verdad seguirás queriendo ese artículo, digamos, dentro de un mes. Y quizá podrías pensar en artículos sustitutos que cumplan el mismo propósito y que no te cuesten un dineral. Esto es solo gratificación postergada, el concepto clave para volverte muy, muy rico más adelante en la vida. A veces, debes renunciar a lo que quieres ahora por una vida financiera más sólida y un mejor futuro. La mayoría no puede postergar la gratificación ni veinticuatro horas, mucho menos cinco o diez años, una disciplina clave para la riqueza. Los ricos en secreto son muy pacientes, esperan no solo ofertas, sino también otras cosas importantes en la vida. La señal número siete: viven en una casa modesta, muy por debajo de su capacidad de pago. Mientras la mayoría se estira con hipotecas apenas pagables, los ricos en secreto hacen lo opuesto. Viven en casas que parecen casi demasiado pequeñas o modestas para alguien con su nivel de ingresos, o simplemente son dueños de su casa y nunca te darían a entender que viven sin hipoteca. Si te compras una casa grande y lujosa que apenas puedes pagar, no solo tendrás más metros cuadrados que decorar, sino que probablemente necesitarás limpiadores, jardineros, y si tienes alberca, un alberquero. Y una vez que tienes una casa grande, no querrás una más pequeña. Tendrás la expectativa de mantener las apariencias para vecinos, amigos y familiares, lo que implica gastar más dinero para mantener tu estilo de vida. Hay muchos niveles de riqueza. Si el objetivo es vivir tu vida más plena, serás más feliz, en mi opinión, si simplemente te mantienes callado. Compartí este tuit en un video anterior, pero creo que resume muy bien los diferentes niveles de riqueza. Brandon Turner (Twitter/X) dice que hay tres niveles de riqueza. Nivel uno: cortas tu césped porque no tienes dinero. Nivel dos: pagas a alguien porque tienes algo de dinero y valoras tu tiempo. Nivel tres: cortas tu césped porque, ya retirado, tienes tanta libertad y tiempo que quieres hacerlo tú mismo, por puro placer. Esto es muy acertado, pues la persona en el nivel tres es probablemente la más rica, disfrutando su vida y cortando su césped por el simple gusto de hacerlo. ¡Gracias por ver el video! Los veo en ese video, o en otro. Gracias por acompañarme. Paz.
