- The Secret Wealthy Family That Ruled America: The Guggenheims: Kind: captions Language: en If you've ever wondered about the surname attached to that iconic white spiral nestled along Central Park East, commanding Fifth Avenue's attention, you're witnessing just the cultural afterglow of what was once America's second richest family. You see, the Guggenheim name adorning museums across three continents represents the final transformation of industrial might into artistic prestige. The ultimate status upgrade engineered by a dynasty whose wealth once rivaled small nations. Their patriarch arrived in Philadelphia with empty pockets but boundless ambition, peddling embroidery doortodoor before reluctantly accepting two Colorado silver mines as payment for a debt. An accidental investment that launched America's most vertical rags to riches trajectory. Within a single generation, seven Guggenheim brothers controlled mining empires stretching from Alaska to Angola. Their combined fortune eclipsed only by Rockefeller himself. Their family meetings resembling miniature United Nations conferences focused on mineral domination. Their descendants proved equally ambitious, conquering Capitol Hill, securing aristocratic titles through strategic European marriages, and in one scandalous case, transforming into the original trust fund rebel who reportedly bedded a thousand lovers, rescued priceless art from Nazi clutches, and held court in a Venetian palazzo while floating down the Grand Canal in a private gondola. adorned with personal staff. In today's episode of Old Money Luxury, we reveal how the forgotten dynasty that once extracted wealth directly from the Earth's crust engineered perhaps history's most successful rebrand, trading mineral monopolies for cultural monuments that ensure their name will echo through museums long after their minds run dry. Forget your modern tech billionaires with their carbonneutral mansions and cultivated public personas. The Guggenheims played the wealth game at a level that would make today's Fortune 500 crowd look like amateurs holding a garage sale. By 1918, this extraordinary dynasty had amassed a fortune between $250 million and $300 million, securing their position as the second wealthiest family in America, according to Forbes. In today's currency, that's billions upon billions of dollars. The kind of wealth that turned their very surname into an international brand of cultural sophistication and architectural wonder. Their financial legacy continues to ripple through modern markets. with Guggenheim Partners managing over $300 billion in assets as of 2023. Another family vehicle, Guggenheim Investment Advisers, casually oversees approximately $50 billion in assets. While most families build wealth through generations of careful planning, the Guggenheims preferred the direct approach, controlling practically the entire global supply of precious metals. Their mining empire sprawled across continents like a metallic octopus. Tentacles reaching into gold mines in the Yukon, tin mines in Bolivia, diamonds in the Belgian Congo and Angola, and copper mines throughout Alaska, Utah, and Chile. The family's strategy involved absolute dominance. They gained control of American smelting and refining company after a bare knuckle struggle with Rockefellerbacked interests. By the end of the First World War, this mineral-hungry dynasty controlled more than 80% of the world's supply of silver, copper, and lead. A monopoly so complete it would make modern regulators reach for smelling salts and antitrust legislation. When swimming in industrial riches, one requires suitable accommodations. In the Guggenheim's case, four massive mansions on a single Long Island Gold Coast estate. Their property featured Hempstead House, a 50,000q ft tuda style monument stretching 225 ft long with 40 rooms and a 60-t tall entry foyer. The mansion existed as an architectural declaration of unlimited wealth. Inside this domestic cathedral sat a magnificent Witzer opus 445 theater organ with massive decorative pipes surrounded by authentic tuda and jackabian antiques imported from Europe. medieval tapestries, oriental carpets, and original masterpieces by Rembrandt, Van Djk, and Reuben's. Solomon Guggenheim established his own personal kingdom at Villaola, a magnificent residence with gardens so grand they rivaled European royal estates. The family's most lavish display of wealth, eventually morphed into cultural immortality. The Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum in New York City, established in 1937, houses his modern art collection in Frank Lidd Wright's iconic spiral building. A structure that resembles a pearlescent nautilus shell spiraling toward architectural heaven. Peggy Guggenheim meanwhile transformed her own Venetian palace, the 18th century Palazzo Vignet de Leone on the Grand Canal into an extraordinary museum showcasing her personal art collection, creating a cultural mecca where she lived surrounded by masterpieces. The titaniumclad Guggenheim Museum Bil Bao designed by Frank Gary turned an unremarkable Spanish industrial city into a global destination that architects and tourists alike speak of in reverent whispers. Beyond art, the Guggenheims fundamentally altered human potential through their foundations. The Daniel and Florence Guggenheim Foundation established in 1924 to advance the well-being of mankind throughout the world. supported Robert Godard's pioneering work on liquid propelled rockets. This crucial early funding literally launched humanity toward the stars, establishing numerous research centers focused on aerospace sciences and exploration. But to hind this metallic empire of silver and gold lurked a family saga that originated far from the drawing rooms of established wealth. The tale began with the desperate gamble of a Swiss immigrant who arrived in America armed only with raw ambition and a merchant's cunning. Qualities that would transform his seven sons into industrial warriors capable of bringing even the mighty Rockefellers to their knees. The name Guggenheim echoes through history with distinctly European resonance. a Jewish western Ashkanazic surname derived from place rather than profession. Linguists trace its origins to either Guggenheim in Alsace, France, or perhaps Jugenheim near Benzeheim in the German state of Hessa. Places whose final syllable Heim signifies homestead in old high German. Over generations, regional pronunciations transformed the name into various forms. Guggenheimimer, Guggenheim, and Guggenheim. Linguistic artifacts of a family's journey through time and space. Behind this name stood real people whose lives bore little resemblance to the opulence that would later define their American legacy. Maya Guggenheim entered the world on February 1st, 1828 in the small village of Langna Aralo, Switzerland. Born to Simon Ma Guggenheim and Chafelli Guggenheim. His childhood unfolded against a backdrop of profound poverty in a community where hunger stalked many households with Canton Argal authorities distributing soup several times weekly to prevent starvation. Tragedy struck Meer's life at age six when his mother died, an event that shattered the family's already precarious existence. Unable to care for his children alone, Simon watched as Meer and his five siblings were separated and placed with different foster families. A traumatic disbanding that left even Simon himself assigned a legal guardian. Despite these crushing circumstances, young Mia revealed the entrepreneurial instinct that would one day transform continents, selling various articles doortodoor throughout Langnau after school hours. The constraints that shaped Meer's world extended far beyond personal misfortune. From 1776 until 1866, Lang now and neighboring Endingan stood as the only places Jews were permitted to live in Switzerland, creating isolated enclaves where Jews comprised half of Endingan's population and one-third of Langna's. These restrictions manifested physically in Langna's architecture where many buildings featured two separate entrances, one for Jews, one for Christians, concrete symbols of societal division. Local authorities prohibited Jews from owning property and enforced strict segregation, ensuring Jews and Christians never lived under the same roof. The catalyst for the Guggenheim's American journey arrived unexpectedly through matters of the heart. Following his wife's death, Simon Guggenheim wished to marry Rachel While, a widow with five children from her previous marriage. Christian authorities swiftly forbade this union, claiming Simon was too poor to provide for such a large family. Though historian Roy Oppenheim suggests anti-Jewish sentiment likely motivated their decision. Faced with these insurmountable barriers, Simon, his fianceé Rachel, and 19-year-old Mia made the momentous decision to immigrate to America in 1847. Ironically, their journey toward unimaginable wealth began with the Swiss state itself funding their departure. As Swiss communities often supported the immigration of poor residents to reduce burdens on local resources, Maya Guggenheim stepped onto American soil, carrying nothing but ambition and the bitter lessons of European prejudice. The streets of the New World offered no immediate fortune for the immigrant family, merely the freedom to marry whom they chose and the right to fail or succeed on their own terms. Within these modest parameters of opportunity, Meer began building what would become a commercial empire. Yet the path from peddler to bureaucrat would demand a kind of ruthless commercial cunning that transformed not just a family's fortunes, but the very balance of global industrial might. The America that greeted Maya Guggenheim in 1847 offered no welcome mat for impoverished immigrants, merely the indifferent opportunity of open competition. The streets of Philadelphia provided no immediate relief from the poverty that had shadowed him across the Atlantic. During those early years, Mia traversed the rough coal towns of northeastern Pennsylvania as a peddler, carrying household goods from door to door, a familiar pattern to the boy who had once sold articles through the streets of Langno. Gradual success came through methodical expansion into manufacturing. First stove polish, then lie, then synthetic coffee, while simultaneously developing a wholesale business in household merchandise. Meer's commercial instincts proved remarkably adaptable, identifying opportunities invisible to others. His first significant breakthrough emerged through Swiss embroidery and lace, a venture leveraging his European connections. Recognizing America's appetite for these decorative luxuries, Maer dispatched two sons to Switzerland to establish factories, generating a modest fortune by importing and selling their craftsmanship throughout the United States. The strategy revealed Meer's fundamental business philosophy, identifying crossber opportunities and deploying family members as trusted left tenants. The transformation from merchant to industrial titan began almost accidentally in 1881 when Meer reluctantly accepted a halfinterest in two Colorado silver mines as payment for an outstanding debt. After investing an additional $20,000 to make the mines operational, money he initially considered wasted, they proved extraordinarily rich beyond anyone's expectations. This fortunate turn propelled Meer deeper into mining, but he quickly recognized the extraction business held a fatal flaw. Smelting companies controlled processing costs, eating into profit margins. Meer responded decisively, building his own smelter in Colorado in 1889, followed by another in Mexico in 1895 that strategically evaded tariff restrictions on Mexican ore. This vertical integration approach controlling both extraction and processing became the signature Guggenheim strategy across their rapidly expanding empire. By 1918, Forbes recognized the Guggenheims as America's second richest family. Their fortunes transformed within a single generation. Meer's crowning achievement arrived when the family's Guggenheim Exploration Company seized control of the American Smelting and Refining Company, a hundred million dollar corporation that consolidated much of the industry under their direction. Daniel Guggenheim, Meer's second son, born in 1856, emerged as the primary architect of the family's expanding influence. Sent to Switzerland at 17 after his father determined he would never succeed academically, Daniel spent 11 formative years in Europe before returning to America after the Colorado mines revealed their extraordinary potential. Under Daniel's leadership from 1890 to 1923, the family business expanded dramatically across continents. In 1888, the Guggenheims founded the Philadelphia Smelting and Refining Company, building their first plant in Pueblo, Colorado, an operation generating approximately $1 million annually in profit. The family relocated their headquarters to New York City, America's financial capital, in 1889. Daniel's crowning achievement arrived in 1901 with the merger creating the American smelting and refining company, consolidating unprecedented control over global metal production. In later years, Daniel transitioned toward philanthropy, establishing the Daniel and Florence Guggenheim Foundation in 1924. Between 1930 and 1941, the foundation notably financed Robert Godard's pioneering work on liquid fuel rockets, an investment in humanity's future that paralleled their exploitation of Earth's resources. The Guggenheims had scaled the heights of American capitalism with remarkable speed, transforming from street peddlers to industrial monarchs within decades. Yet, as the family's sons inherited their father's extraordinary wealth, they would each face a profound question unknown to Meer himself. What meaning could be found in fortune so vast that they could never be spent in a lifetime? And what purpose might justify the sacrifices made to extract such unprecedented riches from the unforgiving earth? The empire Daniel Guggenheim meticulously expanded faced its greatest challenges. not from external competition but through internal transformation as subsequent generations pursued divergent ambitions beyond mining and metallergy. Solomon Robert Guggenheim born February 2nd 1861 in Philadelphia initially adhered to established family patterns studying in Zurich partnering in the Swiss embroidery business and eventually establishing the Yukon Gold Company in Alaska. The Great War marked Solomon's personal watershed. In 1919, while still only 58, he retired entirely from business to pursue his naent interest in art collecting. A passion kindled by his wife Irene Rothschild that would ultimately redefine the family legacy. Solomon's artistic vision crystallized after meeting Baroness Hill vonbe in 1926, who steered his attention toward abstract or non-objective works that broke radically with traditional representation. A pivotal moment arrived in 1930 when Solomon and Rebe visited Wasili Kandinski's studio in Desau Germany. An encounter that launched Solomon's systematic acquisition of modernist masterpieces. His Plaza Hotel apartment in New York City became an impromptu gallery as his collection expanded to include works by Rudolph Bower, Mark Shagal, Fernan Leger, and Lasslo Maholinagi. In 1937, Solomon established the Solomon R. Guggenheim Foundation, followed two years later by the Museum of Non-objective Painting on East 54th Street, commissioning Franklidd Wright in 1943 to design a permanent museum. Solomon initiated architectural revolution alongside artistic patronage, though death claimed him in 1949 before witnessing Wright's completed spiral masterpiece. While Solomon redirected family capital toward cultural influence, his nephew Harry F. Guggenheim ventured into media, founding News Day on September 3rd, 1940. Initially dismissed as merely an occupation to keep his wife Alicia Patterson out of trouble, the newspaper flourished under her leadership as Long Island's population exploded after World War II. The paper's trajectory revealed inherent tensions between the conservative Guggenheim values and evolving American politics. After Patterson's death in 1963, Harry hired Bill Moyers as publisher, but their political differences proved insurmountable. Moyá's liberal leanings and opposition to the Vietnam War created unbridgegable divisions, eventually forcing his departure in 1970. Harry finally relinquished control, selling news day to the Times Mirror Company in 1971. John Simon Guggenheim, born December 30th, 1867, pursued yet another divergent path through electoral politics. After working as chief or buyer at MG Guggenheim's sons in Pueblo, Colorado, John launched a gubanatorial campaign in 1898 that ended dramatically when riots erupted at the Republican state convention, leaving one man dead and several injured. John's political ambitions ultimately found fulfillment in 1907 when Colorado elected him to the United States Senate where he served until 1913. During his congressional tenure, personal tragedy struck when his older brother Benjamin perished aboard the RMS Titanic in 1912, a public catastrophe that brought family grief into national headlines. After his Senate term, John returned to New York City, eventually becoming chairman of American Smelting and Refining Company and serving as its president from 1919 to 1941. The family later expanded into diplomatic circles when Msie Robert Guggenheim briefly served as ambassador to Portugal from 1953 to 1954. These divergent paths illustrated how the consolidated industrial authority Meer had established fractured across subsequent generations. While Solomon sought immortality through art, Harry through media, and Jon through politics, it would fall to an unexpected figure from the family's third generation to create the most enduring and controversial Guggenheim legacy of all through acts of both extraordinary courage and unapologetic rebellion against every convention her conservative relatives held dear. While the male heirs of the Guggenheim dynasty pursued divergent corporate, political, and cultural ambitions, their female counterparts often found themselves navigating the complex intersection of extraordinary wealth and societal expectations. Peggy Guggenheim emerged as perhaps the most consequential female figure in the family's history, rejecting conventional restraint for Bohemian liberation that scandalized her conservative relatives. Born Margarite Guggenheim in 1928 to Benjamin Guggenheim, who would later perish aboard the Titanic, she inherited both tremendous wealth and the burden of her family's industrial legacy without inheriting their commercial instincts. Known for wild parties in 1920s Paris, she developed a reputation as sexually liberated and artobsessed, often referred to as the female Kasanova, and rumored to have had a thousand lovers, including Samuel Beckett, with whom she reportedly experienced a memorable education about modern art during a prolonged day and night in bed. Unlike her uncle Solomon, who collected established artists, Peggy demonstrated remarkable precience in identifying emerging talent. She gave Lucian Freud his first London show and discovered Jackson Pollock when he worked merely as a carpenter at Solomon's Museum, providing him with a monthly $300 stipend and a loan to purchase a Long Island house away from New York City's Temptations. Her astonishing art collection eventually included masterpieces by Salvador Dali, Pablo Picasso, and Joan Mero. Works recognized today as cornerstones of 20th century artistic development. Where the Guggenheim patriarchs had built fortunes by extracting precious metals from the earth, Peggy mined the cultural landscape for treasures that would ultimately prove more enduring than silver or gold. Peggy's life featured periods of geographical and emotional transitions that shaped her collecting vision. Between 1934 and 1939, she resided in Sussex, England, in the village of South Harting, which she described as absolutely dead, like all such places in England, but it was in the midst of the most lovely country. Naturally, it had a fine pub. This brief sojourn in the English countryside reportedly ended her fantasy of Bohemian domesticity and inspired her transformation into the formidable art collector history now remembers. Her courage proved as remarkable as her artistic judgment. In 1940, as Nazi forces invaded Europe, Peggy remained in Paris, acquiring artworks despite her Jewish heritage, an extraordinarily dangerous position. Just before Hitler's soldiers marched into Paris, she had her collection smuggled to New York, hidden amongst bedclo dishes, works that would later form the core of her celebrated Venice collection. While her relatives had once battled Rockefeller interests for control of American smelting and refining company, Peggy fought an entirely different battle. Rescuing modernist masterpieces from the destructive path of fascism, preserving cultural treasures that might otherwise have been destroyed in Hitler's campaign against degenerate art. The personal costs of the Guggenheim legacy manifested tragically through Peagan Vale Guggenheim, Peggy's daughter, born August 18th, 1925. Despite developing her own artistic career combining surrealism and naive art, Peaggan struggled with depression throughout her life and died in Paris on March 1st, 1987 at age 41 after overdosing on medication, representing the darker psychological toll that sometimes accompanied the family's extraordinary wealth and artistic sensibilities. Elellanena May Guggenheim, Solomon's daughter, born in 1896, connected American industrial fortune to European aristocracy through her marriage to Arthur Stewart, 7th Earl Castle Stewart on December 16th, 1920. This union represented a common pattern. Wealthy American industrial families acquiring social prestige through European titles while providing financial support to maintain aristocratic estates and lifestyles. Tragedy shadowed Elellanena's life when her two eldest sons were killed in action during World War II, followed by her husband's suicide with a shotgun in his study at their estate on November 5th, 1961. Beyond individual lives, the family's most enduring scientific contribution came through the Daniel and Florence Guggenheim Foundation established in 1924. The foundation financed Robert H. Godard's pioneering work on liquid fuel rockets between 1930 and 1941. Research so significant that the United States government later settled a patent lawsuit for infringing on Goddard's designs. These investments in aerospace research paralleled the family's artistic patronage, demonstrating their wide-ranging influence across multiple fields of human endeavor. After 87 years advancing scientific knowledge, the foundation officially terminated on June 30th, 2011 with some functions transferred to a program on demography, technology, and criminal justice. The Guggenheim name today adorns museums across multiple continents, foundations supporting diverse causes, and buildings throughout major universities. Physical manifestations of wealth transformed into cultural capital. Standing inside the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum on Fifth Avenue, where Frank Lloyd Wright's spiral ramp elevates visitors toward the light-filled Oculus above, it becomes impossible to separate the artistic treasures within from the industrial fortunes that made them possible. The same family whose mining operations once employed children in dangerous conditions throughout Mexico and South America now funds educational programs teaching millions of children about art and creative expression each year. This profound transformation from extractors of the earth's riches to preservers of humanity's cultural treasures represents perhaps the most successful act of redemption in American industrial history, raising the question of whether great beauty can ultimately transcend the sometimes painful means of its creation. And now we'd love to see you in the comments. Were you familiar with the Guggenheim family's insane history before this video? Our channel thrives off of your stories, so tell us down below. With that said, thanks for joining us for another episode of Old Money Luxury. And cheers until the next time.

- Inside the 10 Richest Families in New York: Kind: captions Language: en What if I told you there's a family so wealthy that they practically own New York City? Imagine looking up at the skyline and knowing that the buildings, the landmarks, even some of the most iconic spots are all controlled by just a few powerful names. From billiondoll empires and real estate to controlling the city's cultural landmarks, these families have shaped the city in ways you can't even imagine. Stay tuned as we uncover the untold story of the billionaires behind the empire that owns all of New York. The Aster family, $20 million, 1830s. Let's kick things off with a family whose wealth helped lay the foundation for New York City's real estate empire, the Aers. John Jacob Aster, a German immigrant, arrived in New York in the late 1700s with big dreams of success. Starting out in the fur trade, he quickly found a way to turn his fortune into something that would change New York forever. Real estate. Aster wasn't buying land randomly. He had a keen eye for spots that would one day become prime real estate. He invested in areas like Time Square and the Upper West Side, both of which would eventually become some of the most valuable neighborhoods in New York. His strategy was a visionary one, and it paid off handsomely. When Aster passed away in 1848, he was one of the wealthiest men in America, having amassed a fortune worth an estimated $20 million, an enormous sum in the 1800s. His real estate strategy was simple but brilliant. Buy land in areas that would rise in value as the city developed. And it worked. Aster's descendants, including his son, William Backhouse Aster, continued this legacy by expanding the family's property holdings and shaping neighborhoods throughout Manhattan. Even today, the Asterame remains anonymous with New York's elite real estate. Their early investments created a blueprint for future billionaires to follow. But let's not forget, while they built wealth, they also gave back to New York. John Jacob Aster 3, for example, donated funds to Metropolitan Museum of Art, ensuring that the city would benefit from his wealth for generations. The Aster family didn't just create real estate empires, they helped shape New York's cultural and architectural history. The Vanderbilt family, $15 million, 1800s. From the fur trade to railroads, the next family we're talking about took a different approach to building their empire. The Vanderbilts, led by Cornelius Vanderbilt, started with a small ferry business, but with ambition and hard work. Cornelius grew his wealth by investing in steamboats. His business grew rapidly, earning him the nickname Commodore. But it wasn't until the 1860s when Cornelius shifted his focus to railroads that his fortune began to grow. Cornelius invested heavily in railroads, buying up several lines and creating the New York Central Railroad. This move made him one of the wealthiest men in America and helped shape New York into a hub for business and transportation. With this massive wealth came a desire to invest in real estate, particularly in Manhattan. One of the family's most famous contributions was a construction of Grand Central Depot in 1871, which would eventually become the iconic Grand Central Terminal. The terminal became a symbol of New York's energy, ambition, and endless possibilities. Its design and central location helped solidify Manhattan's role as the busiest, most vibrant part of New York City. The Vanderbilt family's impact on New York wasn't limited to railroads and stations. They also made significant investments in luxury homes along Fifth Avenue, building grand mansions that became landmarks in their own right. The Vanderbilt mansion at 665. Fifth Avenue became an architectural marvel, setting a new standard for opulence in the city. These homes with their large rooms, intricate designs, and fancy details show the world what wealth and power truly looked like. Grand Central Terminal remains a heart of New York, a reminder the vision and ambition of the Vanderbilt family, the Rockefeller family. $400 billion 1900s. When you think of New York's iconic skyline, you can't miss the Rockefeller Center. But how did this enormous complex come to life? The answer lies with John D. Rockefeller and his family. Rockefeller made his fortune in oil, co-founding Standard Oil in 1870. As one of the wealthiest men in America, he turned his focus to New York City, building the now famous Rockefeller Center in the 1930s. Rockefeller Center covers 22 acres in Midtown Manhattan, and it's home to landmarks like Radio City Music Hall and the Ice Skating Rink. It also hosts the annual Christmas tree lighting, a tradition that brings thousands of visitors to New York every year. The Christmas tree, along with the ice rink, has become one of the most beloved symbols of New York spirit. Beyond that, the center itself became a catalyst of the city's growth, attracting tourists, businesses, and culture from all around the world. But the Rockefellers didn't just build buildings, they built culture. They were major supporters of the arts, helping to establish a museum of modern art, MoMA, and funding the Closters, a branch to the Metropolitan Museum of Art that focuses on medieval art. The family's contributions to the arts helped make New York a cultural capital, a place where the world's best art could be seen and experienced. Thanks to their investments in places like Rockefeller Center and their support for museums, the Rockefellers shaped New York into a worldclass city of culture and commerce. The Lauder family, $4.5 billion, 1940s. Now, let's talk about a family that built their empire in a completely different industry. Beauty. Estee Lauder, born Josephine Esther Menor, started out with a simple dream, creating skincare products. Her family's humble beginnings didn't stop her from making it big. In 1946, she and her husband, Joseph Lauder, founded Estee Lauder Cosmetics, which grew into a global beauty brand. Estee's marketing genius helped the company soar. By 1948, she secured her first apartment store account with Saks Fth Avenue in New York. As the company expanded, so did their presence in the city's real estate market. Leonard Lauder Estay's son continued the family legacy and his influence helped transform New York's real estate scene. He along with his mother invested in properties that reflected the luxury and elegance the family was known for. Leonard's leadership further cemented the family's place in New York's elite circles. Today, the Lauder family is deeply involved in real estate and luxury living. They're known for their connection to 740 Park Avenue, one of New York's most prestigious addresses. Living in this building is not just about the address. It's about being part of a community where luxury and tradition meet. Leonard's impact isn't just seen in the family business. He's also a major supporter of New York's art scene. In 2013, he donated a collection of Cubis artworks valued at over $1 billion to the Metropolitan Museum of Art. This gift cemented his place as one of the city's most influential philanthropists. The Goldman family, $400 million 1960s. Another family that has left an undeniable mark on New York's real estate scene, is a Goldman family. Saul Goldman, born in Brooklyn in 1917, started with humble beginnings. His family was made up of Jewish immigrants from Poland. And Saul's early life wasn't easy. However, he saw an opportunity in the real estate market during the Great Depression. He started buying up foreclosed properties and raised funds from local shopkeepers. By the 1950s, Saul partnered with Alex Delorenzo Jr., and together they began acquiring some of New York's most sought-after properties, including the Chrysler building in 1960. Their strategy was simple. Buy undervalued properties and hold them for long-term appreciation. Saul's approach paid off and the Goldman family became one of the most prominent real estate investors in New York City. Their family company, Solo Management, currently manages over 400 properties, many in prime Manhattan locations. Today, Saul's legacy lives on through Solo Management, which continues to thrive in New York's competitive real estate market. Led by Saul's daughter, Jane Goldman, the family remains a powerful force in real estate. Jane along with her siblings plays an active role in managing the company and ensuring that their properties remain top tier in the city's competitive landscape. Jane's careful approach to business and real estate ensures that the Goldman family's legacy continues to shape New York's future. From fur trading to oil, beauty to real estate, these families have played an integral role in shaping New York City. They didn't just build wealth, they transformed the city into a global powerhouse. Whether through massive real estate holdings, iconic landmarks, or cultural contributions, the influence of these billionaire families continues to be felt today. As you look around New York, you're seeing the lasting legacy of these dynasties. The families that survive money intact are not the ones where no one asks for anything. Asking happens in every [music] family. Money is asked about, borrowed, given, withheld, discussed, [music] and not discussed at every income level. The difference is not whether the asking happens. The difference is whether the people involved can see the dynamic clearly enough to name it [music] and whether the relationship is strong enough to survive the naming. They are the ones where everyone [music] can see the dynamics clearly enough to have honest conversations about them. Clarity is not easy. It is better than the alternative. The alternative is everyone performing a version of the relationship while the real version operates underneath, unspoken, year after year, until someone says something at a holiday dinner that was really about something that happened eight years ago and nobody knew how to name. You have probably already been in that You may be in one soon. Now you know what it is actually about. Knowing does not make it easier. It makes it navigable, which is the [music] most honest thing that can be said about money and family at any level of wealth, from zero to 100 million and every number in between.

maneuvering his simple fery service expanded its reach during the tumultuous times of the War of 1812 serving the far-flung government outposts strewn around New York City as peace was restored Vanderbilt shifted his sights to the steamship industry aligning himself with the Strategic Acumen of Thomas Gibbons possessing an innate radar for identifying opportunities within fledgling markets Vanderbilt seized control of the steamboat and nent railroad Industries effectively transforming the American landscape in particular his strategic Mastery lay in his ability to grow markets uncompromising consequences for dishonesty or idleness and even better this agile Titan was no stranger to Industry Evolution his adaptability became evident when he smoothly transitioned from steamboats to railroads not merely content with business savvy Vanderbilt was also a ruthless competitor Infamous for his aggressive tactics in obliterating competition and with regard to his temperament as a man of few words Vanderbilt was esteemed for his impeccable Jud ment of character he often remained stoic in the face of criticism steadfast in his decisions his unyielding determination and adaptability this drive allowed him to amass a formidable Fortune crowning him as one of the wealthiest Americans of the 19th century in fact his lifestyle could be described as a blockhead and blatherskite William yearned to prove otherwise but lacked the courage to face the commodore's censure however a turning point arrived during a family Voyage to Europe aboard the one to a question about whether the trains were running for public convenience William retorted the public be damned this Outburst reflected the growing Detachment of the Vanderbilt lineage from the principles of their patriarch upon the demise of William Henry Vanderbilt the family's Financial Leviathan passed into the hands of his sons Cornelius Vanderbilt II and William kissum Vanderbilt this heralded a new era in the diny's narrative one marked by an increasingly convoluted distribution of wealth the vast Vanderbilt Fortune once a concentrated reservoir of wealth began to spread thin across an Ever growing family tree with each passing Generation The Inheritance split among a growing number of descendants as family branches multiplied the division of assets exacerbated gradually diluting the individual shares of the once enormous family wealth simultaneously the formidable grasp of inheritance taxes began to gore at the edges of the family's Fortune as one generation passed and handed down their wealth to the next each trans was met with sizable tax implications over time these seemingly harmless nibbles transformed into significant bites out of the family coffers the shadow of such Financial burdens grew longer with each subsequent generation steadily eroding the formidable Vanderbilt Fortune worse still the family was plagued by a series of Reckless Financial choices much of the Vanderbuilt progeny far removed from the industrious ethos of their forebear failed to demonstrate the same business Acumen and financial prudence that Cornelius Vanderbilt had art collections such unrestrained spending only served to accelerate the decline of the once mighty Vanderbilt Fortune this combination of expanding family size punishing inheritance taxes imprudent financial decisions and unabated spending cast a forboding shadow over the Vanderbilt Dynasty the fortune once a Titan in the annals of American Wealth gradually began its descent into Oblivion by the mid 20th century the Vanderbilt Star had considerably dimmed in the American public sphere as evidenced by the fate of their grand Fifth Avenue Mansions many of which were sold de demolished or repurposed as museums a mere 30 years post Cornelius Vanderbilt's death not a single Vanderbilt was listed among the wealthiest Americans tragically within half a century of the commodore's passing the vast Vanderbilt Fortune had entirely evaporated now let us delve deeper into the distinctive factors that precipitated the decline of the Vanderbilt wealth and juxtapose it with a family that despite amassing wealth during the same era managed to sustain it through generations to understand the contrasts let's first consider a hypothetical scenario imagine a dominant figure at the helm of a multi-billion dollar family Empire suddenly succumbing to illness the CEO who doesn't belong to the family has handpicked a COO as his successor yet the board remains divided in its support the company's Financial Health is waning prompting board anxieties without the board's unanimous backing exploring alternative CEO candidates becomes imperative further convoluting matters is the greenhorn board chair a family member with another family member and controlling shareholder expecting this chair to ascend as the next CEO this scenario may seem unusual but in the world of family-owned businesses finding a successor is a complex Labyrinth the Vanderbilt family story mirrors this scenario the dispersion of their Fortune during the third generation corresponded with diminishing family interest in their key asset the New York Central coupled with escalating expenses the Vanderbilts were known for their philanthropy especially William kissum Vanderbilt of the third generation who donated Millions to numerous causes yet the dawn of income and estate taxes and the family's incessant spending hastened their wealth's decline during the initial stages of their wealth accumulation there was a minimal business regulatory framework allowing them a free hand in dominating entire Industries the era they thrived in was marked by an absence of income and estate taxes which meant that the Vanderbilts were able to amass an unimaginable Fortune free of governmental restrictions or financial drains however as societal norms and governmental policies evolved the Vanderbilts found themselves facing new challenges this era also saw an increased regulatory focus on monopolies and business practices adding another layer of complexity to maintaining and growing their Fortune further exacerbating this situation was the Great Depression which waked havoc on the American economy it affected the Vanderbilt's Investments and economic interests leading to a sign ific downturn in their fortunes the Rockefeller family conversely employed shrewd strategies to safeguard their wealth John D rockefeller's business via generation skipping trusts ensured wealth propagation through the generations a generation skipping trust allows the transfer of substantial amounts of money tax-free skipping a generation usually passing from the gr to the grandchildren rather than the children in the rockefeller's case they managed to retain and multiply their wealth by channeling it down to more distant Generations rather than Distributing it among immediate successors the Vanderbilts however after a few Generations lacked an established Core Business that could continuously feed wealth into their trust their approach primarily involved the passing of assets directly from parent to child in each generation this strategy not only exposed their wealth to the successive imposition of estate taxes with each generational transfer but also made their Fortune vulnerable to the lavish spending habits of each inheritor the Rockefellers by structuring their wealth transfer through a generation skipping trust managed to avoid these pitfalls and ensure the long-term preservation of their fam's wealth and what else set the Rockefellers apart David Rockefeller Jr chairman of Rockefeller and Company in a rare interview spoke of a system of values traditions and institutions developed by the family these mechanisms aided the family in staying United and preserving their wealth serving as practical tools for any family trying to instill sound monetary values in their children remarkably the Rockefeller family largely remained unified devoid of public scandals feuds lawsuits or tragedies that typically plague wealthy dynasties today over 250 direct descendants of John D Rockefeller and Laura Spelman Rockefeller formed the family we meet as a family twice a year often more than 100 of us in a room for a Christmas lunch for example said David Rockefeller from the age of 21 family members are invited to these gatherings aptly called The Family Forum here they discuss the family's Direction new members career news and other significant Milestones they ensure everyone feels part of the family including those who married into it preserving the family history is also deemed essential The Rockefellers do this through their familial homesteads places passed down over Generations where they can reconnect with their past I can go back to the place where my great-grandfather lived over 100 years years ago and see how he lived and see how his son and their grandchildren lived added David Rockefeller such practices exemplify the different approaches two similarly wealthy families took to wealth preservation the Vanderbilt Legacy today is a fascinating tale of resilience adaptation and Lessons Learned From the rise and fall of one of America's wealthiest families despite the family's difficulties and the decline of their once vast Fortune some members of of the Vanderbilt family have managed to create their own paths and leave Lasting Impressions on society Gloria Vanderbilt a descendant of the Vanderbilt family faced a difficult life marked by personal tragedies and challenges born in 1924 she was the daughter of Reginald Claypool Vanderbilt and Gloria Morgan Vanderbilt at a young age Gloria was thrust into the spotlight during a highly publicized custody battle between her mother and her paternal Aunt Gertrude Vanderbilt Whitney despite these early hardships Gloria went on to become a highly respected and successful artist fashion designer and author her resilience and determination to forge her own path endeared her to the public and made her a beloved figure throughout her life Gloria Vanderbilt was not only a colorful but also a very successful personality who made many contributions to the society in which she lived and thrived she was an exceptional vivacious Fashion Icon renowned for her distinctive style and the widely successful Vanderbilt's brand in a fascinating aside one of our followers at Old Money luxury shared a personal interaction with Gloria Vanderbilt recalling I met Gloria Vanderbilt at her New York art show in 2003 she was elegant well-bred polite soft spoken and greeted me with a big smile on her face her hair makeup clothes were impeccable and understated her teeth and complexion were perfect I'm so glad I met her I've read her books she lived appropriately she was an iconic figure who needed protection from Devious people people at her level of wealth she had friendships with designers actors singers lawyers professors business people with education and talent she loved her sons deeply Gloria Sons Anderson Cooper and Carter Vanderbilt Cooper also faced their share of challenges growing up as Vanderbilt descendants Anderson Cooper a well-known journalist and television personality has spoken openly about the impact of his family's history on his life and career He has expressed gratitude for the opportunities his family's Legacy has afforded him while also acknowledging the importance of hard work and self-reliance tragically Carter Vanderbilt Cooper passed by his own hand at the age of 23 a devastating loss for the family The Current financial situation of the Vanderbilt descendants varies as the once enormous family fortune has been significantly diminished over the years many of the family's descendants have pursued careers in various fields from journalism to fashion and have built their own wealth and success independent of the family's Legacy the family's remaining assets and lingering influence can still be seen in the form of their historic Mansions such as the builtmore Estate in Asheville North Carolina and The Breakers in Newport Rhode Island these properties serve as reminders of the family's immense wealth and influence during the Gilded Age and continue to attract visitors from around the world the rise and fall of the Vanderbilts offers valuable lessons for future Generations the importance of responsible wealth management strategic Estate Planning and the potential pitfalls of excessive spending and poor investment decisions are all evident in the family's story Additionally the Vanderbilt Legacy serves as a reminder of the importance of resilience and adaptability in the face of adversity wealth in the early 19th century the 13 British colonies and later the fledgling United States were already the domain of established families of considerable means for Generations these families had held sway over the nation's political landscape and their Roots often stretched back to the period before the American Revolution you see the forbears of these families accumulated their fortunes through a myriad of avenues among them Elite Planters prosperous Merchants slave owners ship Builders additionally geography played its role in states such as Virginia Maryland and the Carolinas Acres upon acres of land either bestowed by British royalty or gained through colonial era so-called head rights formed the Bedrock of these families prosperity and their legacy was often not merely one of wealth but of statesmanship indeed they counted among their numbers several of the founding fathers and the early presidents of the United States men who would stand in The Crucible of Revolution and constitutional formation yet with all of that said the American colonies had been largely peopled by Those whom historians describe as the middling sort what we today would identif identify as the middle class certainly America was not a land of hereditary aristocracy rather it was a place where wealth alone could Elevate one standing and the founding fathers themselves were instrumental in rejecting monarchy and hereditary aristocracy as they structured the nation's governance for this their intellectual Inspirations were varied first they took cues from Europe's first written Constitution the Magna Carta celebrating its challenge to monarchical Authority men like the future US president Thomas Jefferson saw the new American Elite as a natural aristocracy one founded not on Birthright but on Merit and ability for example Alexander Hamilton born out of wedlock and hailing from humble beginnings in the Caribbean or Benjamin Franklin a son of a candlemaker could Ascend to the Pinnacle of American society through sheer grit and intellect by contrast Germany the ancestral home of the Asters was a veritable Mosaic of principalities and kingdoms each underpinned by its own rigid aristocratic structure indeed the Asters could never have ascended the social ladder in their German Homeland as they did in America's more fluid Society thus as the early 1800s unfurled America was a land of disperate centers of wealth and influence both new money and old money mingled yet there was no centralized High Society therefore America was at the time a vacuum that would soon draw in the likes of the entrepreneur IAL asers now in a nation still forming its identity John Jacob Aster emerged as an archetype of American capitalism born in Germany he crossed the Atlantic to establish himself as a fur Trader and soon became America's first multi-millionaire specifically Aster's arrival in New York in 1786 marked the dawn of his business Endeavors acquiring knowledge of the fur trade during his voyage to the new world he opened a fur good Shop in Manhattan and Aster was not one to squander an opportunity the J Treaty of 1794 which opened new Canadian markets and the Great Lakes region proved fortuitous for him his shrewd negotiations with native tribes and Market insights allowed him to accumulate a quarter of a million dollars by the year 1800 effectively crowning him the Undisputed magnate of the fur trade but John Jacob Aster's Ventures were not confined to Furs alone in 1834 before the aster House New York City's first luxury hotel welcomed its first guests Aster had meticulously gathered Parcels of land around his previous residence effectively claiming an entire city block in what was then the city's most elegant quarter situated on the west side of Broadway the aster house was a Stones throwaway from City Hall Park and the offices of the New York Herald for decades this establishment remained the load star for the elite and the famous the venue where authors and Statesmen mingled in opulent settings indeed it served as the blueprint for the city's future luxurious accommodations now the patriarch of the aster Dynasty passed away in 1848 leaving a staggering $20 million equal to over 770 million doar in today's currency almost all of which was bequeathed to his son William backous Aster thus the provisions of Aster's will were carefully engineered to preserve the family fortune for the generations to follow a symbol of his C ulating Mind by the 1850s the ASA family had transcended their Mercantile Origins to become social Arbiters of the first order and it was Caroline sherah horn Aster Williams wife who most personified this new societal influence the famed matriarch wielded almost dictatorial control over New York high society and it was her annual Aster ball that became the defining event of the city's social calendar now during the 1870s Caroline Aster commonly known as the Mrs Aster wielded significant influence over American High Society her Fifth Avenue Mansion became the stage for defining the era's social etiquet where she hosted elaborate tees receptions and opulent late night dinners the Pinnacle of her social calendar was an annual ball set meticulously on a Monday night in January where dinner was served at 11:00 p.m. and dancing continued until the break of day Mrs Aster would commonly be flanked by Ward Mall her Confidant and co-architect of social exclusivity together they orchestrated the Society of the Patriarchs a club aimed at consolidating the creme de La Creme of New York Society now the patriarch's balls were recurring events held multiple times each season at Del mono's the city's Premier dining establishment membership in the society came with certain privileges each member could extend invitations to five women and four men for the various social events conceptualized by by Caroline Aster those who met the society's stringent standards were granted entry into the 400 a term coined to represent the elite list of guests who could comfortably fit in Mrs Aster's Ballroom this exclusive list included individuals of impeccable lineage grandchildren of past presidents European royalty and heirs to Monumental fortunes indeed the 400 became synonymous with social exclusivity setting the tone for who was considered in and out of the upper echelons of American society meanwhile in 1887 leis Keller a society columnist and golf offici enardo published the first social register taking a cue from Mrs Aster's famous 400 list his compilation also included names from other notable lists effectively documenting those who made up New York's most distinguished visiting registers overseen by an anonymous advisory committee the social register initially recorded over 5,000 individuals many of whom were descendants of early American settlers historically the list catered to American upper class families epitomizing the WASP stereotype amid this backdrop of social curating tensions flared between the asers and the Vanderbilts the two Titan families of the Gilded Age Alva Vanderbilt slighted for her absence on Mrs Aster's coveted list orchestrated an audacious counter move in 1883 after the completion of pety Chateau her extravagant Fifth Avenue residence Alva dispatched 1,200 invitations for her masked ball hearing all the society chatter Mrs Aster's daughter eager to attend the Vanderbilt Gala thus persuaded her mother to pay a visit to Alva armed with a useful excuse to finally acknowledge the Vanderbilt's entry into High Society Mrs Aster joined in Alva's festivities marking a twisted tale of social rivalry in a setting already steeped in exclusivity and one-upmanship Now The ased High Society annual social cycle of Gilded Age New York commenced in November and extended until the onset of Lent during spring society's finest would frequent the courts of England and Europe only to return for a Stateside summer this recurring sequence of events including balls and dinner parties was not just a social fixture but also held political relevance as many members of the US parliament partook in these social rights subsequently Caroline Aon partnered with her Confidant Ward McAllister aimed to formalize the rules governing this rarified social landscape they were the self-appointed Guardians of old money propriety laboring to sift the worthy from the Unworthy newcomers however simultaneously as the American AA family's influence was reaching its apery in the United States an ocean away the family's tendrils were extending into British Society in 1893 William wal Aster disenchanted by familial Discord on American soil took up residence in England and the estate of clivedon in taplo Waldorf became the unionist candidate for Plymouth in 1908 Nancy ever the devoted spouse campaigned ardently at his side until he took his seat in Parliament 2 years later and and British Society an unforeseen twist of fate Waldorf was elevated to the House of Lords necessitating his departure from the House of Commons into the vacuum stepped Nancy who won his seat and made history as the first woman to sit in Parliament over a span of 26 years she wielded her influence in the advocacy of diverse causes shortened working hours enhanced health care for mothers pensions for widows and equal employment opportunities for women but Virginia always occupied a special place in her heart a portrait believed to depict pocah hontas was her gift to the Commonwealth in 1926 however despite her groundbreaking career Nancy encountered formidable resistance including from none other than Winston Churchill yet her tenure in Parliament set a precedent that reverberated through the halls of power unsettling traditional gender norms and shaping a new pathway for women in politics simultaneously her wealth social standing and vast network of connections became essential Assets in her political Endeavors therefore while the family maintained a formidable presence in American High Society the British chapter of the aster Legacy was inscribing itself throughout the 1920s and 1930s Jacob Aster IV the most affluent passenger aboard his wife Matalin Force Aster survived the tragedy and subsequently gave birth to their son John Jacob Aster the 6 and meline herself was a figure of social Intrigue her marriage to John Jacob Aster IV having provoked Scandal due to their age difference and his recent divorce when several clergy members refused to officiate their wedding the minister who eventually stepped in faced such backlash that he felt compelled to resign decades later as World War II erupted the aster influence across the Atlantic saw another significant various forms of Labor yet it did little to modernize their post-war Ambitions which largely remained anchored in Tradition in the next decade the 1950s the divestment of American assets signified property it laid vacant until its acquisition by Bernard's Township in 1968 for $140,000 furthermore the Wayne of the aster influence during the latter half of the 20th century symbolized a broader societal shift the concept of high society once the near exclusive realm of white Anglo-Saxon Protestants began to diversify making room for emergent families like the Irish Catholic kennedies yet the family's English descendants including vicount Aster and baron Aster of hea Cleveland Ohio on the BR of the 19th century a collage of Hardy Souls undaunted dreams and fierce determination it's here treading through the Thunder of Steel on Rail and the Symphony of Industry piercing the crisp Midwestern sky where our Story begins we meet a young John D Rockefeller a man not born great but upon whom greatness would be bestowed by the trials of Life Young John's eyes reflect an old soul a wisdom gifted by his devout mother Eliza Davidson a pillar of steadfast religious Faith she has imparted in her son the invaluable lessons of Charity frugality and financial stewardship in the muted glow of Candlelight johon Faithfully absorbed these lessons his handwritten Ledger of daily expenditures a constant companion however even as he wrote his father's Shadow filled the room his father William Avery Rockefeller a man with a chameleon-like charm and a penchant for high stakes pedal his Miracle potions blending a mix of naive hope and snake oil he sold dreams to the desperate and unsuspecting this tension an intricate Waltz between integrity and deception forms the foundation of the Rockefeller family's Destiny each moment adds a new layer of resolve the quiet diligence from a mother's teachings and the ruthless business cunning kindled by a father's Gile Embark with us on this journey from the freshly minted wealth of the new money Rockefellers to the prestigious old money Dynasty John would build as we describe how the Rockefeller family went from new money to Old Money our Saga Begins in New York state where our pivotal protagonist John D Rockefeller first experienced life's trials and triumphs raised by his devout Baptist mother Eliza Davidson Rockefeller learned the virtues of Charity frugality and the effective management of money from an early age this foundational training would later become the Bedrock of his extraordinary business Acumen however to fully comprehend the twists and turns of this familial Odyssey from Rags to Riches we must introduce an almost dezian character John D's Father William Avery Rockefeller far from the righteous path his wife tread William Avery lived a life of flamboyant ruses as a traveling salesman he pedal Miracle potions with the grand promise of healing any Affliction from insignificant ailments to terminal diseases astoundingly the key ingredient of these Elixir was petroleum a substance that unbeknownst to young John would power his future Paving his way towards becoming America's first billionaire William Avery however bore a less than reputable reputation he was a man of questionable Integrity dabbling in horse thievery and habitually tangling with the law his unorthodox Antics eventually led to a forced relocation to Cleveland Ohio a move that uprooted young Rockefeller from his childhood home in Upstate New York Eliza Rockefeller a figure of steadfast faith and resilience stood as the Counterpoint to William Avery's volatile lifestyle Eliza's devout religiosity played a significant role in shaping young John D rockefeller's worldview infusing his business strategies with a sense of moral responsibility and ethical diligence that would remain with him throughout his life now despite William Avery's dubious character he surprisingly harbored a staunch belief in the power of Education as such he enrolled John D and his brother William at Cleveland's Central High School the country's first free public high school West of the alany mountains this experience instilled a strong sense of Independence in John D particularly during his father's frequent lengthy absences and remarkably these absences were not without their Silver Lining when he did indeed return William Avery's ponant for deception extended to his own son with John D's father himself once bragging I cheat my boys every chance I get I want to make them sharp this unconventional paternal approach however inadvertently honed rockefeller's business instincts his early exposure to his father's swindles molded him into a shrewd negotiator a business Hawk with an Eagle Eye for spotting potential pitfalls and deceptive Maneuvers in the commercial Arena the motherly figure of Eliza Davidson however was the enduring guiding beacon in John D's life over the course of his life he progressively distanced himself from his father while his mother's influence and memory only Amplified her teachings left an indelible mark on Rockefeller who would go on to recall from the beginning I was trained to work to save and to give upon completion of his education Rockefeller now seasoned with both life and business experience found his heart's Delight in his high school sweetheart Laura Spelman marking the close of one eventful chapter and the dawn of another in his remarkable Journey John D rockefeller's business Journey one marked by scrupulous Financial accur and towering ambition began in September 1855 when at just 16 he entered the business world as an assistant bookkeeper for a small produce commission firm huitt and Tuttle in Cleveland even then his meticulous nature Shone through keeping detailed ledgers that accounted for every scent in his possession a staunch advocate of Charity Rockefeller adhered to the concept of tithing from his earliest days of earning income for himself committing 10% of his earnings to charitable causes in 1859 Rockefeller partnered with Morris B Clark launching a produce commission business they raised $4,000 more than 100,000 in today's dollars a sizable capital for the time the Civil War period proved lucrative as the union Army's demand for supplies soared however it was towards the end of the conflict as the prospect of wartime profits faded that Rockefeller and Clark turned their attention towards oil refining the true Turning Point Point came in 1866 when John's brother William Rockefeller Jr built another refinery in Cleveland pulling Jon into the partnership in 1867 Henry Morris and Flagler joined them establishing Rockefeller Andrews and Flagler which rapidly grew to be the world's largest oil refinery this operation laid the foundation for Standard Oil officially formed on January 10th 1870 which embarked on an aggressive campaign to consolidate the oil Market known as the C Cleland conquest or the Cleveland Massacre Standard Oil in less than 4 months in 1872 absorbed 22 of its 26 Cleveland competitors by 1880 Standard Oil controlled approximately 90% of all oil refineries and pipelines in the united states allowing Rockefeller to effectively dictate the entire industry's pace and Direction however rockefeller's strategies were not without benefits for the public before 1870 only the affluent could afford oil light provided by expensive whale oil however in the following decade thanks to the economies of scale realized by Standard Oil kerosene became widely accessible to the working and middle classes yet these business practices including underselling differential pricing and secret Transportation rebates also invited controversy and by 1880 Standard Oil was reported by the media as the most cruel impudent pitiless and grasping Monopoly that ever fastened upon a country the intense criticism led to the creation of the Standard Oil trust in 1882 a corporation of Corporations to centralize rockefeller's Holdings which further stirred public suspicion despite these challenges Standard Oil stood resilient consistently making profits year after year achieving an aura of invincibility and unparalleled wealth Ida Tarbell a journalist would ultimately lead a movement to challenge this Behemoth her meticulous investigation culminating in a series of damning Articles exposed standard oil's ruthless tactics and ultimately contributed to the dissolution of standard oil as a monopoly leading to the Clayton Antitrust Act without fully throwing in the towel Rockefeller started looking at his transition into retirement while still seeking to preserve his wealth and transform his new money into the more enduring old money he Diversified his Investments and launched the Rockefeller foundation in 1913 with an initial donation of $100 million broadening his legacy Beyond oil and Industry highlighting his commitment to philanthropy however his rise from a new money Tycoon to an old money patriarch was marred by personal challenges and public scorn with some speculating that the Relentless scrutiny caused Rockefeller to develop alopecia despite these obstacles the name Rockefeller had a journey from poverty to the patriarch of one of America's most powerful families that was only just beginning as he passed control of his dynasty to the Next Generation Now John D Rockefeller was not merely an astute businessman he was also an Ardent practitioner of old money principles such as long-term financial planning privacy understated Elegance philanthropy and Thrift these were the values he instilled in his children ensuring the Rockefeller Legacy would span Generations in contrast to the new money families of the Gilded Age The Rockefellers adopted a more discreet and sustainable approach to wealth consider the Vanderbilt and Gould families prominent figures of the Gilded Age whom epitomized new money opulence Cornelius Vanderbilt renowned for his Empire of steam ships and the New York Central Railroad celebrated his fortune with a flourish the manifestation of his wealth was particularly evident in his family's real estate purchases the family commissioned Grand mansions that showcased their affluence such as the opulent Vanderbilt mansions on Fifth Avenue which stood as symbols of their enormous wealth these included the triple Palace a trio of residences designed by famed Architects and decorated with marble gold and other precious materials in addition to these residences Cornelius Vanderbilt II acquired an expensive townhouse on 57th Street which later had to be sold due to financial strain Additionally the marble house built by William kissum Vanderbilt in Newport Rhode Island was another display of the Vanderbilt Fortune This lavish Mansion however was eventually sold due to its exorbitant upkeep costs their pension for Grandeur extended to their social events as well with the Vanderbilt costume ball of 1883 a million dooll spectacle immortalizing their ponant for lavish spending however the Vanderbilt's unchecked extravagance combined with their neglect of long-term financial planning ultimately led to the erosion of their substantial Fortune this financial mismanagement left many of their descendants grappling with relative poverty a stark contra to their forbears opulence similarly the Gould family led by railroad magnate Jay Gould failed to preserve their wealth across Generations Jay Gould was famed for his extravagant lifestyle evident in his creation of Lindhurst a sprawling Gothic Revival Mansion his offspring mirrored his extravagance erecting impressive residences such as a castle-like mansion in Lakewood New Jersey and a French Shadow inspired Mansion in ttwn New York however the Goulds mirrored the Vanderbilts in their of emphasis on sustained wealth management George JG II and Jay G II descendants of this once affluent lineage pursued commendable careers yet the family fortune dwindled over time after George J Gould's demise their once formidable wealth had practically evaporated resulting in their descendants leading lives far from their opulent Heritage today the Vanderbilt and gold legacies serve as potent reminders of the impermanence of wealth without prudent stewardship the next generation of Rockefellers however took a different path rooted in the old money traditions of privacy Thrift and philanthropy these principles played out vividly in the lives of John D Rockefeller senior's children John D Rockefeller Jr the only son and the primary Heir inherited not only his father's vast Fortune but also his commitment to philanthropy he financed the construction of the Rockefeller Center in New York and restored Colonial Williamsburg among many other philanthropic endeavors yet despite his eye-watering size of income he lived a relatively modest lifestyle avoiding public displays of wealth and maintaining a strong focus on his family similarly rockefeller's daughters also embodied the old money principles Elizabeth the eldest married a banker and led a private life out of the public eye Alice despite suffering from various health issues throughout her life was known for her philanthropy and carried the family tradition of supporting educational and health causes alter the third child married a scientist and focused her efforts on supporting scientific research Edith the youngest devoted herself to music and philanthropy continuing the family's commitment to giving back to society however embodying old money principles did not exempt the Rockefeller children from difficulties John D Rockefeller Jr for instance was often criticized for his ruthless business practices just like his father similarly Alice struggled with her health suffering from various illnesses throughout her life the public scrutiny that came with the Rockefeller name was also a constant source of stress for the family yet despite these challenges the old money principles continued to guide them the Rockefeller children following their father's example sought to preserve their wealth for future Generations focusing on long-term financial planning rather than short-term gain they understood the importance of privacy choosing to live away from the public eye they embraced understated elegance and avoided the ostentatious displays of wealth common among new money families the financial landscape of America's Gilded Age was indeed a volatile Crucible with fortunes being made and lost almost overnight within this climate a distinction arose between new money the freshly minted wealth of entrepreneurs and industrialists and old money the inherited wealth of established families that had accumulated assets over Generations the terms in capsulated more than just the age of wealth they signified cultural differences social acceptability and the capacity for wealth preservation The Rockefellers beginning as new money were no strangers to this volatility however their pragmatic approach to wealth accumulation and preservation arguably allowed them to transition into the esteemed category of old money key to this transformation was their ability to integrate into high society and form strategic alliances through marriage the family made insightful choices in matrimony aligning themselves with well-established families through advantageous marriages John D Rockefeller Jr the sole son of John D Rockefeller Senor set the pace in 1901 when he married Abby aldrid Abby wasn't just any bride she was the daughter of US senator Nelson W Aldridge a political Powerhouse whose influence stretched from Wall Street to Washington The Aldrich Family had a strong political lineage and this marital Alliance thrust The Rockefellers into significant political circles offering them not just social prominence but also a chance to influence policy their progeny representing the third generation of the Rockefellers adopted this pattern of strategically beneficial marriages in 1930 Nelson Rockefeller later to become the vice president of the United States married Mary Todd Hunter Clark Mary came from an esteemed Philadelphia family known for its quiet yet significant influence in the city's legal and civic affairs these unions were not solely about the joining of two individuals but rather about the Confluence of power influence and long-standing family heritage The Rockefellers also distinguished themselves through public service and philanthropy as previously noted Nelson held the high office of vice president under President Gerald Ford reinforcing the family's presence in the nation's political sphere simultaneously his brother Lawrence carved out a niche as a venture capitalist and passionate conservationist contributing to the preservation of natural habitats and environmental sustainability continuing the family's trajectory in public service John D Rockefeller IV more commonly called Jay Nelson's nephew and a fourth generation Rockefeller built a significant political career born in 1937 Jay served as West Virginia's Secretary of State and later as the state's Governor impressively holding office from 1977 to 1985 in 1984 he was elected to the United States Senate serving five terms until he decided not to seek re-election in 2014 as a senator Jay championed issues such as Healthcare reform and internet privacy thus maintaining the family's tradition of Public Service additionally embedded in the core of the Rockefeller family's Legacy is their remarkable philanthropy The Rockefellers became known as patrons of education and the Arts helping to shape America's cultural landscape they lent their considerable resources to institutions such as the University of Chicago and the Museum of Modern Art this Dee rooted philanthropy wasn't simply about donating money it was an essential tool for shaping their public Persona showcasing their commitment to societal advancement yet alongside this is their equally noteworthy ability to amass and preserve substantial wealth this Duality reflects the character of John D Rockefeller himself who was known to be a ruthless businessman yet an incredibly generous philanthropist their philanthropic Ventures were strategic they served to not only improve Public Welfare but also to bolster their societal standing assisting in their seamless transition from new money to old money through these actions The Rockefellers were not just cementing their place within societal Elites but also highlighting their dedication to the public good therefore simultaneously the Rockefellers adopted several strategies to safeguard their wealth ensuring that it would endure across Generations one significant step in this regard was the establishment of the Rockefeller foundation in 1913 this move was more than an act of Charity it was a calculated effort to Shield their wealth from heavy estate taxes thereby allowing it to grow and benefit Society simultaneously over time the foundation's immense contributions to public health education and arts helped to further establish and fortify the Rockefeller name trusts also played a critical role in their wealth preservation strategy John D Rockefeller senor formed his first trust in 1891 transferring a significant portion of his fortune to it this move offered both tax advantages and protection against potential heirs mismanagement thereby ensuring the continuity of the Rockefeller fortune in addition The Rockefellers innovatively used holding companies to manage their diverse business interests these corporate structures provided a protective shield for their wealth while also promoting growth this use of corporate entities set a precedent for future wealthy families showing how wealth could be protected and multiplied simultaneously as such the Rockefellers are a perfect example of how one family managed to become synonymous with philanthropy while maintaining an iron grip on their wealth demonstrating a duality that has become part of their enduring Legacy in the opening years of the 20th century the Rockefeller Dynasty stood as an unassailable pillar of American affluence and influence furthermore their palatial Estates sprawled across across prominent Landscapes from the bustling heart of New York to the Sunkissed shores of Florida and the opulent retreats in Newport these were not merely homes but grandiose statements of wealth and Architectural prowess however the looming shadow of the Great Depression would come to Eclipse even these illustrious sanctuaries compelling the family to part with many of their treasured possessions in today's episode of old money Mansions will guide you through the riveting Narrative of these lost Rockefeller Mansions telling the full size of why these lavish homes were relinquished what transpired in the aftermath of their sales and whether The Parting was a mere Financial exigency or an emblematic shift in the American social class system first on our list we enter the John D Rockefeller Mansion but first a quick history lesson
