location to construct a lavish Brownstone Mansion at four West 54th Street in 1864 indeed the Mansion Was An Elegant Abode an embodiment of Williams's prosperity and an early marker of the neighborhood's potential simultaneously in the southern charm of Richmond Virginia Katherine yarington managed a boarding house to support her family following her husband's demise among her five children was a 19-year-old Arabella a young woman who radiated Beauty and had a penchant for inviting drama into her life her life took a significant turn when CIS P Huntington a married New York Railroad magnate initiated a passionate relationship with her subsequently the yarington clan relocated to the Big Apple in 1869 after this Arabella soon had a child Archer Worsham whose paternity was a subject of much gossip the general consensus though unverified was that Archer was indeed Huntington's son with a newfound Financial cushion Arabella secured The Brownstone Mansion at 4 West 54th on its grounds the mansion's rich and multifaceted past gradually receded marking the end of a unique chapter in the story of Victorian domestic architecture at the number two slot in our historic inventory Rockwood Hall emerges as a paragon of the Gilded Age a remarkable residence in Mount Pleasant New York established in 1849 the mansion's lineage included distinguished individuals such as Alexander Slidel McKenzie and William Henry aspenwall however its transformation into a veritable Palace occurred under the ownership of William Rockefeller John D rockefeller's brother William expanded the Mansion to an a inspiring 204 rooms establishing it as the second largest private residence in America during his lifetime and the architectural Grandeur of Rockwood Hall didn't just remain confined within its walls it served as the conceptual blueprint for the Sacred Heart Academy in Cincinnati Ohio designed by Thomas sargant this Academy took shape between 1864 and 1867 as the Rockefeller state park preserve the Mansion passed through a series of custodians including Tech Giant IBM what remains today is but a fragment of its former self a nostalgic Gate House and terrorist foundations remnants that tell the tale of an age of luxury and ambition along the Hudson River architectural features that set Rockwood Hall apart art included a towering four-story structure at the opulence home to an entrance hall drawing room library music room and various other Chambers designed for leisure and refinement Mosaic floors walls crafted from American Oak and intricately designed papier-mâché ceilings greeted the eye at every turn the grand staircase adorned with an American Oak ballustrade was a spectacle unto itself crowned by a glorious Dome of Cathedral glass but its Innovations weren't limited to visual Aesthetics Advanced features like floorboard vents for Heating and electric buttons for calling servants imbued the mansion with a Forward Thinking sensibility fire safety was Paramount as evidenced by fireproof construction and sliding doors in nearly all rooms additionally landscape architect Frederick La Olstead complemented the mansion's structural Beauty with Lush external environs Rolling Hills Scenic Woodlands Meadows and artistically designed Gardens enriched The Mansions surrounding acreage today Rockwood Hall remains an evocative Relic a nestled in the Lush Landscapes of Harnet County North Carolina Overhills holds the third spot on our roster once an expansive 177,000 acre Haven it was a cherished Jewel In The rockella Family Estate breathing Monument to enduring love and Timeless Elegance in a grand study a wash with flickering Shadows Andrew Carnegie sits at a dark mahogany desk the room is nearly dark save for the small circle of light cast by an ornate oil lamp the year is 1889 and outside the window the Pittsburgh steel mills are Ablaze lighting up the night sky like the stars glimmering above the scotsman's palacial estate however inside a different kind of Inferno Burns it is the fire of an idea that will change the course of American history with a clenched jaw and eyes gleaming with resolve carnegi leans forward gripping his quill as if it were a sword as he scribbles intently he hesitates suddenly feeling the weight of the next sentence after taking in a deep reflective breath the magnate finally writes his declaration for the ages the man who dies Rich dies disgraced relieved Carnegie puts down his pen contemplating the magnitude of the words he has just committed to paper it is indeed a declaration that will reverberate through generations shaking the foundations of American capitalism now in our 21st century economic climate the concept of old money signifies not just wealth but the enduring Legacy of families who have maintained and even greatly expanded their fortunes for Generations but what happens when a family intentionally breaks this cycle of generational wealth and gives away their billions on purpose thus consciously pushing their status back down to the middle class in this episode of old money luxury we will explore this exact Enigma Guided by the ideology of their patriarch Andrew Carnegie the Carnegie family went from amassing a colossal fortune that for a a Time crowned him the world's richest man to strategically giving away more than $5.6 billion in today's currency with that mystifying Prelude as a backdrop join us as we now recount the entire Saga and describe how the carnegies went from old money to no money our journey first begins on the Misty Lanes of dun firm Line Scotland where Andrew Carnegie first saw the light of day in 1835 born to No Ordinary Family young Andrew's lineage was steeped in The Humble craft of weaving a tradition nourished by his father William Carnegie and his paternal grandfather the maternal side of his family tree branched off in a different direction Andrew's mother Margaret Morrison Carnegie hailed from a line deeply involved in tanning and shoe making however she was not merely a homemaker but rather the daughter of George lorder a political stalwart who ensured young Andrew's mind was sparked by the heroics of Scottish Legends like Robert the BR Bruce Rob Roy and William Wallace the Carnegie family's daily life was framed by the quaintness of their Weaver Cottage and the looming presence of the Industrial Revolution a transformative period that remodeled Scotland's social Fabric and economic Vitality dunfermline itself was a hub of textile manufacturing but the family's fortunes dimmed as Factory systems replaced traditional handlooms this decline subjected Weavers like William Carnegie to financial hardships casting Shadows of poverty over their lives yet William was not just a weaver caught in the crossfire of industrialization he was a passionate advocate of the chartist movement which called for broad political and social reforms on the other side Margaret Morrison Carnegie's family was also politically active adding another layer to Andrew's inherent views on social responsibility but how did the teenaged Andrew navigate through a world shifting under his feet the answer lies in his relentless pursuit of self-education formal schooling took a backseat as Andrew leaned into books and night school supported by a family tradition that valued intellectual rigor and political activism libraries became his sanctuaries thanks to Uncle George's introductions to literary masterpieces and historical narratives these early experiences seeded his Lifelong Love for libraries and a philanthropic fervor to construct these reservoirs of knowledge across English-speaking countries but Scot 's Industrial Revolution would soon not just alter the nation's economy it would birth complex societal structures trigger Urban migration and lead to governmental reforms amidst this tumult it became clear that the Carnegie family needed to seek greener pastures and to that the burgeoning magnetic Allure of the American dream the notion that in the United States you can make it big through sheer grit and Enterprise and we arrive at the year 1848 when the carnegies decide to pack up their hopes and dreams cross the Atlantic and settle in alagan City Pennsylvania here they would hope to find not just economic sustenance but a stage upon which young Andrew could shape his indomitable Destiny now the Carnegie family had deliberately chosen alagan the budding industrial suburb of Pittsburgh for its abundant economic prospects during this period in the mid 19th century America was a crucible of change fueled by Manifest Destiny and the California Gold Rush of 1849 the nation itself was also on the cusp of an industrial revolution transitioning from agricultural Simplicity to Industrial complexity alany specifically was a mosaic of diverse communities including Germans and croats and offered a plethora of job opportunities in sectors like commercial trading meat packing and leather tanning however despite these favorable conditions the Carn faced initial hardships William Carnegie's weaving business faltered forcing 13-year-old Andrew into the laborious role of a bobbin boy in a cotton mill where he would work 72-hour weeks for a modest $120 per hour roughly $168 a month in today's currency but not one to be confined by menial tasks Andrew navigated through a series of roles that laid the foundation for his later Empire positions such as messenger secretary and teleg operator for the Pennsylvania Railroad served as an informal education in corporate structure and strategy his affiliation with the railroad industry was particularly instructive it offered lessons in management and cost optimization as well as invaluable connections with industry giants like Thomas a Scott and Jay Edgar Thompson these associations helped Carnegie secure early Ventures like the Keystone Bridge Company critical to the railway infrastructure subsequently the Civil War War Amplified Carnegie's already diversifying portfolio especially with his stake in the Colombia Oil Company the conflict skyrocketed demand for both iron and oil boosting his profits although eligible for military service he opted to pay a substitute $850 or roughly $20,000 modern dollars to enlist in his stad a common practice among the affluent and by the early 1870s Carnegie had Diversified his Investments across a broad spectrum his real breakthrough came in 1873 with the recognition of Steel's untapped potential notably he initiated the construction of the Edgar Thompson Works in bradoock Pennsylvania a groundbreaking facility introducing the besser steel making process to America costing an estimated 1.2 million or roughly $30 million in modern currency the mill was a high stakes bet on an unproven sector yet the gamble yielded significant dividends as the facility didn't just Elevate steel production standards it fundamentally altered the fabric of American industry in the Years following Carnegie further expanded and integrated his business Holdings including significant assets like the Scotia or mines Union iron Mills and Lucy furnaces this methodical combination of resources and production capacities set the stage for the emergence of the PowerHouse that became Carnegie steel company in 1889 now by the dawn of the 1890s Andrew Carnegie was firmly established as an industrial Colossus presiding over a domain that extended well beyond the furnaces of his steel operations his Carnegie Steel company was the Bedrock of global steel production redefining benchmarks for both efficiency and profit during his financial Peak Carnegie was an indomitable force in American Enterprise amassing his immense wealth chiefly through steel production Endeavors his holding things were extensive comprising a diverse array of investments from lucrative real estate to varied business ventures one significant acquisition was Scotland's skebo Castle in 1899 which he transformed into a tranquil Retreat far from the industrial hustle you see Carnegie's Monumental success was rooted in two pillars Innovative techniques and shrewd business Acumen his Playbook included various methods aimed at ensuring Carnegie steel remained an industry leader vertical integration strategic merges and an uncompromising attention to cost efficiency were among them for example vertical integration was foundational to his business approach he took ownership of entities along different stages of the steel making process from iron or mines to railway companies this endtoend control enabled him to dramatically lower production costs while simultaneously optimizing operations for greater efficiency his expansionary Ambitions were further realized through mergers and Acquisitions notably the union of assets with HC Frick and Company in 1892 this move fortified the Carnegie Steel company giving him greater leverage over both the marketplace and the supply chain an unwavering dedication to operational efficiency led him to adopt the Bessa process significantly quickening steel production while driving down costs this made steel more affordable and accessible altering the American landscape by facilitating the construction of Bridges skyscrapers and expansive Railway systems however this Zeal for efficiency occasionally came at the cost of Labor welfare the introduction of cuttingedge technology and methods often preceded adequate safety measures this tension came to a head in the Homestead Strike of 1892 a violent Skirmish between workers and management that resulted in 12 deaths and created an enduring fissure between labor and Leadership Henry Clay Frick who was the muscle of Carnegie steel at the time instigated the conflict through wage reductions while Carnegie was geographically removed quietly waiting back in Scotland he had empowered Frick to act unilaterally leading to a violent standoff that left an indelible scar on his otherwise stored Legacy indeed this harrowing incident triggered a significant self- Reckoning for Carnegie ultimately inspiring his Gospel of wealth philosophy this ideological shift diverged markedly from his peers prevailing perspectives emphasizing not just the ownership but also the ethical stewardship of wealth published initially in 1889 the thesis proposed that affluence carried a moral duty to benefit Society at large transitioning from Theory to action Carnegie would subsequently Channel his resources into philanthropy laying the groundwork for educational institutions public libraries and other community focused entities however his philanthropic master stroke would first need an exit strategy From the Rough and Tumble business world of Industrial Age America and therefore he would need a powerful Ally to help strategically guide him out of the corridors of Ruthless Gilded Age Finance now at the turn of the 20th century when the American steel industry was the backbone of a rapidly modernizing economy legendary financier and Machiavellian businessman JP Morgan saw an unparalleled opportunity Steel was not just a commodity it was the lifeblood of industrializing cities the burgeoning Auto industry and wartime production during World War I by acquiring Carnegie steel Morgan could craft the United States Steel Corporation in His image a monolithic entity that at the time was the world's largest company this acquisition enabled Morgan to Corner the steel market and exploit The Soaring demand for this vital resource thus the 1901 sale of Carnegie steel to JP Morgan for an astonishing $480 million equivalent to over 13 billion in today's money was more than just a business transaction it provided Andrew Carnegie with the Financial Muscle to massively escalate his philanthropic activities in sync with his own moral guidelines encapsulated in his Gospel of wealth with this philosophy as his North Star and seemingly endless cash in the bank after the sale of his Empire Carnegie proclaimed that the affluent were morally obligated to to disperse their wealth in their lifetime and this sale armed him with the capital to make a transformative societal impact Keg's subsequent laress left an indelible mark on multiple sectors education peace initiatives and scientific research to name a few among his most enduring contributions were the Carnegie libraries a Global Network of more than 2,500 public libraries indeed his advocacy for public libraries came at an opportune moment when the American public library system was in its nent stage meeting the growing need for accessible knowledge consequently these libraries were a catalyst for education and self-improvement across diverse populations and they ushered in a new era of knowledge democratization local communities soon became the financial pillars of these libraries providing about 80% of Public Funding furthermore Carnegie's focus on libraries spurred a ripple effect inspiring other local philanthropists to invest in their communities these libraries were not just buildings filled with books they were Innovation hubs that enriched their localities and continue to serve their communities today Beyond libraries Carnegie's Financial Legacy led to the founding of educational behemoths like Carnegie melon University an institution renowned for its contributions to science technology engineering and Mathematics additionally Carnegie Hall in New York City stands as an emblem of his patronage in the Arts this iconic concert venue has been a stage for countless musical Legends and continues to uphold its reputation as an architectural and cultural Jewel however the sale of Carnegie steel also had ramifications for Carnegie's personal fortune in keeping with his views on wealth dispersion Carnegie systematically drained nearly all of his family's Financial reservoirs now in 1919 Andrew Carnegie's death led to a circumspect   4. @douglasburnside: You keep stressing how the cost of renting is going to go up year after year. Well, owning a home the cost of taxes, insurance, repairs and maintenance is also going to go up year after year, particularly if the home is in an HOA where annual assessments can add significantly to the home owning expense. I own my home, it is paid for, but my budget includes $10,000 per year for the unexpected surprises that pop just about every month. Most recently it was termites. Next month it is going to be digging up and laying a bunch of pipe in the yard so that the plants are no longer fed the expensive filtered, softened, and sanitized water that is fed into the house for human consumption.

The decision is not as clear cut as you make out, particularly if you look at your house as an investment rather than a home. In most cases, costs over time are less renting than buying, particularly when you factor in the idea of investing the money that would have gone into the mortgage. In my case, I bought a home, and as long as I can keep food on the table I don't care how much it costs me.
  5. @trekmanone1676: I did own during my mid-life but in retirement, sold in favor of renting. The dividends and interest that invested income provided, was more than enough to rent a furnished 2-bed apartment and lots of extra cash left over for food, others expenses or to reinvest. Houses don't provide income but they sure suck in that income.  That got me thinking. I owned my house for ~30 years, paid off mortgage. I know what I paid for it and what I sold it for and I know the expenses needed to maintain a house.  Plug that into a spreadsheet and calculate IRR (Internal Rate of Return). Kind of like a CAGR (Compounded Annual Growth Rate). Reasonable but not rich at all.
Now create a 30-year renting scenario. Even through 2 recessions, the spreadsheet showed I was a millionaire after 30-years and my investments threw off enough cash to fund my cost of living plus reinvest some funds. 
For example, take SPY, an ETF that tracks the S&P500. Buy $10,000 of SPY 30 years old, invest all dividends for 30 years, you are a certified millionaire! Subtract renting expenses from the SPY scenario, you still are a millionaire after 30 years. There you have it. Never buy and own a house. Invest in REITs if you must but don't physically own a house.

- Homeownership And America's Growing Wealth Gap: Kind: captions Language: en So often we'll be out here or we'll be inside and we'll come outside, and there will be White people standing right there on the stoop reading, and it's like, this is trespassing, and if you opened your front door and saw a Black guy standing there right at your front door, you'd be like, "Cops." If she were to show my mother that house, she would lose her job. In those days it was that blatant. I've had agents invoke burning crosses. But that's just the difference in like feelings of ownership. In the United States, home ownership is seen as the most effective way to build generational wealth, with the average homeowner having a household wealth of $231,000 compared to the average renter having a household wealth of just $5,200. But data shows discriminatory policies left many Black Americans out of the economic picture, and the problem is only getting worse. In the second quarter of 2019, Black homeownership hit a 50-year low of 40.6%. In fact, if you look at the 100 U.S. cities with the largest population of Black households today, every single one of them shows a growing divide between Black and White homeownership. It has a lot to do with today's frameworks and a lot of the structural barriers, discriminatory barriers, redlining issues in communities, and also issues with access to credit in our housing finance systems. According to Urban Institute data, the current gap between White and Black homeownership sits at about 30%. That's larger than the 27% point gap that existed in 1960, before the Fair Housing Act was passed. The Fair Housing Act in 1968 was passed right around the same time as the Civil Rights Act. That really enabled and essentially abolished the ability for real estate property owners and others to discriminate on the basis of race, sex and a whole host of other critical factors. It was just a little over 50 years ago that that was enacted, so this is very recent history. Fair housing policies are so recent that stories of blatant housing discrimination still echo for Black Americans. When my parents went to go purchase their home in Toledo, Ohio, my mom actually wanted to buy a home in a predominantly White suburb called Sylvania, Ohio. The real estate agent explained to my mother that she could not show my mom the home because we were Black. And she explained to my mom that if she were to show my mother that house that she would lose her job. So she took my mom to a community in Toledo where she said they were moving Black families who are kind of like in our income bracket. Rice was just six months old when her family moved into their home in 1963. At the time, they were the only Black family on the block. By the time she started kindergarten in 1968, her neighborhood was predominantly Black. That's how quickly the neighborhood changed because of real estate blockbusting and steering practices. Blockbusting is a now-illegal tactic used by real estate agents and developers who convince White homeowners to sell their homes out of fear that racial minorities will move into the neighborhood and lower property values. Steering is the unlawful act of limiting where you show a person a particular home based on race. As our neighborhood was designated as what they called then a "changing neighborhood," meaning the real estate community and the lending community understood that Black people would be moving into this area, the lenders began redlining the area. Redlining is a practice that was started in the 1930s by the Home Owners' Loan Corporation, where neighborhoods would get color grades based on perceived mortgage security. At the time, neighborhoods were coded as green for type A, blue for type B, yellow for type C, and red for type D. Type A neighborhoods were viewed as financially reliable, while type D neighborhoods were viewed as financially insecure. But here's where it gets ugly. Type D neighborhoods were predominantly populated by Black families, meaning that even if a Black person living in that area was credit-worthy, banks would still deny them. So when my dad went to his bank to get a loan, the loan officer actually told him, "Mr. Rice, we're not making loans in that neighborhood." In those days it was that blatant. Like, "We're not making loans below Upton Street." And so that's why my parents had to go to a subprime lender. Subprime loans became a fixture in redlined neighborhoods, and though redlining was outlawed in the 1960s, its impact still has lingering effects on Black people today. Over the last 40 years, property values in redlined neighborhoods gained half the value of those in green-lined neighborhoods. By the '90s, subprime lenders started pushing adjustable rate mortgages, which are loans that typically start off with low interest rates and then drastically increase after just a few short years. We saw payments increasing by 50%. So imagine if one month your mortgage payment is $1,000 and the next month your mortgage payment is $1,500. It's a huge increase. This increase in adjustable rate mortgages led to more borrowers refinancing their homes every two to three years, slowly stripping them of their equity every time they made a payment. According to Rice, brokers pushed subprime loans because they were making a lot of money doing it. In fact, between 1994 and 2000, the amount of new subprime mortgages grew from $35 billion to $140 billion. Between 2004 and 2007, Hispanic Americans and Black Americans were 78% and 105% more likely than White Americans to have a high cost mortgage. Consequently, Black and brown families were disproportionately impacted by the 2007-2010 housing crisis, with both groups being nearly twice as likely as White families to lose their homes. Before the housing crisis hit, the median net worth of Black households was a little over $12,000, compared to the nearly $135,000 net worth of White households. By 2009, those net worths dropped to about $5,700 and $113,000 respectively. While redlining and subprime loans robbed Black families of the opportunity to build wealth in the 20th century, there was something just as insidious on the horizon: gentrification. In major metropolitan areas that were once predominantly Black, homeownership levels have drastically shifted as a result of gentrification and rising housing costs. Gentrification involves the renovation of properties and businesses in underfunded neighborhoods, which some see as a positive thing. The negatives come when long-term residents, who disproportionately tend to be people of color, are pushed out of those neighborhoods as the neighborhoods begin to be well-resourced. Between 2000 and 2013, more than $135,000 residents in 230 neighborhoods across the country were displaced from their homes due to gentrification. Like, we withstood all this shit. We're still withstanding. I definitely feel less at home than I used to, but also kind of more at home because of the businesses. They give me hope. They give me a sense of ownership that I've known them longer than the wave. Parker has watched a wave of gentrification transform his Bedford-Stuyvesant neighborhood in Brooklyn, New York. Parker's parents purchased their brownstone home for $90,000 in 1985. Today, similar properties in his neighborhood sell for well over $1 million. So a lot of my family lives on this block right here. These two houses, these are my mom's best friends. Most of the businesses in Parker's neighborhood are still Black-owned, but home ownership has changed drastically. And a lot of that has to do with marketers and developers literally changing the name of the neighborhood to suit affluent buyers. The whole renaming and remarketing of Bed-Stuy into Stuyvesant Heights, it's an old name, and then there was never a stark difference. It's more like regional than lines, but now they're trying to call the whole Bed-Stuy, Stuyvesant Heights. It's a marketing ploy because Bed-Stuy has now a history, so that it becomes more palatable for these parents to pay for their kids to come live here. Since the 1930s, Bedford Stuyvesant has been a predominately Black neighborhood. As of 2000, the Black community made up three quarters of the population. By 2015, that number dropped to half. Parker's parents retired after the 2008 recession, but the family has three other tenants to lighten the financial burden. And while their home value has increased significantly since 1985, Parker says refinancing is difficult because of his parents' retired income. As he's gotten older, Parker understands how important home ownership is for his family's long-term financial stability. If your parents own a home, help them keep the home. Okay? Contribute. Make an effort. Do some work around the house. Do anything that will guarantee your... How can I say this? Your legacy? Your legacy, yes. Gentrification is taking place in Black and brown neighborhoods across the country, but not all families are as fortunate as Parker's. In Detroit, a third of the city's properties have foreclosed since 2008 due to rising costs and tax inflation. A January 2020 investigation by The Detroit News found that homeowners in the city were collectively overtaxed by at least $600 million dollars following the Great Recession. Homes in the cities are being assessed at a value that was much higher than the market rate. This overtaxation of local properties led to Michigan's Black home ownership rate dropping from over 50% in 2000 to 40% in 2016. In a 2020 report from the Washington Center for Equitable Growth, it was shown that on average, Black and Hispanic residents bear a 10-13% higher property tax burden than White residents nationally. According to experts, county assessors have historically overtaxed Black residents as a form of punishment for economic mobility. This practice of overtaxing Black residents has only gotten worse with the surge of gentrification. So what we see, unfortunately, is people are being squeezed out on multiple fronts as gentrification happens. And if cities want to prevent this from happening, they have the power to do that. They can adopt tax lean and tax forgiveness programs to help people stay in their homes as the values are rising. They can adapt homestead programs so that long term residents who have been living in the communities for a long time are able to stay as the communities become more wealthy, so to speak. Recent studies show that housing discrimination is still prevalent today. A three-year investigation released by Newsday in 2019 found that real estate agents in Long Island, New York asked for different financial qualifications from White homebuyers than they did from Black and brown homebuyers. The report also found evidence that many agents still practice steering when showing homes. To stop these acts of discrimination from taking place, government agencies and advocacy groups have conducted paired testing experiments where they send two subjects, one White and the other a person of color, to act as potential buyers or renters for a home. I've had agents call me colored, and this is in New York. I've had agents invoke burning crosses to dissuade me from buying a home in certain areas. Experiences can vary. For the most part, you do not know. But I have had some experiences where the treatment was so blatant that you just sort of have to maintain your poker face. Don't encourage or or dissuade them from continuing and just try to go about acquiring the information. In situations where acts of housing discrimination weren't as blatant, Carter says he would later find out that an agent told him a property was no longer available when it really was, or that an agent didn't return his call about a property on the market, but returned the call of a White tester inquiring about the same home. Recently, the federal government proposed rollbacks on fair housing policies. In July of 2020, President Trump tweeted that he's rescinding the Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing Act, an act passed by the Obama administration to further the protections of the 1968 Fair Housing Act. In response to the federal government's position on housing discrimination, experts say state officials need to step up to enforce stricter fair housing laws. We've been seeing more people filing with state agencies or going to the state courts in order to exercise their rights under the law. We've been seeing some attorneys general stepping up to the plate to exercise their state's fair housing laws. While states work to uphold fair housing laws, real estate experts recommend that potential first-time homebuyers look to grant programs and financial resources that will make homeownership more accessible. Homeownership is the #1 way for families and individuals to build wealth in the United States. Now, it's not the only way, but it is the #1 way, and I don't see anything on the horizon changing that paradigm. So we have to make sure that communities of color have access to homeownership opportunities. And for Black homeowners wanting to stay in gentrifying neighborhoods, Parker's aunt, a homeowner in Bed-Stuy, says collective action is the only way forward. If three young couples buy a three-family, guess what? They could afford it. But you know what? We have to learn how to live with each other. That's the key, okay? And guess what? What's happening in the world right now is making us live together. Live together, actually. Okay?

debt can sink you it's like swimming with too much weight on your back so to see if you can afford to buy a house based on your income and debt let me break down the numbers for you total housing costs divided by your gross monthly income equals your monthly housing costs just multiply that number by 100 to get your percentage is it lower than 28% it should be let me show you an example let's say your total housing costs are $1,120 a month and you make $4,000 1,120 divided by $4,000 equals 2828 * 100 that's 28% now couple of things I want to point out here first of all that total number it says total housing costs are $1,120 a month total does not just mean your monthly payment if you own it's the sprinklers it's the occasional repair it's even the gas to get to Home Depot it's all of it okay second this number says 28% ideally you would be a little lower than that so you have some margin although again in high cost of living areas like New York LA or frankly a lot of cities these days most people have gotten above this next calculate your 2836 number see if your total monthly housing cost plus your total debt load would be lower than 36% of your gross monthly income that means factoring in things like credit card debt student loans car loans all of it the more debt you have the riskier it is to lend to you and that means you might not be able to get a home loan but more importantly you're putting your household at risk here's how to calculate it amount of monthly debt you owe divided by gross monthly income equals debt to income ratio multiply that number by 100 to get your percentage the lower the number is the better the 2836 rule says that you should ideally have no more than 36% of your debt to income ratio here's an example let's say that you're spending 28% of your gross income on housing and you want to figure out your 2836 number well to find out the 36 number we look at the rest of your debt say you're spending $1,000 a month paying off credit card debt making $75,000 a year or 6250 a month 1,000 divided by 6250 is16 multiply that by 100 that's 16% well guess what in this case you're already spending 28% of your gross income on housing when you add the 16% you have a 2844 number that's higher than 36% that means you're in serious risk this would be troubling if I saw this so I want you to calculate your own 2836 number now there are a few exceptions to this rule if you live in a high cost of living area like New York La SF again some people stretch that number even up to 34 35% but it becomes very very risky next if you have no debt like like no car payment no student loans no credit card debt you might stretch your housing number a little bit I would personally consider going to around 33% but I'm very conservative with my finances if your income is reasonably expected to go up soon such as a promotion again maybe you stretch the numbers a little but I would be very hesitant about doing this after all this you should There's 12 events that are always on their calendars. Hi, I'm Joi-Marie McKenzie, and I am Business Insider's editor-in-chief of our Life division. Billionaires are not hard to find, even if there are only 2,600 of them. In January, billionaires descend on Davos in Switzerland to attend the World Economic Forum. This event is invite-only, and it's a beautiful location, a ski resort where there's tons of fancy dinners, panels. So you may even see Mark Zuckerberg or Bill Gates walking the streets attending a fancy dinner or attending one of these panels, or anywhere in between. In February, it's all about the Super Bowl. In fact, they're trekking to this very small town called Hailey, Idaho, that you and I have probably never heard of, but these billionaires are going every single year for the Allen & Company Sun Valley Conference. In August, they finally get some time to really relax and unplug by trekking to the desert to Burning Man. Billionaires are not doing Burning Man like the rest of us. In fact, even how they get there is pretty cool. They build a pop-up airport where their chartered jets and private jets can land right onto the desert. In fact, they're building these really gorgeous campsites with air conditioning, private chefs, and the likes. So mud, I don't think so. Billionaires are saying farewell to summer in Monte Carlo at the Monaco Yacht Show. It's where they're bringing their yachts and superyachts to the place to be to show off their boats. They feature elevators, spas, hot tubs, even helipads, yes, helipads, for their helicopters. If you thought jet skis were nice, take a look at these boats. The pieces that are sold at this art fair can go for millions of dollars, but they keep it really under wraps and mysterious, so we never quite know what's purchased. But we just know it's really expensive. And if you want to go see it for yourself, tickets are actually really affordable. This year, they're averaging around $50. - This is the Richest Old Money Family in New York...: Kind: captions Language: en Every street in New York tells a story. Every brick, every brownstone, every skyscraper holds a secret. But behind the city's modern hustle, behind the noise and the energy, there's a foundation. And it wasn't built just with steel and concrete, but with ambition, rivalry, and a truly staggering amount of wealth, all laid by a handful of powerful dynasties. Now, these aren't today's headline grabbing billionaires minted overnight by some new tech or a market trend. These are the old money families whose names are literally etched into the city's DNA. Aster, Vanderbilt, Whitney. Their influence is like a quiet hum beneath the roar of the city. A force that shaped our social circles, our museums, and the very skyline we see today. And when we say richest, we're not just talking about who has the most digits in their bank account right now. While some of these fortunes have been spread out over generations, we're talking about a different kind of wealth. The kind that bought Manhattan when it was still farmland, that built the institutions, and that wrote the rules of society long before the rest of the world was paying attention. So, we're pulling back the curtain to journey through the Gilded Age and beyond, uncovering the 10 dynasties whose influence still shapes New York in ways you can and can't see. You can't talk about old money in New York without starting with one name, Aster. They were the benchmark, the original Titans. Their story is the American dream, just on a scale that feels almost mythological. And it all started not in a Park Avenue ballroom, but with a determined young immigrant from Germany. John Jacob Aster landed in America in 1783 with little more than a few flutes to his name and a relentless drive. He jumped into the brutal fur trade. But what set Aster apart was his ruthless foresight. He built a near monopoly, the American fur company, stretching from the Great Lakes to the Oregon coast. But it wasn't fur that would cement his family's legacy. It was what he did next. With an almost prophetic vision, Aster started sinking his profits into what everyone else thought was just cheap farmland, the island of Manhattan. He bought up huge trackcts of land north of the city limits, earning him the nickname the landlord of New York. People thought he was crazy, but Aster knew New York was going to grow. He rarely sold anything. He leased it, creating a river of income that flowed for generations. By his death in 1848, he was the richest man in America. His kids and grandkids kept the strategy going, but it was the later generations who turned that wealth into undeniable social power. Caroline Sherah Horn Aster, the legendary Mrs. Aster, became the absolute queen of New York society. Her annual ball and its exclusive 400 guest list wasn't just a party. It was a declaration of who mattered in America. If you weren't on that list, you were a nobody. You can still see the Aster name all over New York from Atoria Queens to the iconic Waldorf Atoria Hotel. Even their philanthropy was foundational. John Jacob Aster left money to create the Aster Library, a radical idea when public libraries weren't really a thing. That library eventually merged with others to become the New York Public Library, one of the city's most important cultural pillars. The family's influence faded a bit in the 20th century, but their name will forever be the bedrock of New York's aristocracy. If the Aers were the reserved old school elite, the Vanderbilts were the explosive force that blew the doors off New York society. Their story is all about raw, unapologetic capitalism and a lifestyle so over-the-top it came to define an entire era. The family patriarch Cornelius Vanderbilt or the Commodore was the total opposite of John Jacob Aster. He was brash, foul-mouthed, and an absolute genius. Born on Staten Island, the Commodore started his empire at 16 with a $100 loan from his mom to run a ferry service. That little boat was the start of a shipping and railroad empire that would dominate American transportation. He crushed his rivals without a second thought, building a network that connected the country. When he died, his fortune was worth more than the entire US treasury. While the Commodore made the money, his kids and grandkids spent it with a style the world had never seen. The Vanderbilts didn't just join New York society. They took it over. They lined Fifth Avenue with incredible mansions, each one bigger and grander than the last, earning it the nickname Vanderbilt Row. These weren't just homes. They were limestone and marble palaces designed to scream, "We've arrived." Outside the city, they built summer cottages in Newport, Rhode Island, like the Breakers and Marble House, which were anything but cottages. But this generation also started the family's tradition of giving back with Emily Thorne Vanderbilt Sloan White financing the creation of New York's Sloan Hospital for Women in 1888. Unlike the Aster fortune, which was mostly tied up in real estate, the Vanderbilt wealth was easier to divide and spend. Over the years, the massive fortune thinned out and many of those grand Fifth Avenue mansions were torn down. Still, the Vanderbilt legacy is everywhere. From Vanderbilt Avenue to Grand Central Terminal, a stunning monument to their railroad empire, they set a whole new standard for what it meant to be rich in America. So, the Aers owned the land and the Vanderbilts owned the rails. But one family came to own the flow of money itself, the Morgans. The House of Morgan wasn't just a bank. It was an institution so powerful it could steady or sink the entire American economy. And at the top was John Peront or JP Morgan. A man whose presence was as intimidating as his balance sheets. Born into a wealthy banking family, JP Morgan didn't start from scratch. He inherited a powerful platform and used his genius and sheer force of will to take it to another level. In an era of wild unregulated capitalism, Morgan basically became America's central banker before the Federal Reserve was even a thing. He took over failing companies, slashed costs, and merged them into massive efficient trusts. That's how we got giants like General Electric and US Steel, the nation's first billiondoll corporation. You could really see his power during national crisis. In the panic of 1907, when a market crash nearly took down Wall Street, the government didn't step in. JP Morgan did. He famously locked the country's top financiers in his library on Madison Avenue and refused to let them out until they pledged enough of their own money to save the economy. He was a one-man central bank. But Morgan's legacy goes way beyond finance. He was an obsessive art collector, buying up thousands of masterpieces and rare books. His personal collection was so important it became the foundation for the Morgan Library and Museum, one of New York's greatest cultural treasures. The museum stands as a monument to a man who believed great wealth came with a duty to preserve culture. The Morgan name is still synonymous with the peak of financial power, and he was the architect of modern American finance. The Whitneys represent a different kind of old money. They weren't defined by just one industry. They were masters of adaptation with a deep, deep love for the arts. Their fortune grew from a mix of investments in everything from banking and tobacco to New York City's street cars. William C. Whitney was a major player in the late 19th century and his family's venture capital firm JH Whitney and Company, founded in 1946, was one of the first of its kind. Smartly investing in new technologies that kept the family fortune growing. But the family's most lasting legacy. That's all thanks to one woman, Gertrude Vanderbilt Whitney. A sculptor herself, Gertrude became a champion for modern American artists. In the early 20th century, the art world, including big players like the Met, mostly ignored contemporary American painters. Gertrude thought that was ridiculous. She started showing artists from the Ashan School at her own studio in Greenwich Village. In 1929, she offered her collection of over 500 works to the Met. They turned her down, so she decided to start her own museum. The Whitney Museum of American Art opened in 1931 with a revolutionary mission to focus only on the art of the United States. That one move completely changed American culture, giving a platform to generations of artists from Edward Hopper to Jasper John's. The Whitney family shows how old money can stay relevant not just through wealth, but by shaping a nation's creative soul. Few names in American history carry the same weight as Roosevelt. While we know them for giving us two of the nation's most transformative presidents, their story started right here in New York, where they were an influential dynasty long before Teddy or Franklin ever saw the White House. The Roosevelts arrived from the Netherlands back when New York was still New Amsterdam. They slowly built their wealth and status over centuries as merchants, bankers, and real estate developers. The family eventually split into two famous branches. The Oyster Bay Roosevelts who gave us Republican President Theodore Roosevelt and the Hyde Park Roosevelts. The Democrats who produced President Franklin D. Roosevelt. They weren't just living in New York, they were building it. Theodore Roosevelt's father co-founded the American Museum of Natural History, a cornerstone of the city's culture. Teddy himself, born on East 20th Street, was a product of the city, and his progressive politics were a direct response to the gilded age excess he grew up around. He went after the very corporate trusts that families like the Morgans had built. His fifth cousin, Franklin, followed a similar path from New York's elite to the presidency, and his wife, Elellanena Roosevelt, Teddy's niece, completely redefined the role of the first lady and became a global champion for human rights. The Roosevelts are the perfect example of aristocratic duty. They use their inherited privilege for public service, proving that influence isn't always measured in dollars, but in the lasting impact you have on the world. In New York's elite circles, power takes many forms. For the Ssburgger family, that power has always come from the printed word. For more than a century, the Sulsberers have been the guardians of the New York Times, turning a struggling local paper into the most influential news organization on the planet. The family story with the Times began in 1896 when Adolf Ox bought the nearly bankrupt paper. He gave it its famous motto, all the news that's fit to print, and set the standard for its journalistic integrity. Control later passed to his son-in-law Arthur Hayes Sulsberger and it's been in the family ever since now in its fifth generation with publisher AG Sullsberger. So how have they kept it in the family? A special dualclass stock structure gives the Ox Sultzberger family trust voting control protecting the paper from outside influence and keeping it as the trust says unselfishly devoted to the public welfare. Their role as stewards of the nation's paper of record has given them immense almost quiet power. Their decisions about what to cover and how have shaped public debate, held leaders accountable, and written the first draft of modern history. They're a different kind of New York dynasty, one whose influence is built one headline at a time. While the Ssberggers command the world of news, another New York dynasty, the New House family, has quietly dominated the glossier and more glamorous side of media. Their private company, Advanced Publications, has shaped culture, fashion, and ideas through some of the most famous magazines ever published. The empire started with Samuel First New House Senior, who began by buying the Staten Island Advance in 1922. But the family's game-changing move came in 1959 when they bought Conde Nast. That single purchase brought Vogue, The New Yorker, and Vanity Fair into their portfolio, turning them into the arbiters of American culture. Samuel's sons C and Donald took the company to new heights. C in particular became a legend at Conde Nast. He was intensely private, but had an incredible gut feeling for what was cool. Under his leadership, Conde Nast's magazines didn't just follow trends, they created them. Vogue became the fashion bible and a revived vanity fair became the essential chronicler of power and celebrity. Because their company is private, the new house family's vast wealth is often out of the public eye. But it's not just from magazines. They have a diversified portfolio that includes major stakes in companies like Warner Brothers Discovery and the social media site Reddit. Unlike families who put their names on buildings, the new house legacy is on millions of coffee tables and in the phone screens of readers around the world. If you want to find the oldest of New York's old money, you have to go back way before the Aers or the Roosevelts. You have to go back to when New York was a Dutch colony called New Amsterdam. And there you'll find Peter Styverent. Stverent wasn't a businessman. He was the last Dutch director general of the colony, ruling with an iron fist and a famous wooden leg. He brought some muchneeded order to the rowdy settlement, building early infrastructure like the wall that gave Wall Street its name. When the English sailed in to take over in 1664, Stivverent wanted to fight. But the citizens, tired of his strict rule, basically said, "No thanks." After the surrender, Stverent eventually returned to New York to live on his buerai, the Dutch word for farm. And that farm is the key to his family's longlasting legacy. It was a huge piece of land that covered much of what we now call the East Village. As the city exploded around them, his descendants sold and leased off parcels of this ancestral land, turning their historic name into a real fortune. Today, the Stverent name is woven into the city's map like no other. There's Stverent Street, Stverent Square, and the massive Stverent Town residential complex. His legacy is also complicated. He was involved in the slave trade and was intolerant of religious diversity. Facts that have led to a modern re-examination of his place in history. The last direct descendant of Peter Stverent died in 1953. But the family's influence, all stemming from that one massive farm, has echoed through the city's entire history. While many old money families built their fortunes on Manhattan's Gilded Age opulence, the Lafra family made their name building for a totally different crowd, the city's middle class. Their story is a real estate saga rooted in the outer burers. The dynasty started with Harry Lfrack, an immigrant from France in the early 1900s, but it was his son, Samuel J. Lfra, who turned the family business into an empire. Sam was a classic New York character, bold, media savvy, and driven by a simple idea. We build for the masses, not the classes. His masterpiece is Lafra City in Queens. Announced in 1960, it was a massive project, a 40 acre development of 2018story apartment towers. It was designed to be a city within a city. And at the time, it was the largest privately financed apartment complex in the world. For decades, it provided affordable modern homes for thousands of families. Now in its fourth generation, the LERA organization has expanded way beyond that, developing luxury properties and the huge Newport waterfront community in Jersey City. They represent a more modern kind of real estate dynasty, one that got rich not by building Fifth Avenue mansions, but by betting on the millions of working people who are the real engine of New York. In the cutthroat world of New York real estate, the Ruden family stands out for being quiet, steady, and playing the long game. For a century, they've been building and managing premier properties with a philosophy that's the total opposite of a get-richquick developer. The family firm was founded in 1925 by Samuel Rudin and his siblings who established a core principle that guides them to this day. Build, own, and operate for the long haul. Unlike developers who build and flip, the Rudins have historically held onto their properties, managing them directly. This patient strategy has given them a portfolio that includes iconic office towers like 345 Park Avenue and three time square. Now led by the third and fourth generations, the family is also deeply involved in the city's civic life. During New York's fiscal crisis in the 1970s, Lewis Rudden co-founded the Association for a Better New York, rallying the private sector to help save the city from bankruptcy. They were also key in expanding the New York City marathon to all five burers. The Ruddens show a different kind of old money power. It's not flashy, but it's as solid as the foundations of the many skyscrapers they own across the Manhattan skyline. The Gilded Age left behind more than just powerful family names. It also left behind architectural ghosts. To continue this journey, make sure you watch our next video, The Lost Gilded Age Mansions of New York, where we uncover the spectacular palaces that once lined Fifth Avenue and the incredible stories behind why they're gone. So, what does it all mean? From the AA's real estate empire to Morgan's financial dominance, from the Vanderbilt's wild extravagance to the quiet cultural power of the Whitneys and Sullsburgers, the story of New York is tied forever to the stories of these families. They did more than just get rich. They built the city's skeleton and helped give it a soul. Their money built skyscrapers and their patronage created worldclass museums and libraries that still enrich millions of lives. Their rivalries and ambitions literally defined what an American aristocracy could be, setting the standard for wealth and power for everyone who followed. And that influence isn't just history. It lives on in the institutions they built, the companies they started, and the very street names we walk down every day. New billionaires may arrive in New York every year, but they're playing in a city that these old money dynasties first dreamed up and then built into a reality. They are the architects of the world's capital. A legacy of ambition and power etched forever into the bedrock of Manhattan.

- What Life Was Like for the FILTHY Rich in 1920s New York City: Kind: captions Language: en The champagne cork started popping at midnight December 31st, 1929 in the grand ballroom of the Plaza Hotel. Outside, the stock market had already collapsed. Bread lines were forming. But inside, silk gowns worth more than a factory worker's annual salary brushed against customtailored tuxedos. This wasn't just a New Year's Eve party. It was the final desperate performance of an era built on outrageous excess. A world about to vanish forever. The day began not with an alarm clock, but with the silent arrival of a valet drawing back velvet curtains in a Fifth Avenue mansion bedroom larger than most immigrant apartments. A fleet of butlers, footmen, chambermaids, and a personal chef all moved through the 40 room house with the precision of a military operation, all before the masters of the house even opened their eyes. The dressing ritual was a production in itself. A wealthy man didn't simply put on clothes. He was dressed. His valet would present him with a selection of customtailored suits, each pressed to a razor's edge. Shirts were stiff with starch. Shoes polished to a mirror finish. For the lady of the house, it was even more elaborate. She'd be laced into silk undergarments by her maid, then helped into a morning gown, a specific outfit just for breakfast and receiving morning visitors. This entire process could take over an hour. The goal was to achieve an appearance of effortless elegance, but the machinery behind it was anything but effortless. Breakfast was served not at a table, but in bed or a private sitting room on solid silver trays. The menu wasn't bacon and eggs. It was a curated selection of imported delicacies. Fresh peaches shipped overnight from Florida, kippers from Scotland, coffee beans roasted that morning. The newspaper arrived, ironed to remove any ink smudges that might soil a gentleman's hands. Alongside it came the social secretary's briefing, a typed memo detailing the day's critical engagements, which charity lunchon was mandatory, which debutant ball couldn't be missed, which powerful hostess would be offended by an absence. This wasn't a schedule. It was a battlefield map for the day's social warfare. And then the first cocktail was served, often before noon. A pickme up of gin or a fortified wine was considered essential medicine for the lingering effects of the previous night's party. The rich weren't just drinking for fun. They were self-medicating the exhaustion of maintaining their own lifestyle. Meanwhile, the true engine of this luxury remained invisible. Downstairs in labyrinthine service corridors, the staff worked in a rigid hierarchy beneath a stern butler and housekeeper. They weren't allowed to speak above a whisper. They entered and exited rooms through hidden passages so the family would never witness the labor that sustained their fantasy. The gilded cage was immaculate because an army of unseen hands polished the bars every single day. By late morning, the social machinery cranked into high gear. The epicenter was the Waldorf Historia Hotel's Peacock Alley. This wasn't a hallway. It was a runway. Everyday New York's elite would parade through in their latest fashions. Women in Parisian couture, men in London tailored suits. They weren't going anywhere in particular. The point was to be seen. To display wealth so blatantly that it silenced any questions about its source. You'd stroll slowly, acknowledging some, pointedly ignoring others. A single misstep, an outdated hat, a rumor of financial trouble could trigger social annihilation. Lunch was the next performance. At the Ritz Carlton or Del Monaco, a quick bite was a 3-hour 12c course tasting menu. These meals were strategic. Business deals were struck between courses. Marriages were negotiated over dessert. You didn't just eat, youworked with a fork and knife. The food was almost secondary to the conversation, which was a delicate dance of flattery, gossip, and subtle intimidation. The weight staff were trained to be ghosts, anticipating every need without ever being noticed. A raised eyebrow was all it took to summon another bottle of illegally imported French champagne. Afternoon meant shopping on Fifth Avenue, but not as you know it. The filthy rich didn't browse. They were expected by appointment. Personal shoppers at Burgdorf Goodman or Wakers would have entire collections laid out in private salons. Money was never discussed. Everything was charged to an account. The bill would be sent discreetly to the husband's office at the end of the month. A woman might spend the equivalent of a Model T on an afternoon shopping spree without ever touching a dollar bill. This was the ultimate flex. Consumption so routine it became abstract. Then came the mandatory charity events. This was where appearance became a brutal calculation. Philanthropy was a competitive sport. Donating to the right hospital or museum was a ticket into certain circles. But the donation itself was less important than being photographed making it. Society pages would report who gave what and more importantly who was seen there. It was a suffocating paradox. You performed generosity not out of compassion but to buy social insurance. It was a world where your net worth was constantly being assessed by everyone in the room and the price of admission kept rising. As the sun set, the respectable facade of the daytime elite completely vanished. The real business of being rich began after dark. This was when the private speak easys hidden behind unmarked doors in brownstones along the upper east side swung open. You didn't find these places. You were invited. A discrete card with an address and a time, no name. You'd arrive, give a password to a peepphole, and be ushered into a world that officially didn't exist. The bootleggers who supplied these places offered a premium service for this clientele. While the average person drank bathtub gin that could blind you, the rich had access to rare scotch smuggled from Scotland and real French champagne. The markup was astronomical. A bottle of champagne that cost $5 in France would sell for over $100 in a private club, over $1,500 today. But money was no object. The point was the exclusivity. Drinking illegal alcohol was a thrill. But drinking good illegal alcohol was a status symbol. It proved your connections reached into the criminal underworld itself. Then there were the underground jazz clubs in Harlem. This is where racial barriers for a few hours temporarily dissolved. Wealthy white socialites would flock to the cotton club or smaller, more illicit spots to hear music they couldn't find anywhere else. They called it slumbing. They were tourists in a culture they exploited for entertainment, paying to see black performers, while the clubs often barred black patrons. The irony was thick. They were celebrating the freedom and rebellion of jazz while living lives bound by the strictest social codes. It was a borrowed thrill, a dangerous fantasy that ended the moment their chauffeur-driven car pulled up outside their mansion. The parties themselves reached levels of extravagance that are hard to fathom. We're not talking about a few people standing around with drinks. We're talking about hostess like Margarite Missy Maloney throwing a circus themed party with live lions and acrobats performing in her ballroom or Hope Hampton commissioning a solid silver bathtub to be filled with champagne for her guests. These events could cost more than the average American home. They weren't parties. They were public declarations of financial invincibility. The themes became more outlandish as the decade wore on. Roman orgies, Egyptian tombs, nursery rhymes acted out by adults, a desperate search for a novel experience in a life that had run out of them. And for the truly decadent, there were the hidden cocaine and opium parlors. Cocaine was the fashionable drug for the fast set, seen as a modern, energizing vice. It was sold discreetly by physicians or at certainies. Opium dens, often run by organized crime syndicates, catered to a different crowd seeking escape. The morning after rituals for those who indulged too heavily, were a science in themselves. Doctors would make house calls to administer vitamin injections and rejuvenating tonics laced with stimulants. The hair of the dog cocktail, another drink to cure a hangover, was standard practice. Their entire existence became a cycle of unsustainable stimulation and painful recovery, all funded by seemingly endless wealth. But this glittering surface masked a profound and constant terror, the fear of exposure and financial ruin. The lifestyle was a financial trap. Mansions required armies of staff. Gowns were worn once. Parties had to outdo the last one. Many of these fortunes, especially the new money, weren't as solid as they appeared. They were built on stock market leverage and debt. To maintain the illusion, families would mortgage their estates to pay for their social seasons. The pressure was immense. One failed business deal, one social misstep, and the whole house of cards could collapse. This pressure led to ruthless social calculations, most visibly in arranged marriages. For the elite, marriage was a corporate merger, not a romantic union. Fortunes were merged to create dynasties. A daughter from a family with old money but dwindling resources would be paired with the son of a crude but cash-rich industrialist. Love was an accidental bonus. These unions were negotiated in drawing rooms by parents with lawyers present. The resulting marriages were often lonely leading spouses to seek companionship elsewhere which only increased the demand for discreet private investigators hired to track weward partners. The psychological toll was devastating. The term nervous exhaustion was a common diagnosis for wealthy women. The constant performance, the pressure to conform, the emptiness of a life dedicated solely to appearance. It broke people. They weren't working in factories. They were dying of boredom and anxiety. The solution wasn't rest. It was a discreet retreat to a private sanitarium in the countryside, a sort of spa for the mentally frayed, where they would be rested with sedatives until they were ready to reenter the social world. It was a system designed to patch people up just enough to keep spending. And hovering over everything was the threat of scandal. In a world where reputation was everything, blackmail was a constant danger. This is why the private investigators weren't just hired by jealous spouses. They were hired by the rich to investigate each other, to find leverage. The fear wasn't just of financial loss. It was of social death. [laughter] To be cut from invitation lists, to be snubbed on Peacock Alley was a fate worse than bankruptcy for some. It was a gilded prison. They had everything anyone could want. Yet they lived in fear of losing it all. The very excess that was meant to display their power had become their ultimate vulnerability, a chain of obligation and expectation that grew heavier with every passing year. The collapse didn't come from a stock market ticker. It came from a camera flash at a party that went too far. While the public thought the elite downfall was economic, the real undoing was moral. In their relentless pursuit of novelty, secret society parties began crossing every boundary. The one thing their money couldn't control was a single guest who brought a camera. The resulting photographs weren't just scandalous. They were evidence of a decadence so profound it couldn't be explained away. The very excess meant to display their invincibility became the proof of their corruption. The 1929 crash merely made official what was already rotting from within. Their world wasn't ended by economics. It was eroded by a profound emptiness they could never fill. Subscribe to Untold 20s.

  7. @cavasotti [2 likes]: Out of state works but the team you build is everything. I have 10 doors across 3 states (AL, FL, SC) and the properties that perform consistently are the ones where I vetted the PM carefully and set clear communication protocols upfront. Biggest mistake I see: people treat PM selection like an afterthought. It is the most important hire you make as an out-of-state investor. Things to verify before you commit: how they handle maintenance approval thresholds, how frequently they disburse, what their vacancy and turnover process looks like, and whether they will share detailed line-item statements rather than summary-only reports. A PM that sends vague monthly statements and slow disbursements will cost you far more than a slightly higher management fee from a transparent operator. Interview at least 3 PMs per market and ask them to show you a sample statement from a current client. You will learn everything you need to know from that one document.
  8. @TexasPropertyInsider [1 likes]: It’s the first guide I’ve seen that marries tech‑driven scouting with real‑world safeguards, giving investors like me the confidence to scale beyond our backyard.
  9. @thomasreedy4751 [1 likes]: IDK … I have been in enough smelly houses to know that I’d like to perform an actual sniff test.

  10. @josephcacciapaglia4753 [1 likes]: I've bought pieces of investment properties in 14 states in the last couple of months. Fractional real estate is by far the easiest way to invest out of state.
  11. @cavasotti: The PM vetting process is where most out-of-state investors skip steps and end up regretting it. I am invested across 3 states with PMs in each market and a few things I learned early: ask for a sample owner statement before you sign. How they present your financials tells you a lot about how organized they actually are. Ask for a reference from an investor who left them, not just the happy clients they hand-pick. And call their office as a prospective tenant first to see how fast they respond. Once you are in the relationship you are trusting them with your asset from a distance. The vetting work upfront is how you avoid spending the next year trying to exit a bad PM contract. Also read the full fee schedule closely. Management percentage is the headline number but placement fees, renewal fees, and repair authorization thresholds are where real cost differences show up. I have seen 8 percent managers cost more than 12 percent managers once you add everything up.

- How to Use Your Equity to Buy Another Home (Step-By-Step): Kind: captions Language: en in this video I'm going to show you exactly how to buy another house using your home's equity and it could be another primary residence it could be an investment town home it could be a multi-unit but I'm going to show you exactly how to tap in your home's equity and apply that towards purchasing another property my name is Justin lotten I've been a mortgage lender for 18 years and I've helped over 5,000 families buy invest and purchase properties around the United States let's get into it so what is home equity home equity is the value of your home which is determined by your loan balance subtracted from your home's value so let's say your home's value is about $400,000 and let's say you have a mortgage balance of $300,000 $400,000 minus $300,000 leaves you with $100,000 of home equity understanding your home equity is critical for a variety of reasons number one your home equity is established with your down payment so if you put down 20K or 20% that starts off your home's equity and the growth now your home's Equity is built in two different ways primarily the first way is home appreciation if your home values going up every year you're building home equity the second way you build home equity is by paying down your balance every single month we call that principal reduction so every month you make a mortgage payment a portion of that payment goes towards your home equity some of it goes towards interest and taxes but a portion of it goes towards your Equity so every month you're chipping away at your loan balance ideally if home value is appreciate every single year you build equity up and you build equity down and that Equity can be leveraged to help you make other Investments so I'm sure you've heard this before but the the number one way people build wealth and store wealth is in real estate and specifically that refers to the home equity in people's real estate holdings some people have one home some people have three homes some people have big apartment buildings or an entire portfolio so that being said your home's Equity changes every single month every month you're making a mortgage payment and every month home values can go up or down now your home's Equity can be determined in two ways it can be determined by an appraisal which is a snapshot on where the market is now or it can be determined if you actually go and list your home for sale and you negotiate a price that which someone is willing to pay it's not cash you can access in the bank tomorrow but there are ways to tap into it and to build your wealth so we've touched on what home equity is and how it's determined let's talk about the various ways you can access your home equity the first way is a home equity loan you're essentially getting a mortgage for a portion of the equity it's a second mortgage it's a loan and you receive a lump sum of cash from there you make monthly mortgage payments to pay down that balance every single month just like you do on your primary mortgage for example let's say you wanted to borrow $60,000 you would apply for a home equity loan you would get that $60,000 after the underwriting and mortgage process which takes a few weeks once you close you get that money it's yours to do whatever you want and you pay it down every single month just like you do on your first mortgage I have this magical thing called a home equity loan now the second way is a home equity line of credit which is also known as a HELOC this is a little bit different you're not getting a lump sum at closing you're getting an open line of credit so let's use the same $60,000 after you go through the underwriting process and get approved for your HELOC you have a $60,000 line that you can tap into whenever you want so initially you may not need to use the funds let's say you want to do some repairs you get a quote it takes 30 days to get a bid and now you know that you need to spend $110,000 on a new garage well you can borrow $10,000 of that $660,000 line and then every month you only pay on that $10,000 the idea here is to not get all the money up front to have access to it and you pay as you use it a really easy way to understand this is to think about a HELOC as a credit card you use what you need and you pay down your balance every month but you don't get it all in one lump sum now the third way to access your home equity is through a Cash out refinance this is a little bit different than the first two ways because now you have one new loan so here's how it works let's say your mortgage balance is $300,000 and you want to take out $60,000 for Home Improvements instead of getting a second mortgage for $60,000 and having two loans at two different rates you do one new loan for $360,000 000 and the difference between what you owe and what your new loan is is what you get to keep in one lump sum now you have one mortgage payment it's based off the $360,000 loan it keeps your finances simple and it houses all of the debt under one interest rate for Simplicity now here's the fourth and last way to access your home's equity and it's kind of straightforward it's basically selling your home now I'm not saying you need to go out and sell your home but at some point you might be ready to make a move ready to downsize or ready to relocate when you sell your home a realtor is going to help you determine your home's value you're going to look at your mortgage St to determine what you owe and the difference is your home equity so all in all what you'll walk away with after you sell your home is the sale price minus the real estate transaction fees and minus your actual mortgage balance and any taxes owed at closing so now we understand what home equity is and how you can access it let's talk about some of the common ways that you can use your homes Equity to your benefit so the first and most common way people use their homes Equity is to buy a new primary residence in other words you sell the home you're living in now you take the money you made which is your home equity and you apply that towards a down payment on your new home which you're going to reside in the most common example of this is when a young family outgrows the space they have and they need a bigger home with a yard and a basement now the second way to use your home's Equity is to buy an investment property and this is one of my favorite strategies to make more money what you do is you take a home equity line of credit or home equity loan and you use that money to purchase another property it might be a 2 unit it might be a 4 unit it might even be a single family home you get some renters in that property and now you start having some cash flow which you can use for other Investments you can use it to pay down your current mortgage the idea here is to build another stream of income and now to have a second property that's building more Equity that contributes to your total net worth now if you're paying attention here you'll start to notice some patterns as a mortgage lender this is the most common way we help people buy their first investment property by using the equity in their primary residence and over time what happens is you start to build more equity in every property that you own and you can tap into that Equity to purchase another investment property and to build your real estate portfolio over time the third way to use your home Equity is to pay off high interest rate debt like a credit card or an auto loan as I record this video credit card rates are at an all-time high between 25 to 30% mortgage rates are still around 7% by eliminating High interest rate credit card debt or even a high interest rate auto loan it could drastically lower your total monthly overhead for example let's say you took out $440,000 of home equity to pay off all your credit card debt and to pay
