for a net Savings of about $700 per month and the last way to leverage your home equity is to establish an emergency Reserve fund now a lot of people sleep on this and many firsttime and second time buyers don't even consider it but here's a deal there might come a time where you need a major repair where you need to do some maintenance where you come across a business opportunity or an funds when you need them in a pinch so look we've talked about all the exciting ways you can leverage your home's Equity how to access that cash to make more money but I'd be remissed if I didn't share some of the things to be mindful of and some of the downsides of borrowing too much money no we're not borrowing money so the first thing to consider obviously is as you borrow more money money against your home it reduces your home's Equity let's say in a couple years you want to go sell your home but real estate values have dropped if your loan balance is here and real estate values come down to here you have no equity to make when you sell your home and you might even need to bring some money to the closing table to get rid of that property the second thing to be mindful of is your debt to income ratio this is what lenders look at which is basically how much debt you have compared to how much income you bring in so when you get that money we're looking to make sure you can make those monthly payments but for example let's say a year or two passes and you lose your job or the financial circumstances in your household change you still got to make that payment every month and - Keeping up with the Joneses: Who Were the Joneses and What Happened to Their Mansion?: Kind: captions Language: en We’ve all heard the phrase “Keeping up with&nbsp; the Joneses” when comparing our homes to our&nbsp;&nbsp; neighbors’ homes. But who were the Joneses and why&nbsp; was their house so special? Hi everyone, ken here!&nbsp;&nbsp; today we are heading out to Rhinecliff,&nbsp; New York to explore the Joneses mansion!&nbsp;&nbsp; Make sure to hit that subscribe button so you&nbsp; never miss an exciting episode of "ThisHouse" In 1913, cartoonist Arthur Momand introduced&nbsp; a comic strip titled, “keeping up with the&nbsp;&nbsp; joneses” where the fictional McGinis family&nbsp; struggled to keep up with their neighbors,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Joneses, who had the nicest house in the&nbsp; neighborhood. The McGinises would copy what the&nbsp;&nbsp; Joneses did to not only keep up appearances,&nbsp; but also to show that they had good taste.&nbsp;&nbsp; While this is most likely where the saying comes&nbsp; from, the meaning of the phrase has deeper roots. In the mid-1800s, before the prominence&nbsp; of the Astors and the Vanderbilts,&nbsp;&nbsp; New York’s high society revolved around 4 primary&nbsp; families, the Livingstons, the Schermerhorns,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Masons, and the Joneses. All three of&nbsp; these families were considered old money,&nbsp;&nbsp; bringing vast wealth to the new world from Europe.&nbsp;&nbsp; The Masons and the Joneses jointly owned the&nbsp; third largest bank known as Chemical Bank.&nbsp;&nbsp; Being at the top of society, with more money than&nbsp; the other 99 percent of New Yorkers combined,&nbsp;&nbsp; these families defined social norms and traditions&nbsp; in the New World. When one of these families would&nbsp;&nbsp; sport a new style, members of New York’s lower&nbsp; upper class would immediately rush to their&nbsp;&nbsp; tailors to have new outfits made, showing that&nbsp; they too could be fashionable and trend setting. Perhaps no single person had a tighter&nbsp; grip on what was considered fashionable&nbsp;&nbsp; than Elizabeth Schermerhorn Jones. When&nbsp; she would wear an outfit out in public,&nbsp;&nbsp; not a week would go by before every lady of&nbsp; high society was wearing something similar.&nbsp;&nbsp; When she decorated her home and hosted a&nbsp; dinner, you could bet that within a month&nbsp;&nbsp; all of her friends’ homes were redecorated&nbsp; to imitate hers. Much in the same way that&nbsp;&nbsp; we see people imitating celebrities today,&nbsp; society in the mid-1800s emulated Mrs. Jones. Before Newport, Rhode Island became the choice&nbsp; playground of the Gilded Age’s rich and famous,&nbsp;&nbsp; the choice area for summer homes was still up in&nbsp; the air. Elizabeth headed up north to Dutchess&nbsp;&nbsp; County New York and purchased a scenic&nbsp; 80 acre lot overlooking the Hudson River.&nbsp;&nbsp; In 1853, construction began on her summer house.&nbsp; The all brick mansion quickly became an icon of&nbsp;&nbsp; high society. She had hired master mason, John&nbsp; Byrd to decorate the façade with rectangular&nbsp;&nbsp; and custom molded bricks. The resulting structure&nbsp; boasted elaborately detailed terracotta cornices&nbsp;&nbsp; composed of repeating arches. Elsewhere, the&nbsp; house boasted terracotta Solomonic columns&nbsp;&nbsp; supporting intricate layers of arching brick&nbsp; work. All around the façade, you could find&nbsp;&nbsp; unique brick patterns creating rhythms around&nbsp; the house’s many towers. Each and every window&nbsp;&nbsp; surround was thoroughly planned to offer a&nbsp; diverse, but cohesive interest to the façade. The floor plan, designed by architect&nbsp; George Veitch, was planned to accommodate&nbsp;&nbsp; large social events with each room flowing&nbsp; into the next and anchored on a grand hall. Entering the home through the vestibule, you would&nbsp; see the grand hall, clad in wood paneling allowing&nbsp;&nbsp; natural light to illuminate the interior&nbsp; space through a third floor attic skylight. We can pass through the old&nbsp; growth wood arcade to find the&nbsp;&nbsp; grand staircase gently rising towards the landing. Up here we will find an ornate wooden&nbsp; balustrade skirted by wainscotting&nbsp;&nbsp; below the steeply pitched skylight&nbsp; following the rise of the roof above. Every room was finished out so perfectly that&nbsp; Elizabeth hosted her first event in the house,&nbsp;&nbsp; her friends rushed to buy up the land&nbsp; around it and develop their own homes,&nbsp;&nbsp; each making the effort to outdo the last. When Elizabeth passed away in 1886, the&nbsp; mansion was sold into the Finck family.&nbsp;&nbsp; The Fincks were prominent brewers, but were&nbsp; known to be as tasteless as they were ruthless.&nbsp;&nbsp; The family constantly fought each other for&nbsp; control of their own brewing empire which lead&nbsp;&nbsp; to their ultimate demise. From suing each other&nbsp; to sabotaging each other’s departments in the&nbsp;&nbsp; company, the house passed through the family&nbsp; rapidly while in a constant state of neglect.&nbsp;&nbsp; This continued until 1934 when Rhinecliffe was&nbsp; purchased at a foreclosure auction. But by this&nbsp;&nbsp; time, the mansion was too far gone. The land&nbsp; was split up and sold off until just two and&nbsp;&nbsp; a half acres remained of the original&nbsp; 80 acre estate. It then sat abandoned,&nbsp;&nbsp; slowly deteriorating as vines crept in through&nbsp; broken windows. Soon enough, moisture built up in&nbsp;&nbsp; the walls and plaster began falling on the termite&nbsp; infested floors. Eventually, the roof collapsed,&nbsp;&nbsp; leaving a hollow shell the only testament of&nbsp; America’s roots in conspicuous consumption. While the once grand mansion was slowly&nbsp; reclaimed by nature it continued to change hands.&nbsp;&nbsp; As recently as 2017, the house was purchased by&nbsp; a developer. And at the tail end of last year,&nbsp;&nbsp; in December of 2022, emergency stabilization&nbsp; plans were filed by the developer in a&nbsp;&nbsp; last-ditch effort to save the mansion. Only&nbsp; time will tell whether or not these efforts&nbsp;&nbsp; will be successful as the developer&nbsp; attempts to Keep up with the Joneses. What are your thoughts on this&nbsp; video? Let me know down below&nbsp;&nbsp; in the comments section. And while you’re there,&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure to hit that subscribe button so you&nbsp;
